Course Cover Letter Writing: The Heading ======================================= A well-crafted cover letter is an essential component of your application package when you are applying for courses at universities or colleges. A strong heading sets the tone for the rest of the letter by providing important information about you and the course you're interested in. This article will guide you through writing a clear and impactful heading for your course cover letter, with in-depth explanations, key tips, and guidelines. Step 1: Your Contact Information ------------------------------- At the top of the page, include your full name, mailing address, phone number, and email address. Make sure this information is up-to-date and professional looking. Using a consistent format throughout all your correspondence can help create a polished image. *Key tip:* Use a legible font such as Times New Roman, Calibri, or Arial in size 12 points. Keep formatting simple, avoiding unnecessary bold or italic text that might distract from the content. Example: John Smith 123 Main Street Anytown, USA 12345 Phone: (123) 456-7890 Email: johnsmith@email.com Step 2: Date ----------- Include the date below your contact information, aligned to the right side of the paper. This adds a formal touch and shows attention to detail. Example: September 1, 2023 Step 3: Recipient's Details -------------------------- Address the recipient appropriately using their title (Mr./Ms./Dr.) followed by their last name. If you do not have specific names, use generic salutations like "Dear Admissions Committee" or "To Whom It May Concern". Place the recipient's details two lines beneath the date, aligning them to the left side of the paper. Examples: * Mr. John Doe Director of Admissions University of XYZ 123 University Ave XYZ City, State ZIP OR * Dear Admissions Committee, Step 4: Course Name and Program ------------------------------ In the next line, specify the exact name and program of the course you are applying for. Including these details demonstrates that you have done thorough research about the course and conveys genuine interest. Examples: * Bachelor of Science in Computer Engineering, School of Engineering * Master of Arts in Creative Writing, Department of English Step 5: Application ID Number (if applicable) --------------------------------------------- If provided with an application ID number during the initial stages of your application process, mention it here, following the course name and program. This ensures easy tracking and organization of your application materials by admissions committees. Example: Bachelor of Science in Computer Engineering, School of Engineering (Application ID: ABCDE1234) Following these steps will enable you to write a precise and effective heading for your course cover letter. By including relevant information clearly and concisely, you demonstrate respect for the reader while also highlighting your own organizational skills. As always, remember to proofread meticulously before submitting your final draft! David Eric "Dave" Grohl (born January 14, 1969) is an American rock musician and singer. How to Make Envelope Books Take 5 sheets of construction paper. Fold each sheet in half. Glue the sheets together back to back. Open the page. Glue an envelope to the bottom portion. The question is glued to the outside of the envelope. The answer is placed on a sheet of paper and placed inside of the envelope. Illustrations can be drawn or glued on the top portion of the folded paper above the envelope. *Make sure not to use too much glue. Decorate the cover with designs using markers, glitter glue, or let the students use their imagination to think of unique decorations. Types of Envelope Books Joke or Riddle Books You put the riddle on the outside of the envelope and the answer on a slip of paper which goes inside the envelope. You can make a book based on letters to characters in a book your students have read. On the outside of the envelope you write who am I. On the inside the student writes out a description of the character and his role in the book. Science Question and Answer Book Students write questions on the outside of the envelope. They write up the answer and place it on the inside. You can find detailed instructions with illustrations under PDF Files. You don't height tell me exactly Chi. Maybe it was just by the closet. A community is all populations of different species that live in the same place. An ecosystem is a community and its environment. Ecosystem ecology studies how energy and nutrients move through an ecosystem. All living things need energy to survive, move, grow, and reproduce. A trophic level is the number of times energy moves from one living thing to another, before reaching a particular living thing. The first trophic level, called producers or autotrophs, gets energy from the environment. They use the energy to make organic compounds. Most producers, such as plants, take in energy from sunlight, but some take it from inorganic compounds. Other trophic levels, called consumers or heterotrophs, get their energy by eating other living things. All animals are consumers, and there are three kinds: herbivores, carnivores, and omnivores. Herbivores eat only plants, carnivores eat only other animals, and omnivores eat both. Decomposers are living things which break down dead things. A food web shows the movement of energy in an ecosystem. [Justin is the son of the babysitter.] yeah, pretty much what she has in her school. Maharashtra is in the west part of India. It is on the coast of the Arabian Sea. Six other states of India have borders with Maharashtra. It covers an area of . It is bigger than Italy, but smaller than Oman. In traditional Indian geography it falls under the West Indian zone. Well she was really interested in the different clothes, huh? What is blood? Blood is made up of three basic types of cells: - Red blood cells . The hemoglobin in the red blood cell carries oxygen to all parts of your body to give you energy. If your red blood cell count is low (anemia), you may look pale and feel tired. If your hemoglobin is very low, you may need a blood transfusion. - White blood cells. White blood cells fight infections. The white blood cells that fight the best are called neutrophils (segs). Your neutrophils are called "bands." - Platelets. Platelets help prevent bleeding by clotting the blood. These cells travel through your body in a white/yellow liquid called plasma. When your platelet count is low you may bleed when you get a cut, bruise or nose bleed. You may, if your count is very low, need a platelet transfusion to prevent bleeding. Where do blood cells come from? Your body is constantly producing blood cells. Blood cells are made in the bone marrow, the spongy tissue inside your bones. They come from a type of cell called a stem cell. The stem cell divides in response to signals made by your body. It will either become a red blood cell, a white blood cell, or a platelet. These signals are called growth factors. Blood cells live for a specified time in your body. White blood cells live for a few hours to a few months. Red blood cells live for 120 days. Platelets live for five to nine days. Why does chemotherapy affect my blood cells? Chemotherapy affects rapidly growing cells more than slower growing cells. Some normal cells have a fast rate of growth. These include your blood cells. A side effect of chemotherapy may destroy young blood cells as they are growing. It can take anywhere from seven to 21 days for your blood cells to reach their lowest numbers. Your nurse or doctor will tell you when your blood cells counts are expected to be low. This is called your nadir. You will have your blood tested periodically to see how many of each type of blood cell you have. This is called having your counts checked. What happens when my counts are low? Rec blood cells are measured by values called hemoglobin (Hgb) and hematocrit (Hct). The normal value for hemoglobin is 14.0 to 18.0; hematocrit is 37 to 52. If your red blood cell count is low, you may feel more tired or short of breath than usual. This is called anemia. Your doctor may order a red blood cell transfusion for you. Sometimes your red blood cells may be low because of the disease itself. Your doctor may prescribe a growth factor called erythropoietin (EPO) to help increase your red blood cell counts. One type of white blood cells are neutrophils. When neutrophils are low, this is called neutropenia. You may not see the usual signs of infection so review the signs of infection at the bottom of this page. A normal white blood cell count is 4,000 to 10,000. Sometimes your doctor may order a growth factor called GM-CSF or G-CSF to help your white blood cells recover more rapidly. The neutrophils fight most common infections. For that reason, this portion of the white cell count is also monitored. This is called the Absolute Neutrophil Count (ANC). A normal platelet count is 130,000 to 400,000. A platelet count below 50,000 may make you more at risk for bleeding. This is called thrombocytopenia. If your platelet count drops, your doctor may order a platelet transfusion for you. What should I do if my counts are low? Talk with your nurse or physician about avoiding crowds and public places such as shopping malls, airplanes, buses, trains, etc. You may need to wear a mask if your white blood count is very low. Good hygiene is important. Shower or bathe daily and pat yourself dry. Wash hands often and well. Always wash them before eating and after using the bathroom. - Try to avoid nicks, cuts, and tears in your skin. - If you nick, cut or burn yourself, wash skin immediately with soap and water Bandage the cut if necessary. - Avoid going barefoot. - Wear gloves when doing any physical activity that might dry or damage your skin. - Be careful cutting or trimming your nails to avoid small nicks. - Do not have a manicure. - Use cuticle cream remover instead of picking, tearing or cutting cuticles. - If you shave, use an electric razor. - Do not take rectal temperature. - Do not clean out litter boxes or bird cages. Avoid all contact with animal urine or stool. - Avoid long exposure to the sun. Wear sunscreen of at least SPF 30. - Avoid getting a sunburn. - Brush your teeth two to four times per day with a soft toothbrush and rinse with a mixture of salt and water or baking soda and water. Good mouth care will help decrease the number of germs in your mouth. - Prevent constipation. Straining to have a stool could cause small tears in the rectal area. - Do not use suppositories or enemas unless told otherwise by your nurse or doctor. - If needed your physician can recommend a stool softener. - Wipe very gently but thoroughly after a bowel movement. - Tears in the lining of the intestine or rectal area can be dangerous when the white blood cell count is low. - Increase your dietary fiber with fruits and vegetables. - Drink eight 10-ounce glasses of liquid a day. - Perform gentle exercise such as walking daily. - Take short rest periods between activities. - If you receive blood or platelets at a hospital other than Dana-Farber, make sure they are irradiated or leukoreduced. Obtain a medical alert bracelet. - Do not take medications containing aspirin or ibuprofen unless your doctor tells you to. These drugs can make the platelets less effective. - If your platelet count is low: - you should not have sexual intercourse. - women should not use tampons or douche. - You should avoid trauma during sexual relations, maintain cleanliness and lubrication. Signs of infection Be sure to notify your nurse or doctor immediately if you have any abnormal bleeding. This includes nose bleeds, blood in urine or stool, dark tarry stools, bleeding gums or easy bruising. Be sure to let your nurse or doctor know if you develop any of the following signs of an infection: - a fever of 100.5 degrees or more - loose stool for more than two days - severe night sweats, shaking chills - shortness of breath, cough or sore throat - headaches or neck stiffness - frequent urination, burning with urination - redness, swelling, pain or discharge from anywhere on your body - extreme fatigue [mother is looking at the book that Leila gave Nina for Christmas. Title: How to Become a Fan of the Wild and Crazy Kids TV Game Show The Wild and Crazy Kids TV game show was an iconic children's program that aired from 1990 to 1992. The series featured kids competing in wacky and zany challenges, often involving water balloons, mud, or other messy elements. If you want to become a fan of this classic television show, here are some steps to help guide you through the process. Step 1: Learn About the History of the Show To truly appreciate any form of media, it is important to understand its history and context. Research when and where the show first aired, who the hosts were, what kind of games they played, and why the show became popular. This will give you a solid foundation of knowledge about the show and allow you to better enjoy and understand it. Key Tip: Look up interviews with the cast and crew members to get insight into their experiences working on the show. You can find these online by searching for "Wild and Crazy Kids" on video-sharing platforms like YouTube. Step 2: Watch Episodes Online Thanks to the internet, many episodes of Wild and Crazy Kids are now available to watch online. Websites such as YouTube have entire playlists dedicated to the show, making it easy for new fans to access and enjoy the content. Key Guideline: Be prepared for outdated fashion choices, cheesy special effects, and over-the-top acting - all hallmarks of '90s TV shows. Embrace these quirks as part of the charm and fun of watching Wild and Crazy Kids. Step 3: Engage With Other Fans Joining a community of fellow enthusiasts can enhance your viewing experience. Participate in online discussions about the show, share your favorite moments, and connect with others who love Wild and Crazy Kids just as much as you do. Key Tip: When engaging with the fandom, be respectful and open-minded. Everyone has different opinions and perspectives; listening to others will only enrich your understanding and appreciation of the show. Step 4: Host Viewing Parties Invite friends over for themed viewings of select episodes. Prepare snacks reminiscent of the early '90s (think neon-colored fruit roll-ups and Dunkaroos) and encourage guests to dress up in clothing styles from the era. By immersing yourself fully in the world of Wild and Crazy Kids, you can deepen your connection to the show. Key Guideline: Make sure everyone feels included and enjoys themselves during the viewing party. Encourage discussion about the episodes but also remember that sometimes people simply want to sit back and take in the nostalgia without feeling pressured to contribute. Step 5: Share Your Passion Spread the word about Wild and Crazy Kids among family, friends, and coworkers. Introduce younger generations to the show so they too can experience the joy and excitement it brings. Sharing your passion for Wild and Crazy Kids may even inspire others to join the fandom! Key Tip: When sharing your enthusiasm for the show, avoid coming across as pushy or dismissive of others' preferences. Remember, being a true fan means celebrating the things you love while still recognizing and appreciating the interests of those around you. Yes that jug o' tea is cold. [MOT and EXP briefly discuss the camera and why it isn't working] Title: Automatically Include Tolls on iPhone Maps App Directions Introduction: This tutorial will guide you through enabling the option to automatically include tolls when using Apple's built-in Maps application on your iPhone. This feature is particularly useful if you want to get an accurate estimation of costs associated with your journey or simply prefer routes that avoid toll roads whenever possible. By following these steps, you can ensure that the directions provided by the Maps app take into account any potential toll fees during your trip. Step 1: Open Settings App First, locate the grey gear icon labeled "Settings" on your home screen or within the app library and tap to open it. The settings app allows users to customize various aspects of their iPhones functionalities, including those related to navigation apps like Apple Maps. Step 2: Scroll Down and Tap 'Maps' Once inside the Settings app, scroll down until you find the entry named 'Maps'. Tapping this option will reveal several subcategories regarding different map features and options. Step 3: Select Driving & Navigation Under the main heading of 'MAPS', you should see a subsection called 'Driving & Navigation.' This category contains settings specifically tailored towards optimizing the driving experience while using Apple Maps. Go ahead and tap on it. Step 4: Enable 'Avoid Tolls' Option Within the 'Driving & Navigation' menu, look for the toggle switch titled 'Avoid Tolls.' Slide this switch to the right so that it turns green. When enabled, this setting instructs the Maps app to calculate routes that exclude toll roads from the suggested itinerary. Keep in mind that depending on your location and destination, avoiding tolls might result in longer travel times. Key Tips and Guidelines: - If you ever need to revert back to default behavior (i.e., including tolls), just return to this same menu and slide the 'Avoid Tolls' switch back to the left, making it white again. - Be aware that some rural areas may lack non-toll alternatives, thus disabling the ability to completely avoid tolls. However, enabling this feature generally encourages the Maps app to prioritize routes without toll booths where feasible. - Note that there are other valuable options within the 'Driving & Navigation' menu, such as 'Preferred Transportation Type,' which lets you choose between driving, walking, cycling, or transit modes; and 'Navigation Voice Volume,' which adjusts the volume level of turn-by-turn voice guidance during navigation. Conclusion: By following these straightforward steps, you have successfully configured your iPhone's Maps app to automatically consider tolls when providing directions. With this knowledge at hand, you can now make more informed decisions about your travels, potentially saving both time and money along the way. Safe journeys! Cleaning Stains from Stainless Steel Sinks: A Comprehensive Guide Stainless steel sinks are popular due to their durability, elegance, and resistance to corrosion. However, they can still accumulate stains over time if not properly maintained. This guide will provide you with an in-depth explanation of how to clean stains off stainless steel sinks effectively. By following these steps meticulously, your sink will regain its original luster and shine. **Things You'll Need:** * Dish soap or mild liquid detergent * Non-abrasive sponge or cloth * White vinegar * Baking soda * Olive oil (optional) * Soft microfiber towel * Distilled water **Step 1: Empty the Sink** Remove any dishes, food particles, or debris from the sink. Rinse it thoroughly under warm running water to eliminate loose dirt. Make sure all visible residue is gone before proceeding to the next step. **Why this is important:** Removing physical objects and initial grime ensures that no abrasive elements scratch the surface during cleaning, which could potentially damage the protective layer of stainless steel. **Step 2: Create a Cleaning Solution** In a bowl, mix equal parts white vinegar and warm water. Alternatively, you can create a paste using baking soda and warm water. Both solutions serve as effective non-toxic alternatives to commercial cleaning products. **Key Tips:** * Avoid using harsh chemicals like bleach or ammonia because they may discolor or pit the stainless steel finish. * If you choose to use baking soda, add just enough water to form a thick paste; too much water will make it less effective at lifting stains. **Step 3: Apply the Cleaning Solution** Dip your non-abrasive sponge or soft cloth into the cleaning solution and wring it out so it's damp but not dripping wet. Gently rub the stain in circular motions until it starts to fade away. For tougher stains, leave the cleaning solution on for several minutes before scrubbing. **Guidelines:** * Always move in the direction of the grain to prevent cross-scratching. * Be patient - persistent yet gentle effort usually pays off when dealing with stubborn stains. **Step 4: Rinse Thoroughly** After successfully removing the stain, rinse the area well under warm running water to get rid of any remaining cleaning solution. It's crucial to remove all traces of the cleaning agent to avoid buildup or further staining. **Step 5: Dry Properly** Using a soft microfiber towel, dry the sink completely. Leaving moisture behind can lead to mineral deposits or water spots, especially if you have hard water. If necessary, use distilled water to ensure there are no impurities left behind. **Optional Step 6: Polish Your Sink (For Extra Shine)** If you want to give your sink an extra gleam, put a few drops of olive oil onto another soft cloth and buff the sink in the direction of the grain. The oil fills in tiny scratches and enhances the shine, giving your sink a polished look. By consistently maintaining your stainless steel sink using these methods, you'll preserve its longevity and aesthetic appeal while ensuring a hygienic environment for food preparation. Remember, regular upkeep is vital in preventing future stains and maintaining the pristine condition of your sink. [the volume on the recorder went down so it's difficult to hear what CHI and MOT just said] Title: How to Enable the Steam In-Game FPS Counter The Steam client offers an integrated feature that allows gamers to display their Frames Per Second (FPS) while playing games. This can be helpful for monitoring performance and ensuring smooth gameplay. Here's our comprehensive guide on enabling the Steam in-game FPS counter: **Step 1: Launch the Steam Client** Firstly, open your Steam application on your computer. The desktop icon or Start menu shortcut should do the trick. Log into your account if you aren't already logged in. **Step 2: Access Settings Menu** Click on "Steam" located at the top left corner of the window, then select "Settings" from the dropdown list. This will open the Settings window where all preferences and configurations are managed. **Step 3: Navigate to the In-Game Tab** Once the Settings window is up, look for the tabs along the left side of the screen. Click on the one labeled 'In-Game'. It contains settings related to behavior during gaming sessions within the Steam ecosystem. **Step 4: Locate the "In-Game FPS Counter" Option** Scroll down until you find the section named "In-Game FPS Counter". Three options are available here - "Off", "Top Left", and "Top Right". Choose either Top Left or Top Right depending on where you prefer the FPS counter to appear on your screen. Selecting 'On' without specifying position defaults to Top Right. **Key Tips:** * Choosing Top Left places the FPS counter at the upper left corner of the screen. * Picking Top Right situates the FPS counter at the upper right corner of the screen. * If unsure, try both locations and see which works best based on your personal preference or monitor setup. **Step 5: Confirm Changes by Closing the Window** After deciding on an appropriate location for the FPS counter, simply close the Settings window by clicking 'OK'. Any changes made will automatically save upon closing the window. **Step 6: Test Your New Setting** To verify whether the FPS counter has been successfully enabled, launch any game through Steam. After loading into the game, check if the FPS count is visible according to your chosen placement. By following these steps, you have now activated the Steam in-game FPS counter! Monitoring frames per second can provide valuable insight into your system's gaming performance, helping optimize visual settings for optimal playability. Enjoy seamless gaming with real-time feedback on your PC's capabilities. Do I think it was a basically proper assessment of what he actually does? What fortunes have been his! [as Bro picks up car that fell to the floor] Does anyone know where these rockets go? [MOT takes the crayon and starts to color] Purpose. To make a model of an elbow. Materials. Small box with lid, masking tape. - Place the box and lid together on a table with their open sides down. - Connect the box and lid with two strips of tape as shown. - Hold the box with one hand and raise the lid with your other hand. - Hold the box with one hand and lid with the other hand. Try to bend the lid down. So Now We Know The combined box and lid represent an elbow. The box represents the bone in your upper arm and the lid is the bone in your lower arm. The tape pieces are ligaments that hold bones together. Where the two boxes meet represents a joint. Since the box can only bend in one direction, the joint is a hinge joint. This experiment was taken from Janice VanCleave's Play and Find Out About the Human Body and used by permission of the publisher, John Wiley & Sons, Inc. For more information about joints, check out Janice VanCleave's Human Body for Every Kid (ages 8-12) and Janice VanCleave's Super Science Models (ages 8-12). Why does my elbow only move in one direction and my head moves all around? A: There are more than 200 bones in your body. The place where bones meet is called a joint. Some joints, such as your elbows, can only move in one direction. Your head can move in many different directions because it is attached to your neck which has different types of joints. Before birth, a human baby's skeleton is made of cartilage (a tough, rubbery flexible tissue that is sometimes called gristle). But by birth, most of the cartilage has changed into about 350 bones. As the baby grows, many of these bones grow together. An adult skeleton has about 206 bones. Cartilage still makes up some parts of the adult skeleton and keeps some bones, such as those in your knees, from rubbing together. Where bones come together is called a joint. Some are fixed joints, which allow no movement, such as the bones of the skull. Other joints are slightly moveable joints, as are the joints in your spine. But most joints are moveable joints, meaning they are able to move freely, such as your elbow or knee. A tough band of slightly elastic, fibrous material called a ligament connects bones at joints. Your elbow, knee, and the knuckles of your hand are examples of a moveable joint called a hinge joint. This joint allows only back and forth movement, like a door swinging on its hinges. The elbow allows the bones of the lower arm to be raised and lowered. In the elbow, the rounded end of the humerus bone of the upper arm fits into a cradle formed by the ulna and radius bones of the forearm. "I hit my funny bone!" is often said when the elbow is hit. But it is not the humerus bone that causes the not-so- funny tingling sensation. Instead, it is a nerve that is close to the surface that causes the pain when knocked against the humerus bone. Your head is connected to your neck, which is the top seven of the 26 bones of your spine called vertebrae. In between the vertebrae are disks of cartilage. It is the cartilage between the vertebrae that allows the spine to bend and twist somewhat. Thus the joints between the vertebrae are considered slightly moveable joints. The first vertebra of your neck is different in that it can move up and down. The flexibility of this vertebra, called the atlas, allows you to move your head upward or downward, such as when you look up at the sky or down at the ground. A moveable joint, called a pivot joint, is between the first and second vertebrae of your neck. Pivot joints allows rotation but not up and down movement, so the pivot joint in your neck allow you to turn your head left and right. Place your hand on your neck as you turn your head and you'll discover that only the upper part of your neck is turning. The flexibility of the cartilage disks between the remaining five neck vertebrae allows you to tilt your head to the side and crane your neck backwards and forward. Was this article helpful to you? Title: How to Edit a Clan Battlefield on RuneScape Introduction: RuneScape is a popular MMORPG that allows players to create their own clans and engage in clan wars. One feature of clan wars is the ability to edit your clan's battlefield, which can give you an advantage in battles. This tutorial will guide you through the process of editing a clan battlefield on RuneScape. Step 1: Access the Clan Camp To begin editing your clan battlefield, first make sure you are a ranked member of a clan with the appropriate permissions to edit the battlefield. Then, head to the Clan Camp located west of Falador. Speak to the Clan Settings NPC named iYesha to access the Clan Management interface. Key tip: If you don't see iYesha upon entering the Clan Camp, double-check your membership status or ask a higher-ranked clan member if they have granted you the necessary permissions. Step 2: Open the Clan Citadel Interface Once you've accessed the Clan Management interface via iYesha, click on the "Clan Citadels" tab at the top of the screen. Here, you can manage various aspects of your clan citadel, including the battlefield. Guideline: Make sure your clan has built a clan portal within its citadel before attempting to edit the battlefield; otherwise, you won't be able to proceed further. Step 3: Enter the Battlefield Editor In the Clan Citadels interface, locate the section titled "Battlefield Setup." Clicking this option will open the Battlefield Editor, where you can customize the layout of your clan's battlefield. Key tip: Familiarize yourself with different terrain types available (e.g., flat land, forest, swamp) as well as structures like walls, gates, towers, etc., since these elements can significantly impact combat strategies during a war. Step 4: Design Your Battlefield Layout Using the tools provided in the editor, design the layout of your battlefield by placing terrain tiles and structures according to your preferences. You may add up to two keep doors, one gatehouse door, four wall sections, and several other objects such as resource gatherings points and decorative items. Be strategic when designing your battlefield, keeping potential tactical advantages in mind. Important note: While designing your battlefield, ensure that there is enough space between structures for units to move around freely. A cramped battlefield might hinder movement and result in less effective strategies. Step 5: Save Your Changes After finalizing your battlefield design, save your changes by clicking the green checkmark icon at the bottom right corner of the editor window. Upon saving, a confirmation message will appear stating that your edits were successful. Guideline: Regularly test out new designs and iterate on them based on feedback from fellow clan members. Remember that practice makes perfect! Conclusion: Editing a clan battlefield on RuneScape requires patience, creativity, and strategy. By following this tutorial step-by-step, you should now be able to craft unique and functional battlefields tailored specifically to your clan's needs. Good luck, and happy battling! The Kuiper BeltThe outer edge of our Solar System is not empty. There are many, many huge spheres of ice and rock out near Pluto's orbit. Astronomers call this huge group of planetoids "Kuiper Belt Objects", or "KBOs" for short. The Kuiper Belt is a bit like the asteroid belt, but much farther from the Sun. Scientists think there are many thousands of KBOs. Astronomers have discovered several hundred so far. KBOs are gigantic balls of ice and rock. Some are small, some are tens of km across, and some are as big as the planet Pluto, and maybe larger! They orbit the Sun on the edge of the Solar System, near Pluto. They orbit between 30 to 50 AU (1 AU = Earth to Sun distance) from the Sun. Some astronomers think the Kuiper Belt goes out to 100 AU. KBOs take 200 years or longer to orbit the Sun! Some astronomers had a theory about the Kuiper Belt in the early 1900s. One of them was Gerard Kuiper. In 1951, Kuiper said that some kinds of comets might come from the Kuiper Belt. The Kuiper Belt was named after Gerard Kuiper. The first KBO was discovered in 1992. It was given the odd name "1992 QB1". The planet Pluto is also a Kuiper Belt Object. There are probably a bunch of other KBOs as big as Pluto or bigger that we haven't found yet. This is why astronomers are having a hard time deciding what a planet is. Is Pluto a planet? Are any of the other KBOs? Astronomers have found one object, called 2003 UB313 for now, that looks like it is bigger than Pluto. Some people are calling it the "tenth planet". There are a couple of different kinds of KBOs. The different kinds have different orbits. Some have orbits like Pluto's. They are called "plutinos" (mini-Plutos). Some have orbits that are more like circles. They are called "cubewanos". There are other objects besides KBOs out on the edge of the Solar System. The Oort Cloud is much, much further out than the Kuiper Belt. All of the objects on the frozen edge of the Solar System can be put in one big group. Astronomers call that group "Trans-Neptunian Objects" (TNOs) because they orbit further from the Sun than Neptune. KBOs and objects in the Oort Cloud are all Trans-Neptunian Objects. So are some other odd misfits that are in-between the Kuiper Belt and the Oort Cloud, including Sedna and 2003 UB313. Some of the best known KBOs are Pluto, 1992 QB1, Orcus, Quaoar, Ixion, and Varuna. Strange names for strange objects! Is that what I heard before that loud noise? He lay down on the ground and he blew and blew really hard. Suddenly, the mineral popped out of the ground! The bear was so excited. He looked around and saw a big bird in the sky. He waved up at the bird and yelled "Come down and help me!" The big bird landed next to the bear and, together, they managed to pick up the big mineral and carry it away. Title: How to Deal with a Clingy Child Clinginess in children is a common behavior where they feel an intense need to stay close to their caregivers, often exhibiting anxiety or distress when separated. While clinginess can be challenging, there are strategies to help your child become more independent and confident. This tutorial will provide you with a comprehensive guide on dealing with a clingy child. ### Step 1: Understand the Causes Before addressing clinginess, understand that various factors might contribute to this behavior. These include: * Fear of separation * Anxiety or stress * Changes in routine or environment * Attachment issues * Temperament (some children are naturally more cautious or sensitive) Recognizing these causes will enable you to address the root issue effectively. ### Step 2: Provide Reassurance Children who exhibit clingy behaviors usually lack confidence. Offer reassuring words, physical affection, and emotional support to boost their self-esteem. Let them know that you believe in their abilities and encourage them to try new things independently. However, avoid overdoing it as excessive reassurance may reinforce clingy behavior. ### Step 3: Establish Predictable Routines A consistent daily schedule provides structure and familiarity, reducing anxiety associated with transitions. Gradually introduce changes to routines to prepare your child for different situations. For instance, if starting school soon, visit the institution together beforehand, meet teachers, and discuss what to expect during the day. ### Step 4: Encourage Independence Teach your child basic skills like dressing themselves, brushing teeth, or making simple decisions. Give them opportunities to practice these tasks independently. Initially, they might make mistakes or take longer than usual; patience and positive encouragement are crucial during this learning process. ### Step 5: Foster Healthy Attachments Secure attachments between parents and children promote healthy development and independence. Spend quality time bonding with your child through activities such as reading books, playing games, or having conversations. Remember that secure attachment does not mean constant physical proximity but rather knowing that someone reliable is available whenever needed. ### Step 6: Gradual Separation When separating from your child, do so gradually. Start by leaving them alone for short periods while still within sight. Gradually increase the duration and distance until they're comfortable being apart from you. Always say goodbye calmly and clearly explain when you'll return. Avoid sneaking away as this could heighten their anxiety about future separations. ### Step 7: Manage Your Own Anxiety Your reactions can significantly impact your child's feelings. If you appear anxious or upset when separating, it may worsen their clinginess. Instead, remain calm, positive, and confident, conveying the message that everything is under control. ### Key Tips and Guidelines: * Be patient - changing clingy behavior takes time. * Use distractions - redirect their attention towards enjoyable activities when feeling anxious about separation. * Praise effort and success - recognizing small achievements strengthens their self-confidence. * Model confident behavior - demonstrate courage and resilience in your actions. * Seek professional help if necessary - persistent clinginess despite efforts to improve could indicate underlying issues requiring expert intervention. By following these steps and guidelines, you can successfully deal with a clingy child, fostering their growth into confident, independent individuals. Explore and apply the concept of reality vs. imagination. Children will: Be able to explain how to make arts and crafts. Orally use words about types of arts and crafts and the things that are used to make them. Extend oral vocabulary by speaking about the concept of imagination. Use key concept words [art, drawing, painting, project, creative, imagination]. Explain Use the slideshow to review the key concept words. Explain that children are going to learn about making things: How to make things. The difference between imagination and reality. How team spirit helps a team perform better. Model After the host introduces the slideshow, point to the photo on screen. Ask children: Why do we make things? (for fun, for a school project, etc.) Ask children: What are some things we can make? (drawings, paintings, sculptures, etc.) Say: Art is a fun and creative way of expressing your ideas, thoughts, and opinions. What are some things we use to make arts and crafts? (crayons, scissors, glue, etc.) Guided Practice Guide children through various arts and crafts they could make, asking them what materials they would need to make the item. Always have the children identify the name of the arts and crafts item. Apply Play the games that follow. Have them discuss with their partner the different topics that appear during the Talk About It feature. Two Weights?Math brain teasers require computations to solve. Martin rushed into the room bearing good news. "Joseph, your idea worked! The company liked the idea of using only two types of weights to measure heavy objects!" announced Martin, giving the letter to Joseph. "I told you so. Given any two types of weights, you can measure objects that are above a certain weight," explained Joseph, reading the letter, "Well, as long as the two weights are not both even." Martin thought for a moment and then realized that he had no clue what Joseph meant by that, so he asked, "Huh? What? Isn't the new weight system designed to measure all types of objects?" Joseph smiled and replied, "Technically, yes. However, this system can't measure objects that weigh 1 pound, 2 pounds and other lighter objects. Besides, both weights are heavier than 10 pounds." "Really? But then why did the company like it?" wondered Martin, "What use does it have then? Can it measure 300 pounds? 90 pounds? 69 pounds?!" "Yes, yes, and no." Joseph laughed, "You're not getting the point. The company only weighs things 120 pounds or heavier. This weighing system can't measure 119 pounds but any object above 119 pounds can be expressed as a sum of combinations of these two weights." After hearing that, Martin was even more confused. Finally, Joseph said, "Look, 17 (5+5+7) can be expressed as a sum of only 5s and 7s. 18, on the other hand, can't. It works on the same principles. Think about it. You'll get it eventually." Assuming everything has integer weights, what were the two types of weights that Joseph suggested? HintTry it out with small numbers. What is the largest number that can not be expressed as a sum of 3s and 4s? The answer is 5. Try it out with other numbers. You'll find a pattern eventually. AnswerThe two weights are 11 and 13. If you played around with the hint, you'll realize that the largest number that cannot be expressed as a sum of Xs and Ys is equal to (X-1)(Y-1) - 1. We are told that the largest number that can not be expressed as a sum of a combination of the two weights is 119. 119 = (x-1)(y-1) - 1 120 = (x-1)(y-1) From here, we only have to list 120's factors: (1,120), (2,60), (3,40), (4,30), (5,24), (6,20), (8,15), (10,12). There are 8 possible combinations. However, we were told that both weights were above 10 pounds. Thus, only one pair remains (10,12). So, x=11 and y=13. Try it out. You can't make 119 using only 11s and 13s but you can make 120 and above using only 11s and 13s. See another brain teaser just like this one... Or, just get a random brain teaser If you become a registered user you can vote on this brain teaser, keep track of which ones you have seen, and even make your own. Title: How to Fill Out an Envelope to Mail a Letter Introduction: Mailing a letter is a simple yet crucial skill that everyone should know. An essential part of this process is filling out the envelope correctly to ensure your letter reaches its intended recipient without any issues. This article will provide you with a comprehensive guide on properly addressing and mailing a letter using a standard envelope. Key Tips and Guidelines: * Always write clearly and legibly, preferably in black or blue ink. * Use block letters (all capital letters) when writing addresses. * Avoid abbreviating street names unless necessary (e.g., "St." for Street). * Double-check all information before sealing the envelope to prevent errors. Step 1: Choose the Right Envelope Size Select an appropriate envelope size based on the contents of your letter. Common sizes include #10 (business), A2 (monarch), and A6 (commonly used for greeting cards). Make sure there's enough space inside the envelope for your letter and any additional enclosures. Step 2: Address the Recipient Place the recipient's address on the front side of the envelope, typically centered slightly below the middle point. The format includes: Recipient's Full Name Their Street Address (House Number & Name/Building Name) Apt./Suite/Unit Number (if applicable) City, State ZIP Code Example: JANE DOE 123 MAIN STREET APT 4B SPRINGFIELD, IL 62701 Step 3: Add Return Address Write your return address in the upper left corner of the envelope. It serves as both your contact information and indicates where the post office should send the letter if undeliverable. Format it similarly to Step 2 but add your name last: Your First and Last Name Your Street Address City, State ZIP Code Example: JOHN SMITH 456 ELM AVENUE CHICAGO, IL 60601 Step 4: Place Stamp(s) Correctly Affix the stamp(s) on the top right corner of the envelope. If you need more than one stamp due to weight or distance, place them side by side vertically along the edge. You can also purchase special stamps like forever stamps which adjust their value according to inflation. Step 5: Seal the Envelope Lick or moisten the adhesive strip at the flap of the envelope, then press down firmly until sealed completely. Be careful not to tear or wrinkle the paper during this process. Step 6: Send Your Letter You have two options here: drop off the letter at a USPS mailbox or take it directly to the Post Office counter. For security reasons, avoid leaving mail in unsecured boxes overnight. Instead, plan ahead and find a nearby collection box during business hours. Conclusion: Filling out an envelope might seem straightforward, but taking care to write clearly and following these steps ensures your correspondence arrives promptly and safely. Remember to double-check every detail before sending your letter! Once upon a time there was a brave man who wanted to save the world. He looked high and low until finally he found the perfect place. He collected the foolish things and threw them away. Finally the place was saved. Everyone in the world was so happy and thankful. Lesson 20 Section 3 than the whole, which is represented by 1. Note that the number that follows "of" must be the denominator. For that number signifies the whole; the numerator is the part of the whole. 3 out of 5 signifies the ratio of 3 to 5: three fifths. That is the ratio of those who responded yes to the whole. (Lesson 18.) Moreover, the word fraction in everyday usage means a ratio. When 3 out of 5 responded yes, we ask, "What fraction responded yes?" It would be very wordy to ask, "What is the ratio of those who responded yes to the total number surveyed?" Yet that is what the former question means. To write a fraction -- "3/5 responded yes" -- is a vulgarism. You will never see it in any newspaper or journal. When we say it, of course, there is no problem. In calculation, we often use a fractional symbol to represent a ratio rather than a measurement. Lesson 27, Question 1. Example 1. In a class of 20 students, 3 were absent. What fraction were absent? What fraction were present? What percent were absent? What percent were present? The number that follows "of" -- 20 -- is the denominator. "3 out of 20 is how many out of 100?" Since 100 is 5 20, then the missing term is 5 3: 15% were absent. The rest, 100% - 15% = 85%, were present. Example 2. The whole is the sum of the parts. In a class, there are 17 girls and 12 boys. What fraction of the class are girls, and what fraction are boys? Answer. In this problem, we are not given the whole number of students. But the whole is the sum of the two parts: Girls + Boys = 17 + 12 = 29. Therefore, 17 out of 29 are girls: And 12 out of 29 are boys: Compare Lesson 18, Example 12. Example 3. Calculator problem. In a class election, 135 students voted for candidate A, and 212 voted for candidate B. What percent voted for A, and what percent voted for B? Solution. Again, the whole is the sum of the two parts: 135 + 212 = 347 Therefore, 135 out of 347 voted for A, while 212 out of 347 voted for B. To find the percent that voted for A, press (Lesson 10.) See This is approximately (We could anticipate that this would be less than 50%, because 135 is less than half of 347.) For the percent that voted for B, press This is approximately (We could anticipate that this would be more than 50%, because 212 is more than half of 347.) Or, since 38.9% voted for A, then the number that voted for B is 100% - 38.9% The student should easily find this to be 61.1%. (Lesson 6, Question 6.) Please "turn" the page and do some Problems. Is there an Equation for a Spring? Photo of Lab As we have seen in class a spring is an elastic object in that if you apply a force to the spring it will stretch and if this force is removed the spring will return to its orignal shape. This lab is concerned with answering the question "Is there an equation for a spring?", or in other words can we describe how a spring will stretch mathematically? Part 1: When you pull on a spring is it easier at first and then harder, or aways the same? Part 2: There is a setup on your table which will allow you to measure how far your spring will stretch as you add masses. These masses supply a force to the spring. What force is this? How do you obtain that force from the mass? Part 3: Line up the weight hanger so that its bottom lines up with the zero on the scale. This is the mark that all measurements will be made relative to. The scale numbers mark off centimeters (cm) so this will be your unit for distance. Gradually add mass (use the units of grams) and measure the distance stretched as you add mass. Be careful not to add too much mass since as you know if you stretch a spring too far it will not go back to its original shape and the spring is ruined. Remember as you add mass you use the total mass on the hanger for each distance measurement. So, as you add mass the mass will get larger and the distance stretched will get larger. Continue to add mass until you have ten measurements at least. Part 4: Now from your answers in part 2 for each mass determine the amount of force this represents. In our case since we are using grams and centimeters, g = 980 cm/s^2. The resulting unit for force in this system of measurement is called a dyne. Next to each calculated force put the distance the spring stretched. Part 5: Now let's make a graph of our data to help us see the relationship between the applied force and the distance stretched. The amount of force applied is on the y-axis and the distance stretched is on the x-axis. Make a plot of your data remembering to use as much of the graph paper as possible. Draw a best line through your data points. Describe the shape of this line. From looking at your data points and your line describe a general relationship that you see as far as force versus distance. Do you know of an equation that may describe this? If so, what is it? Did your spring act as you thought it might from your answers in part 1? [holds the coin up for GAR to see] [mother fitting the Eskimo's hat into the puzzle] You used a few of them here with him. Composing Your Own Music: A Step-by-Step Guide ============================================= Composing music can be an incredibly rewarding experience, allowing you to express yourself creatively and emotionally through sound. Whether you're a seasoned musician or just starting out, anyone can learn to compose their own music with patience, practice, and the right guidance. This comprehensive guide will take you through the process of composing your own music, from start to finish. Defecating in a cup may seem like an unusual or even unpleasant task, but there are certain situations where this might be necessary, such as medical testing or emergency survival scenarios. This guide will provide clear, detailed instructions on how to defecate in a cup safely and hygienically. **Things You'll Need:** * A clean, dry cup or container (preferably with a lid) * Toilet paper or wipes * Water (for rinsing) * Soap and warm water (for cleaning hands) * Disposal bag or container (optional) ### Step 1: Choose the Right Container Select a suitable cup or container that is large enough to accommodate your needs while also being easy to handle. It should be made of a material that can be thoroughly cleaned and disinfected after use, such as glass, stainless steel, or hard plastic. Using a container with a lid will help contain any odors and make transportation easier. ### Step 2: Find a Private Location Find a private location where you can comfortably perform this task without interruptions or distractions. This could be a bathroom, toilet, or another secluded area. Make sure the space is well-ventilated to minimize any potential odor buildup. ### Step 3: Prepare Your Equipment Gather all necessary materials before beginning the process. Having everything within reach will ensure a smooth and efficient experience. Place the cup or container close by along with toilet paper or wipes for cleanup. If possible, have access to running water, soap, and a disposal method for used materials. ### Step 4: Assume a Comfortable Position Assume a comfortable position that allows you to control your bowel movements effectively. Sitting on a toilet seat is usually the most convenient option, but other positions such as squatting over the container may also work depending on personal preference and available facilities. ### Step 5: Begin Defecation Process Relax and take deep breaths to help initiate the defecation process. Allow the feces to fall directly into the cup or container, taking care not to touch the inside walls with anything other than the fecal matter itself. Avoid causing splashes or spills by controlling the speed at which the feces enter the container. ### Step 6: Clean Up Immediately Once the defecation process is complete, immediately clean yourself using toilet paper or wet wipes. Be thorough to avoid any residual mess or discomfort. Dispose of used materials properly in a waste bin or sealed disposal bag. ### Step 7: Rinse and Clean the Container If possible, rinse out the container with water to remove any remaining residue. Use soap and warm water to clean the interior and exterior surfaces of the container thoroughly. Dry the container completely before storing or reusing it. ### Step 8: Wash Hands Wash your hands with soap and warm water for at least 20 seconds to eliminate any bacteria or germs transferred during the process. Dry your hands completely using a clean towel or air dryer. **Key Tips and Guidelines:** * Always practice good hygiene when performing this task to minimize health risks. * Use gloves if handling someone else's fecal matter to protect against infection. * Dispose of all used materials properly according to local regulations. * Consider labeling the container as "biohazard" or "medical waste" if required by law or for safety reasons. * Never attempt to defecate in a cup in public places or areas where privacy cannot be guaranteed. They can either form on land or in the ocean. The Cascade Range in Washington, Oregon and northern California is made of volcanoes. Some of the largest volcanoes are on divergent boundaries, which form the mid-ocean ridges. The mid-ocean ridges have big volcanic mountain chains that run through the Atlantic, Pacific and Indian Oceans. The mountains in the mid-ocean ridges can grow tall enough to create islands such as Iceland or the Azores. Rings on her fingers an' bells on her toes. [sounds as if she comes over and gives MOT a kiss] Title: How to Construct a Simple Rain Gauge Building your own rain gauge is an enjoyable and educational activity that allows you to measure precipitation in your backyard. This tutorial will guide you through creating a simple yet accurate rain gauge using basic materials. **Total Time:** Approximately 1 hour (excluding drying time) **Materials Needed:** 1. A clean plastic bottle (2-liter soda bottles work best due to their size and sturdiness) 2. Ruler or measuring tape 3. Scissors or box cutter 4. Permanent marker 5. Waterproof sealant (optional) 6. String or twine (if using hanging method) 7. Large nail or screw (if using hanging method) **Instructions:** Step 1: **Prepare Your Bottle** Thoroughly rinse out the bottle with water to remove any residue from its previous contents. Dry it completely before proceeding to the next steps. Ensuring no moisture remains prevents bacterial growth and makes the adhesive more effective when sealing later on. Step 2: **Mark Measurements** Using your permanent marker, make marks along the side of the bottle at regular intervals - we recommend starting at every inch mark up to 10 inches. These lines serve as visual indicators for collected rainfall amounts. Key Tip: Make sure these lines are clear and easy to read so you can accurately measure rainfall levels. Step 3: **Cutting the Bottle** Carefully cut the top off the bottle about four inches down from the neck. The exact height isn't crucial; just ensure there's enough room left standing to comfortably hold liquid without spilling over. Discard the top part of the bottle. Guideline: Cut slowly and steadily to avoid jagged edges which might provide areas for leakage. Step 4: **Invert Top Portion** Turn the upper portion of the bottle upside-down and insert it into the bottom half such that the mouth rests inside the main body. It should look like a funnel directing water into the marked section. Key Point: Align the opening of the inverted top piece with the bottom edge of the main body to maximize collection area while minimizing evaporation. Step 5: **Seal if Necessary** If necessary, apply your chosen waterproof sealant around the point where the two parts meet. Allow sufficient drying time according to manufacturer instructions. Sealing ensures no leaks occur between the joined sections, maintaining accuracy in measurements. Step 6: **Secure Position** Decide whether you want to place your rain gauge on a level surface or hang it from a post or fence. For ground placement, simply position it in an open space away from obstacles. If hanging, tie some string or twine securely around the neck of the bottle and attach it to a large nail or screw hammered into a wooden structure. Remember: Choose a location free from overhead coverings or wind disturbances to get accurate results. And voila! You have constructed your own rain gauge. Regularly check your device after rainfalls and record the amount of rain accumulated against the corresponding markers. Over time, this data could help inform gardening decisions, weather predictions, or scientific studies. Happy observing! Title: Defend Against a Choke from Behind Using Martial Arts Introduction: Being choked from behind is a common attack scenario that can lead to unconsciousness if not defended properly. This tutorial will teach you effective martial arts techniques to defend against a chokehold from behind. The steps provided are applicable for various martial arts styles such as Brazilian Jiu-Jitsu (BJJ), Krav Maga, Judo, and others. Remember, practice makes perfect - always train with a qualified instructor and a trusted training partner. Step 1: Recognize the Attack First, understand the signs of a potential choke. An aggressor may approach from behind, grab your neck with both hands or one hand, and apply pressure. Be aware of this threat and prepare yourself mentally and physically to respond accordingly. Early recognition allows you to react quickly and decisively. Step 2: Break the Grip To break the grip, bring your elbows together and down towards your waist while pushing your chin down into your chest. This movement constricts the space between your shoulders, making it harder for the assailant to maintain their grip. At the same time, tucking your chin protects your airway and prevents the completion of the choke. Key Tips: - Keep your body relaxed; tension reduces mobility and reaction speed. - Maintain awareness of your surroundings during execution. Step 3: Create Space Once you have broken the initial grip, create distance by stepping forward and backward simultaneously using both legs. This action generates momentum, enabling you to escape the choke more efficiently. It also disrupts the assailant's balance, creating an opportunity to counterattack or disengage. Guidelines: - Focus on generating power through your hips rather than just your leg muscles. - Move explosively but keep control over your movements to avoid losing balance. Step 4: Apply a Shoulder Lock (If Escaping Isn't Possible) If escaping isn't feasible due to multiple attackers or other factors, consider applying a shoulder lock to neutralize the immediate threat. To do so, reach up and grasp the thumb of the attacking arm around your neck. Bend your elbow, pulling the attacker's arm across your chest diagonally while turning your body away from them. Important Notes: - Only attempt this technique under proper supervision and after mastering fundamentals. - Applying excessive force can cause injury - ensure proportional response based on the level of danger posed. Step 5: Disengage Safely After successfully defending against the choke, immediately move out of harm's way. If there are additional threats nearby, be prepared to defend yourself further. Always prioritize safety and seek professional help when necessary. Recommendations: - Regularly drill these techniques to build muscle memory and confidence. - Consult credible sources like books, videos, or instructors to refine your skills. Conclusion: Learning how to defend against a choke from behind using martial arts requires consistent practice and patience. By following the steps outlined above, you increase your chances of effectively responding to such attacks. However, remember that real-life situations might differ, so stay adaptable and continue improving your self-defense capabilities. " Here what you got etcetera. " This was Prussia's high moment. The idea to improve the human species by selective breeding is called eugenics. The name was proposed by Francis Galton, a bright scientist who meant to do good. He said that the human stock (gene pool) should be improved by selective breeding policies. This would mean that those who were considered "good stock" would receive a reward if they reproduced. However, other people suggested that those considered "bad stock" would need to undergo compulsory sterilization, prenatal testing and birth control. The German Nazi government (1933-1945) used eugenics as a cover for their extreme racial policies, with dreadful results. aw, no okay give it to her. A Change Book Age: Preschool through elementary Time: 30 minutes or more Type of activity: Arts and crafts Markers or crayons What to Do: Step One: Discuss with your child how Lazlo's situation changed throughout The Brand New Kid. Then, talk about how your child's own situation has changed since the beginning of her school year. Some discussion starters: feeling more confident being on her own, making new friends; enjoying more activities, etc. Step Two: Ask her to draw a picture of what she looked like on the first day of school. Then, ask her how she felt (sad, scared, excited, etc.). Write these words on the bottom of the picture. Step Three: Next, ask her to draw a picture of how she looks now. Be sure to include any special activities she enjoys doing, her friends, her favorite teacher, etc. Then, ask how she feels about school now. Write these words on the bottom of the picture. Step Four: Staple the pages together. Explain that you're going to put the "Change Book" away for a while. Step Five: In a few months, or at the end of the school year, take the book out again. Now your child can draw another picture and label how she feels. Step Five: Read the entire book together and notice how your child has changed throughout the year. Discuss the things that happened to make her feel different than she did in September. More on: Crafts for Kids You don't want to give it to the baby? Bro clean up your plate and cup. Title: How to Frighten Anyone at All on Halloween Halloween is a time for thrills and chills, and what better way to celebrate than by scaring someone out of their wits? This guide will provide you with detailed steps and helpful tips to frighten anyone this Halloween. Remember, the goal is to create a fun and scary experience while respecting others' boundaries and ensuring safety. ### Step 1: Plan Ahead To successfully frighten someone, careful planning is essential. Consider the following factors when creating your plan: * The age and personality of the person(s) you intend to scare. Younger children may be more sensitive to fear-inducing situations, so adjust accordingly. * Your location - indoors or outdoors - as well as any existing decorations that can enhance the atmosphere. * Time constraints; ensure there's enough time to set up and execute your plan without rushing. Key Tip: Make sure your plan aligns with local laws and community standards regarding safe celebrations. ### Step 2: Choose a Scary Theme Selecting a theme will help focus your efforts and make your haunted scene more cohesive. Popular themes include classic horror tropes like zombies, vampires, ghosts, or serial killers, but feel free to get creative! Once you have chosen a theme, gather appropriate props, costumes, and music to bring it to life. Key Guideline: Opt for high-quality props over cheap alternatives to increase realism and effectiveness. ### Step 3: Create an Atmosphere An immersive environment plays a significant role in delivering a good scare. Here are some ways to build tension and suspense: * Use dim lighting or flickering candles to cast eerie shadows. * Play unsettling background noise or sound effects related to your chosen theme (e.g., creaky doors, distant screams). * Set up jump scares using unexpected objects or animatronics placed strategically around the area. Key Tip: Test the lighting and sounds beforehand to guarantee optimal results during execution. ### Step 4: Master Timing and Delivery Timing is crucial when attempting to frighten someone. Build anticipation slowly, then strike suddenly when they least expect it. Be mindful of body language and facial expressions; maintaining a serious demeanor adds authenticity to your performance. Key Guideline: Practice makes perfect - rehearse your timing and delivery until you feel confident executing them naturally. ### Step 5: Engage Your Victims Interactive experiences often yield greater reactions than static displays. Try incorporating these elements into your setup: * Hide behind walls or corners and deliver lines tailored specifically to the person being scared. * Create challenges or puzzles for victims to solve, only revealing the scare after completion. * Design escape rooms where participants must navigate through various frights to reach safety. Key Tip: Personalize interactions based on knowledge about the victim to maximize impact. ### Step 6: Monitor Reactions and Adjust Accordingly Observe your audience's responses carefully throughout the encounter. If someone appears genuinely frightened or uncomfortable, give them space and allow time to recover. You can always dial back the intensity if necessary. Key Guideline: Always prioritize safety and wellbeing above trying to scare someone. By following these steps and keeping our key tips and guidelines in mind, you'll be able to create a truly terrifying experience for anyone this Halloween while maintaining respect and consideration for all involved. Have book ready. 1.Opening: Introduce the lesson. "Today, we are going to read the book "Alphabet Mystery" by Audrey and Bruce Wood. As I read this story I want you to think about a few things we have talked about this week. I want you to search for clues to find out if this story is fiction or non-fiction. What does it mean if a story is non-fiction? (A story that is real, true, or fact) Tell me what fiction stories are. (A book that is fake, not true, made up.) By the end of this book, we need to decide whether this book is fiction or non-fiction." "What else have we been talking about this week? (Student says main idea.) Define the word main idea. (It means what the story is MOSTLY about.) By the end of this story, I want your help to find the main idea of this story. What are the two things we are looking for in this story, detectives? (Main idea and non-fiction/fiction)" 2. Read Story Suggested Higher Order Thnking (HOT) questions/comments to ask during the story: Knowledge- Can you identify which letter is missing? (x) Evaluation- Can you decide from the first couple of pages, whether this book is fiction or non-fiction? (Fiction) Keep it in your brain until the end of the story! Connection- (Make connection sign) I can make a connection because when I was younger, I would make words in my alphabet soup. Thumbs up if you have ever done that. Analysis- Can you relate the picture of little 'x' and the xylophone? (The xylophone begins with the letter 'x'.) Application- Can you show me what it means to tremble? (Shake in fear.) Analysis- Can you deduce what those drops of water are in the picture? (They are 'M's' tears.) Knowledge- Identify what present the 'K' took. Point to K. (Key) The 'b' took Point to B. (banana) The 'd' took a Point to D. (drum). Analysis- What can you deduce from each present? (Every letter got a present that begins with that letter.) 3. Closure: Sum up what was in the story. "Thumbs up if you enjoyed this book. All right detectives, let's look at our main idea chart and fill out the information. First we are going to talk about the main idea of the story. Turn and talk with your shoulder partner about what you think the main idea of the story is. Can you identify the main idea of this story? (The main idea was that 'x' was missing and the alphabet was trying to find him.) Draw picture of main idea. First, can anyone decide whether this story was fiction or non-fiction? (Fiction) What clues in the story proved that it was fiction? (Flying pencils, talking letters,) I am going to write that 'x' is missing because that is the main idea of the story. What does that mean again? (It means what the story is mostly about.)" 4. Smartboad extension: "In the story, 'x' was missing from the alphabet. Now we have a snake that is missing a couple letters." Transition to smartboard. (See attachment) "I want you to put the missing letters in order on the snake." "Everyone point to the A on the snake. What letter do you think should be next? (B) There are a few resources you can use to help you around the classroom. Point to the alphabet train. Point to the alphabet word wall. Both of these can help you if you get stuck." "Now point to the B. What letters go between the B and the E? Turn and talk to your shoulder partner about what letters go between the B and the E. Can someone tell me what we are doing on this assignment?" (We are sequencing the alphabet.) Use name sticks to call students to the board to sequence alphabet. Closing thoughts: Sum up what we talked today. "Today we talked about the story Alphabet Mystery. We talked about the book being fiction. We also talked about the main idea. Finally, we worked on ordering the alphabet." Higher Order Thinking (HOT): Can you think of a time in our day when we use ABC order? Catching a bug may seem like a simple task, but there are many factors to consider to ensure that you catch the bug safely and humanely. This tutorial will guide you through the process of catching a bug using a container and a piece of paper. **Step 1: Choose the Right Container** The first step is to choose an appropriate container to catch the bug. The container should be large enough to accommodate the bug without crushing it, but small enough to make it easy to transport. A clear plastic or glass container with a lid is ideal as it allows you to observe the bug and prevent it from escaping. Key tip: Avoid containers made of materials that can harm the bug, such as metal or sharp plastics. **Step 2: Find the Bug** Look for the bug in its natural habitat. Observe carefully and avoid disturbing its environment too much. Once you have located the bug, approach slowly and quietly so as not to scare it away. Key guideline: Be patient; bugs often move slowly and can be challenging to spot at first glance. **Step 3: Prepare the Paper** Take a sheet of paper, preferably white or light-colored, and fold it in half lengthwise. This creates a crease down the middle of the paper, which acts as a guide when attempting to contain the bug. Key tip: Use stiff paper, such as cardstock, as it provides more stability than flimsy paper. **Step 4: Position the Paper** Place the folded paper behind the bug, gently nudging it towards the center of the paper. Move slowly and deliberately to avoid startling the bug. Keep your hands steady and maintain a safe distance from the bug. Key guideline: Allow the bug plenty of space to move around. **Step 5: Contain the Bug** Once the bug is near the center of the paper, quickly bring both sides of the paper together, trapping the bug inside. Hold the edges firmly against the tabletop or other flat surface to prevent the bug from escaping. Key tip: Make sure the bug is completely contained before lifting the paper off the surface. **Step 6: Transfer the Bug to the Container** Gently slide the paper into the container while keeping one side pressed against the opening. Slowly lift the paper, guiding the bug into the container. Place the lid securely on top once the bug is fully inside. Key guideline: Take care not to crush the bug during this process. **Step 7: Release the Bug (Optional)** If you caught the bug for observation purposes only, release it back into its natural habitat after studying it. To do this, hold the container upside down over a suitable location and remove the lid. Gently shake the container until the bug falls out. Key tip: Wait a few moments to ensure the bug has left the container before walking away. By following these steps, you'll be able to catch a bug safely and efficiently. Remember always to treat bugs with respect and handle them gently to minimize stress and potential injury. Happy bug hunting! That you enjoyed how he ate all the food on Saturday because it was all stuff that you like to eat. Now I'm Diandra's father and Diandra's my Mother. Verbs end with "-as" when they are in present tense. English uses "I am", "you are", "he is". But in Esperanto, there is just one word for "am", "are", "is" - "estas". Similarly, "kuras" can mean "run" or "runs". Infinitives end with "-i". For example, "esti" means "to be", "povi" means "to can". It is easy to make past tense - always add "-is" ending. To make future tense, add "-os". For example: Look at the fish in the picture. Leave! For where? What for? To get what?" An interjection is a word that expresses some kind of emotion. It can be used as filler. Interjections do not have a grammatical function in the sentence and are not related to the other parts of the sentence. If an interjection is omitted, the sentence still makes sense. It can stand alone. When you are expressing a strong emotion, use an exclamation mark (!). A comma (,) can be used for a weaker emotion. - Ouch! That hurts. - Well, I need a break. - Wow! What a beautiful dress! Interjections do the following: 1. Express a feeling-wow, gee, oops, darn, geez, oh: 2. Say yes or no-yes, no, nope: - Oops, I'm sorry. That was my mistake. - Geez! Do I need to do it again? - Oh, I didn't know that. 3. Call attention-yo, hey: - Yes! I will do it! - No, I am not going to go there. - Nope. That's not what I want. 4. Indicate a pause-well, um, hmm: - Yo, will you throw the ball back? - Hey, I just wanted to talk to you about the previous incident. - Well, what I meant was nothing like that. - Um, here is our proposal. - Hmm. You really need to be on a diet. What should be B's expression? A: I got a perfect score on the math exam. B: (Well. Wow! or Um.) What should be C's expression? C: ! (Nope, Hey, or Geez) My computer just broke. We have to buy some new um XXX. The butcher the baker the candlestick maker they all jumped out of a rotten potato was enough to make a man stare. Antarctica has some important features hidden by the ice. One is Lake Vostok, which has been covered by ice for at least 15 million years. The lake is 250 km long and 50 km wide. Another is the huge Gamburtsev mountain chain, which are the size of the Alps, yet entirely buried under the ice. The Gamburtsev range has a nearby massive rift valley similar to the East African Great Rift Valley. It is called the Lambert system. Title: How to Cancel Your Experian Account Online Introduction: Experian is one of the three major credit bureaus that tracks your credit history and provides you with credit reports and scores. If you have an account with them but wish to cancel it for any reason, this tutorial will guide you through the process of doing so online. This tutorial assumes that you already have access to your Experian account via their website. Key Tips and Guidelines: * Make sure you are logged into your Experian account before starting this process. * Have your personal information ready (such as full name, address, date of birth, etc.) as they may be required during cancellation. * Keep track of confirmation numbers or emails related to the cancellation. Step 1: Log in to your Experian account. Visit <https://www.experian.com/> and click on "Sign In" at the top right corner of the page. Enter your email address and password associated with your Experian account, then press "Sign In." Why this step matters: To cancel your Experian account, you need to log in first to ensure proper authentication and easy navigation throughout the cancellation procedure. Step 2: Access your account settings. After logging in, hover over your name displayed near the upper-right corner of the screen, and from the dropdown menu, select "Account Settings." Why this step matters: The "Account Settings" option allows you to manage various aspects of your Experian account, including its cancellation. Step 3: Initiate the account closure process. On the left side of the new page, locate and click the link named "Close My Account," which can usually be found under the "Subscription Information" section. Why this step matters: By clicking on "Close My Account," you begin the formal cancellation process, ensuring no further charges or services continue without your consent. Step 4: Review subscription details and confirm account closure. You might see a summary of your subscription along with additional options such as pausing membership instead of closing. Confirm if all the details mentioned are correct and proceed by selecting "Continue to Close Account" or similar button. You might also need to provide a reason for leaving, choose according to preference. Why this step matters: Double-checking your subscription details ensures there won't be any confusion about what service(s) get cancelled while preventing accidental deletion of important accounts needed later. Step 5: Verify account deactivation. Upon successful completion of the previous steps, you should receive a confirmation message informing you that your Experian account has been closed. Additionally, look out for an email sent to your registered email address summarizing the same. Why this step matters: Verifying the account deactivation confirms whether everything went smoothly, helping avoid future disputes regarding unauthorized usage or recurring payments. Conclusion: Cancelling your Experian account doesn't take much time once you know where to find the necessary links within your account settings. Being aware of your subscription details and verifying the cancellation help maintain control over your financial data and prevent unnecessary costs.
