If you're switching from an Android device to an iPhone, you might be wondering how to transfer your WhatsApp chats to your new phone. This tutorial will guide you through the process step-by-step. Please note that due to restrictions imposed by both Google Drive and iCloud, direct transfers between Android and iOS devices are not officially supported. However, there is a workaround using email chat backup. Key Tips & Guidelines: 1. Make sure you have enough storage space in your email account to accommodate the size of your chat backups. 2. The method described here only backs up text messages; media files like photos and videos won't be included in the transferred data. 3. You must perform these steps before switching to your new iPhone as they can't be done once the transfer has been made. Step 1: Backup Your Chats on Android On your Android device, open WhatsApp and go to Menu > Settings > Chats > Chat Backup. Tap 'Back Up Now' to start backing up your chats. Wait until the process finishes. Explanation: This creates a local copy of all your chats on your Android phone which we'll later access via Email. Step 2: Email the Backup File After creating the backup, stay in the same menu (Chat Backup). Tap 'Email Chat History'. You'll then be asked whether you want to Attach Media or Send Without Media. Choose according to your preference but keep in mind that selecting 'Attach Media' may result in large emails that could fail to send depending on your mail server settings. Explanation: Sending the backup file via email allows us to receive it on our iPhone where we can extract and view its contents. Step 3: Receive the Email on Your iPhone Now switch to your new iPhone. Open the received email and download the attached .txt file. Save it somewhere accessible such as Files or Notes apps. Explanation: Downloading the attachment moves the backup file from the email onto your iPhone ready for viewing. Step 4: View the Backup File Open the saved .txt file with any text editor app (like Apple's built-in Notes app) to view your backed-up chats. Explanation: Although this isn't a perfect solution because you cannot interact with the messages like you would within the actual WhatsApp app, at least you retain a record of them. Unfortunately, due to platform differences and encryption methods, true cross-platform migration of WhatsApp conversations remains unfeasible without third-party tools, which often require payment and still don't guarantee full functionality. Conclusion: While moving WhatsApp messages directly from Android to iPhone isn't straightforward, following this guide should help you preserve your chat history in some form when transitioning between devices. Remember, if retaining conversation histories is important to you, consider this before changing platforms. The Bronze Age is the time period when people made tools from an alloy (a mixture of metals) called bronze. Bronze is a mixture of mainly copper and tin: usually nine parts copper to one part tin. Materials like wood and stone were also used for tools, but bronze was better for cutting and chopping, and was easy to shape. The Bronze Age was not at the same time everywhere, because different groups of people began to use bronze at different times. In Western Europe, the Bronze Age lasted from about 2000 BC until 800 BC. In the Middle East, it started about a thousand years earlier. For example, bronze was first used in Mesopotamia around 3300 BC. Does it fit on the baby's foot? What else are you going to tell the tape recorder? I'm not going to read if you're going to be wild. So um we're wondering if you could try to a White a quick letter to him. = = = children/CHIDES_NA/HSLLD/HV5/LW/emilw5.cha = = = Calculating Profit Margin: A Comprehensive Guide Step 1: Understand Profit Margin Before diving into the calculations, it's crucial to understand what profit margin is. Profit margin is a ratio that indicates how much net profit a business makes for every dollar it generates in revenue. It's an essential metric for businesses as it provides insight into their financial health and efficiency. Key Tip: Remember, higher profit margins are generally desirable as they indicate greater profitability. However, comparing profit margins across different industries may be misleading, as some sectors naturally have lower or higher profit margins than others. Step 2: Gather Necessary Financial Information To calculate the profit margin, you need two pieces of information: total revenue (also known as gross sales) and net income (or net profit). You can find these figures on your company's income statement or profit and loss statement. If you're calculating the profit margin for a potential investment, look for this information in the company's annual report or financial statements. Guideline: When gathering data, ensure accuracy by using the most recent financial reports available. This will give you a more accurate picture of the company's current financial situation. Step 3: Calculate Net Income (Net Profit) Net income represents the amount of money left over after accounting for all expenses, taxes, and interest payments. To determine net income, subtract total expenses from total revenue. Key Formula: Net Income = Total Revenue - Total Expenses Step 4: Determine Total Revenue Total revenue refers to the sum of all earnings before deducting any costs or expenses. Essentially, it's the grand total of everything a business has sold during a specific period. Step 5: Calculate Profit Margin Ratio Now that you have both net income and total revenue, you can calculate the profit margin ratio. Divide net income by total revenue, then multiply the result by 100 to express the profit margin as a percentage. Key Formula: Profit Margin Ratio = (Net Income / Total Revenue) x 100 For example, if a company had $500,000 in net income and $2,000,000 in total revenue, its profit margin ratio would be calculated as follows: Profit Margin Ratio = ($500,000 / $2,000,000) x 100 = 0.25 x 100 = 25% This means the company earns a 25% profit margin, meaning it retains $0.25 for every dollar generated in sales. Step 6: Interpret the Results A high profit margin suggests that a business effectively manages its costs and operates efficiently. Conversely, a low profit margin could indicate poor cost management or intense competition within the industry. Analyze trends in your profit margin over time to gain insights into your company's financial performance and identify areas for improvement. Key Tips: - Compare your profit margin to industry averages to assess your relative competitiveness and financial health. - Monitor changes in your profit margin to detect early warning signs of potential problems or opportunities. By following these steps and understanding the importance of profit margin, you can accurately gauge your business's financial wellbeing and make informed decisions about future growth strategies. The name comes from the Vandals, a tribe that attacked and damaged the city of Rome in 455 CE. - Mock and Flt - English Zone - Students Corner Ratio & Proportion A ratio is a comparison of two numbers. We generally separate the two numbers in the ratio with a colon ":". Suppose we want to write the ratio of 8 and 12. We can write this as 8:12 or as a fraction 8/12, and we say the ratio is eight to twelve. - In a certain class, the ratio of passing grades to failing grades is 7 to 5. How many of the 36 students failed the course? The ratio, "7 to 5" (or 7 : 5 or 7/5), tells you that, of every 7 + 5 = 12 students, five failed. That is, 5/12 of the class flunked. Then ( 5/12 )(36) = 15 students failed. - Express the ratio in simplest form: $10 to $45 This means that you should write the ratio as a fraction, and you should then reduce the fraction: .10/45 = 2/9. This reduced fraction is the ratio's expression in simplest form. Note that the units "canceled" on the fraction, since the units, "$", were the same on both values. So there is no unit on the answer. - Express the ratio in simplest form: 240 miles to 8 gallons Depending on the text (or instructor), you may be supposed to keep the units on a ratio. In this case, you would have (240 miles)/(8 gallons) = (30 miles)/(1 gallon), or, in more common language, 30 miles per gallon. To compare ratios, write them as fractions. The ratios are equal if they are equal when written as fractions. Example: Are the ratios 3 to 4 and 6:8 equal? The ratios are equal if 3/4 = 6/8. These are equal if their cross products are equal; that is, if 3 x 8 = 4 x 6. Since both of these products equal 24, the answer is yes, the ratios are equal. Remember to be careful! Order matters!A ratio of 1:7 is not the same as a ratio of 7:1 Let x : y be a ratio, which can also be written as x/y. We will try to find out what will happen when a constant a is added both to the numerator and denominator CASE I (x/y < 1 ) If x/y<1, then addition of a constant positive number to numerator and denominator leads to a bigger ratio than the ratio itself, i.e. x/y < (a+x)/(a+y) for x/y<1 where a is a constant positive number. e.g. 1/2 is less than 1 and when we add 2 to both numerator and denominator we get 3/4 and 3/4 is grater than 1/2 . Similarly, Subtraction leads to a simillar ratio, i.e., x/y > (x-a)/(y-a) for x/y<1 Lets consider a fraction 5/10, if 5 is subtracted from numerator as well as denominator, we get 0 and it is less than 5/10(i.e. 1/2) Thus the rule for the case of subtraction is the reverse of the case of addition, as can be easily seen by the given example. CASE II (x/y > 1 ) The above rule gets totally and exactly reversed for x/y >1. Therefore, x/y > (x+a)/(y+b) and x/y < (x-a)/(y-b). A proportion is an equation with a ratio on each side. It is a statement that two ratios are equal. 3/4 = 6/8 is an example of a proportion. When one of the four numbers in a proportion is unknown, cross products may be used to find the unknown number. This is called solving the proportion. Question marks or letters are frequently used in place of the unknown number. Example: Solve for n: 1/2 = n/4. Using cross products we see that 2 x n = 1 x 4 =4, so 2n = 4. Dividing both sides by 2, n = 4 / 2 so that n = 2. 1.Compounded Ratio of two ratios a/b and c/d is ac/bd, i.e., ac : bd. Duplicate ratio of a : b is a2 : b2 Triplicate ratio of a : b is a3 : b3 Sub-duplicate ratio of a : b is a : b Sub-triplicate ratio of a : b is a : b Reciprocal ratio of a : b is b : a 2.Proportion. Four (non-zero) quantities of the same kind a, b, c, d are in proportion, written as a : b :: c : d iff a/b = c/d 3.The non-zero quantities of the same kind a, b, c, d, ... are in continued proportion iff a/b = b/c = c/d = ... In particular, a, b, c are in continued proportion iff a/b = c/d. In this case b is called the mean proportion; b = ac; c is called third proportional. If a, b, c, d are in proportion, then d is called fourth proportional. 4.Invertendo. If a : b :: c : d then b : a :: d : c Alternendo. If a : b :: c : d then a : c :: b : d Componendo. If a : b :: c : d then (a +b) : b :: (c +d) : d Dividendo. If a : b :: c : d then (a -b) : b :: (c -d) : d Componendo and dividendo. If a : b :: c : d then (a +b) : (a -b) :: (c +d) : (c -d) i.e., a/b = c/d => (a +b)/(a - b) = (c +d)/(c +d) 5.If a/b = c/d = e/f = ..., then each ratio = (a +c +e +...)/(b +d +f +...) Which is same as ( pa + qc + re +. . . )/( pb + qd + rf + . . . ) a/b = c/d = e/f =(a +c +e +...)/(b +d +f +...) = ( pa + qc + re +. . . )/( pb + qd + rf + . . . ) = ( pna + qnc + rne +. . . )1/n/( pnb + qnd + rnf + . . . )1/n where p, q, r, etc. are constants such that all of them are simultaneously not equal to zero Examples 1. If a doctor prescribes 30 units of insulin in 500 ml to be administered over 2 hours, how many drops per minute should be administered if the set is calibrated to deliver 20 drops per ml? The rate we want is 500 milliliters over two hours, which we can write as a fraction: 500 ml / 2 hr We know that there are 20 drops per milliliter, so that 20 drops/1ml = 1 since the numerator is the same amount as the denominator. If we multiply the rate by this fraction, we won't change the rate. We then have ml in the numerator and the denominator, so they will cancel: (500 ml /2hr)*(20 drops /1ml) = (500*20) drops / (2*1hr) = 5000 drops/hr We're not done yet, because we need to know the number of drops per MINUTE. But that's easy using another "unit multiplier". Since 1 hour = 60 minutes, we can write 1 hour / 60min = 1 I chose to put the hour on top to cancel the hours in the bottom of our fraction, 5000 drops/hour, when we multiply: ( 5000 drops/ 1 hour) *(1hour/60 mins) = 5000/60 drops/min = 83.333 drops/min 2. A petroleum distributor has two gasohol storage tanks, the first containing 9 percent alcohol and the second containing 12 percent alcohol. They receive an order for 300,000 gallons of 10 percent alcohol. How can they mix alcohol from the two tanks to fill this order? Let x = volume of type(1) 100-x = volume of type(2) in 100 gallons of mixture. Then 0.09x + 0.12(100-x) = 10 gallons -0.03x + 12 = 10 2 = 0.03x and so x = 66.6667 gallons So we use 66+(2/3) gallons type (1) and 33+(1/3) gallons type (2) per 100 gallons of mixture. For 300,000 gallons, we multiply up by 3000 to get 200,000 and 100,000 of type (1) and type (2) respectively. 3.A cable television company plans to begin operations in a small town. The company knows that about 600 people will subscribe to the service if the price per subscriber is $5 per month, but that for each 5-cent increase in the monthly subscription price, 4 of the original 600 people will decide not to subscribe. The office begins operations, and its total revenue for the first month is $1500. How many people have subscribed to the cable television service? Let x = number of increments of 5-cents added to the price. So the number of subscribers = 600-4x, and subscription = 5+.05x So we have: (600-4x)(5+.05x) = 1500 3000 - 20x + 30x - 0.2x^2 = 1500 -0.2x^2 + 10x + 1500 = 0 0.2x^2 - 10x - 1500 = 0 so x = -65.1387 or x = 115.13 The negative value we ignore, as the question does not say that they would gain subscribers by reducing the price. So they have increased the price by 115 increments of 5 cents = $5.75 Total cost of subscribing = $5 + $5.75 = $10.75 Total number of subscribers = 600-460 = 140 Total income = 140*10.75 = $1505 - which checks approximately. 4.Milk that has 5% butterfat is mixed with milk that has 2% butterfat. How much of each is needed to obtain 60 gallons of milk that has 3% butterfat0.05x + 0.02y = 0.03*60 = 1.8 You might like to avoid decimals by multiplying the whole equation by 100: 5x + 2y = 180 Now you have a system of simultaneous equations: x + y = 60 5x + 2y = 180 You have probably learned some method of solving this. If not, the easiest way is to solve the first equation for y: y = 60 - x and then replace y in the second equation with (60-x). This gives you a single equation to solve to find x. 5.Sterling Silver is 92.5% pure silver. How many grams of pure silver and sterling silver must be mixed to obtain 100g of a 94% Silver alloy? The amounts of the two input mixtures are unknown; the amount of the output mixture is 100g. Let x = grams of 92.5% silver alloy Then, since the total weight is 100g, we have (100-x) = grams of 100% silver We now write an equation relating the amounts of pure silver in the two "input" mixtures and in the "output" mixture: "x" grams at 92.5% silver + (100-x) grams at 100% = 100g at 94% (x)(0.925) + (100-x)(1.0) = (100)(0.94) 0.925x + 100 - x = 94 6 = 0.075x 6000 = 75x 6000/75 = x 80 = x So to make 100g of an alloy of 94% silver, you need to mix 80g of 92.5% alloy and 20g of pure silver. And now here is a completely different approach to the same problem. I prefer this method, because I find the calculations are usually easier. Understanding why this method works is probably a bit more difficult than understanding the traditional method, but it works for me, so I use it. Take a look at this alternative method and see if you like it. We have two "input" mixtures; one of 92.5% silver and the other of 100% silver. We want to make a mixture of 94% silver. If I think of plotting these percentages on a number line, I see that the "distance" from 92.5% to 94% is 1.5%, while the "distance" from 94% to 100% is 6%. And now here is the key to my method: The distances from 92.5% to 94% and from 94% to 100% are 1.5% and 6%; these two distances are in the ratio 1:4. This means that the two "input" mixtures must be mixed in the ratio 1:4 to get the 94% alloy. If there are to be 100g of the 94% alloy and the two input mixtures are in the ratio 1:4, then there must be 20g of one input and 80g of the other. Because the resulting alloy is closer to 92.5% than 100%, the required amounts of the inputs are 80g of the 92.5% alloy and 20g of the 100% silver. 6.Person A has a cup of coffee. Person B has a cup of tea. Person A takes a teaspoon of coffee and puts it in the tea. Person B mixes the teaspoon of coffee and tea, then takes a teaspoon of the mixture and returns it to the coffee. Is there more coffee in the tea, or more tea in the coffee, or are they the same? The volume of each cup after the swap is the same. That means that whatever coffee is missing from the first cup must have been replaced with exactly the same amount of tea. 7.Chris has 3 gallons of a solution that is 30 percent antifreeze, which he wants to use to winterize his car. How much pure antifreeze should he add to this solution so that the new solution will be 65 percent antifreeze? you start out with x gallons of antifreeze that's been mixed with y gallons of water, and the total is 3 gallons, so I'd say x + y = 3 is a good equation to start with. You also know that the solution is 30 percent antifreeze, so x/(x+y) = .30. Since you have two equations and two unknowns, these equations are enough to tell you both x and y. Maybe you should find x and y before going any further, and then you can write the next equation more simply. Now we want to add some more antifreeze until the total is 65 percent antifreeze. Say you're adding z gallons. The equation is (x+z)/(x+y+z) = 0.65 The x+z is the total amount of antifreeze, and the x+y+z is the total amount of solution, antifreeze+water. Since you already know x and y, you can put them into this equation and you'll just have a single equation with z in it. That should do the trick! 8.Suppose 30 liters of a solution with an unknown percentage of alcohol is mixed with 5 liters of a 90% alcohol solution. If the resulting mixture is a 62% alcohol solution, what is the percentage of alcohol in the first solution? Think of the amount of alcohol in each solution. In the first one, it is 30*(x/100) liters. In the second one, it is 5*(90/100). In the last one, it is 35*(62/100) (because the total amount of the mixture is 35 = 30 + 5 liters). When the first two solutions are mixed, the total amount of alcohol is the sum of the amounts in the two ingredients, so you get an equation: 30*(x/100) + 5*(90/100) = 35*(62/100). 9.A group of chemists are conducting an experiment to produce a new liquid material. One chemical contains 15% sodium (Na) and the other chemical contains 30% sodium (Na). Once they mix the two samples the resulting chemical contains 22% sodium (Na). How many milliliters (ml) of each sample must be mixed to obtain 600 ml of the new chemical? Since the first material is 15% sodium, it means that if we have some amount, A, of the material, we can determine the amount of sodium it contains by taking 15% of that. Does that make sense? amount of sodium from first material = 0.15*A For example, if we have 100 ml of the material, then 15 ml of it will be sodium, and 85 ml will be something else. Similarly, if B is the amount of the second material, amount of sodium from second material = 0.30*B Now, if we combine the two chemicals to get a new chemical, which is 22% sodium, then the total amount of combined sodium is amount of sodium from both sources = 0.22*(A+B) So we can use that to set up an equation: sodium from sodium from sodium from first material + second materal = both materials 0.15*A + 0.30*B = 0.22*(A+B) Now, this is kind of a problem, since we have two variables but only one equation. But in fact, we _have_ a second equation, because we know that A + B = 600 So now you have two equations, and you can use substitution or elimination to find the values of the variables: 10.An advertisement for an orange drink claims that the drink contains 10% orange juice. How much pure orange juice would have to be added to 5 quarts of the drink to obtain a mixture containing 40% orange juice? You have 5 quarts of "drink" that is 10% orange juice. Thus the 5 quarts of drink contain 5*10% = 5*.1 = .5 quarts of orange juice, and hence must contain 4.5 quarts of other stuff. Now we're going to add 100% orange juice and we want to know how much to add so that the new mixture will be 40% orange juice. Well, what if you add 1 quart of 100% orange juice? What would that do to the concentration of orange juice in the mixture? It would mean that we now have 1.5 quarts of pure orange juice mixed in a carton that now contains 6 quarts of liquid. Thus the concentration of orange juice in this 6-quart jug would be 1.5/6 = .25 = 25% So we haven't added enough, but maybe you see what's going on. In a more general situation: If we add x quarts of pure orange juice to the original mixture that contains .5 quarts of OJ and 4.5 quarts of something else, then what we have is a mixture of 5+x quarts that contains .5+x quarts of OJ. So after we've added x quarts of pure OJ, the concentration of OJ will be (.5+x)/(5+x) We want to know when this will be equal to .4 So solve for x: (.5+x)/(5+x) = .4 .5+x = 2 + .4x .6x = 1.5 x = 1.5/.6 = 2.5 Thus you should add 2.5 quarts of pure OJ to make the concentration of the new mixture 40% OJ [GAR continues to remove items from the container] No I didn't buy any for Uncle Tommy honey. That was one good thing, but look at this floor. D' ya want to look at some baby ducks? Some have suggested that God can do things that defy conventional logic. If He lives in other dimensions a different logic may apply. Some say that God cannot make a square circle. This is true in a two dimensional world. However, a cylinder can be a square viewed from the side and a circle viewed from the top. Likewise, a triangle can have three angles that add to more that 180 degrees. This is possible only on a three dimensional surface like a sphere. It can't be done on a two dimensional plane. (Plane geometry). Addition of an additional dimension changes the logic. [the washer which makes up the top of the diamond tips over and falls onto the silver stick] that's him drinking water over here. The Ross Sea was discovered by James Ross in 1841. In the west of the Ross Sea is Ross Island. In the east is Roosevelt Island. The southern part is covered by the Ross Ice Shelf. In the west of the Ross sea, McMurdo Sound is a port which is usually free of ice during the summer. The southernmost part of the Ross Sea is Gould Coast, which is about two hundred miles from the Geographic South Pole. All land masses in the Ross Sea are claimed by New Zealand. A 10 metre (32.8 feet) long colossal squid weighing 495 kilograms (1,091 lb) was captured in the Ross Sea on February 22, 2007. Ecological importance and conservation [change] The flora and fauna are similar to other southern Antarctic marine regions. In summer there are a lot of plankton. They provide food for larger species, such as fish, seals, whales, and sea- and shore-birds. The coastal parts of the sea have a number of rookeries of Ad lie and Emperor penguins. They have been seen at a number of places around the Ross Sea, both towards the coast and outwards in open sea.. The Ross Sea is one of the last parts of seas on Earth that is mostly unaffected by human activities. Because of this, it is almost totally free from pollution, overfishing and the introduction of invasive species. Some people want to make the area a world marine reserve. Materials Required: The Trumpet of the Swans written by EB White Concepts Taught: reading, ssr, think aloud The Trumpet of the Swan THINK ALOUD technique I keep the OWL questions in mind throughout the chapters and the days. I seldom cover each question on one day, but I do use the vocabulary, I observe, I wonder, This links to me . . To teach the children how to interact with text and to think about their thinking, I decided to read The Trumpet of the Swan, written by E.B. White aloud. After each chapter, I am going to think-aloud about the chapter. First read the title and the book jacket or back cover (or both). THINK ALOUD: "I wonder who Louis is. I wonder if Louis is the trumpeter swan. How did Sam Beaver and the swans start talking to each other?" Read Chapter 1's title: SAM. THINK ALOUD: "Oh the back cover said that Sam agrees to help. I wonder if Sam is the boy on the cover. Read Chapter 1 just the first page then revisit the first paragraph. THINK ALOUD: Reread the beginning: "Walking back to camp through the swamp, Sam wondered whether to tell his father what he had seen. "I know one thing," he said to himself. "I'm going to that little pond again tomorrow. And I'd like to go alone. If I tell my father what I saw today, he will want to go with me. I'm not sure that's a very good idea." THEN think aloud, "I am curious about why he doesn't want his dad to come." Finish Chapter 1 THINK ALOUD: Hmmmmmm I wonder what is going to happen? I am a little bit interested in the story but not very interested. I am going to read more tomorrow. I observed that Sam likes to write things down because right here on page 5 it says, "On the day he found the swan's nest, this is what Sam wrote in his diary." SIDE NOTE: Because this is the first time the kids are hearing a chapter book I am not going to start recording the swan facts yet. I am going to read three chapters then suggest to the class that we keep a class notebook about swans. Then I will reread chapters 1-3 skimming for facts and we'll record them on a notebook chart. What book did we start yesterday? Does anyone remember who wrote the book? (E.B. White) Who did we read about? (Sam seeing a swan, not wanting to tell dad) Who is the main character? (Sam) Read Chapter 2's title The Pond THINK ALOUD: I wonder if Sam is going to the pond alone or with his dad. Read Chapter 2 THINK ALOUD: What didn't the swans know I wonder? I wonder if Sam's dad is following Sam and Sam doesn't know it? Do you think Sam should tell his dad? I am more interested in the book now. I wish we had more time to read another chapter. Read Chapter 3's title A Visitor THINK ALOUD: I wonder who the visitor is? I wonder if the guest is Sam because he sees the swans or maybe his dad might be spying on Sam. I wonder if the swans see Sam or Sam sees the swans - maybe both are visitors. Read Chapter 3 THINK ALOUD: My favorite part of this chapter was where the boy says he felt happy when he was in a wild place among the wild creatures. Sitting on his log, watching the swans, he had the same good feeling some people get when they are sitting in church. It reminds me of when I am at the beach in the summer, watching my two kids lay in the water and feeling the sun warming my skin. Do you remember how the first chapter ended? Reread the last line from his journal: Why does a fox bark? I think the author is foreshadowing -hinting- about the trouble with the fox in this chapter. Do Rivet for the word: Cygnets The title of chapter 4 is The Cygnets What is that? A newborn trumpeter swan. I wonder if we should keep track of the facts we are learning about trumpeter swans. Let's keep a list like Sam is. Brainstorm what we know so far. If your students can't think of any, here are some pages. I would slow this way down and reread parts of the book. It isn't the point to only concentrate on the swan facts. We study animals and I like finding new facts about animals so I tie this into the book. This shouldn't be a distraction. If it bugs you don't do it. page 2 - first long paragraph page 5 - second line page 8 - last half of page page 9-14 - all of it THINK ALOUD At this point I read the title and show wonder in my eyes saying, I bet cygnets are the baby trumpeter swans. Let's find out about those babies! I bet the mama is as excited as I was when I had my babies! Read Chapter 4. THINK ALOUD I wonder why Sam fell asleep wondering about what he would be when he grew up. I thought that he would think about those baby cygnets. Read Chapter 5's title Louis. THINK ALOUD I think that we are going to find out about Louis. Read Chapter 5. THINK ALOUD This reminds me of how my mom and dad treated me. They loved me even though I started talking at around age 7 or 8. Doctors said that I would not be able to read or talk. And here I am! Read Chapter 6's title Off to Montana and jump right into Reading Chapter 6. THINK ALOUD I wonder if Sam will see the swans again? I wonder if Sam will ever tell his dad? Read Chapter 7's title School Days Read the chapter. (note to teachers - This is a good chapter to lead into problem solving in math.) THINK ALOUD I noticed that this class is unusual. It has a swan, the math lessons have more than one answer. The kids are reading big words like CATASTROPHE in first grade. (I would also use this chapter as a spring board for recording what happens in the classroom. Teachers could go and reread the journals that Sam has written and see if it tells an overview of the book.) I think cob is going to do something but I don't know if it will be a good plan. Read Chapters 9-11 continuing with making comments about the book. Dry ice does not occur naturally on earth but is man made. It is colorless. People use dry ice to make things cold, and to make drinks fizzy, kill gophers, and freeze warts. The vapor of dry ice causes suffocation and eventually, death. Caution and professional assistance is recommended whenever dry ice is in use. And I have a feeling I know what this is. Mickey traded the gold for good things to eat. in regards to geometry A cube has how many corners Answer: 8 Corners Try to picture a cube sitting in front of you and count the points on top, 4. The same is true for the side laying flat on the table. A total of 8 corners. A cube has 8 vertices (corners, points), 12 edges (line segments), and 6 faces (planes). One formula that works for any solid with flat sides and no holes is vertices+faces-edges=2. For me, I like to picture the cube as two squares with arms connecting them. I count up the corners on one square and multiply that by two. Since there are four corners on one square the equation you get is 4*2=8. So, your answer is 8. Title: How to Empty a Trash Bin Introduction: This tutorial will guide you through the process of emptying your computer's trash bin. This is an essential task for maintaining the organization and performance of your system. Whether you are new to computing or have been using computers for years, this simple guide will provide clear, concise steps to help you manage your digital waste effectively. Key Tips and Guidelines: 1. Always double-check before deleting files permanently. Once deleted, they cannot be easily recovered. 2. Regularly emptying your trash bin can help free up storage space and improve overall system performance. 3. Be cautious when handling sensitive documents; ensure they are securely disposed of if necessary. 4. Some applications may create temporary files that end up in the trash bin. Clearing these regularly can also benefit application performance. 5. The exact method might vary slightly depending on the operating system (OS) you are using. However, we will cover both Windows and macOS here. Step 1: Identify Your Operating System Determine whether you are running Microsoft Windows or Apple macOS. Each OS has its unique way of managing the trash bin. If you are unsure which one you have, refer to your device documentation or search online for specific identification methods based on your hardware brand. Step 2: Access the Trash Bin Icon For Windows users, locate the Recycle Bin icon usually found on the desktop. For macOS users, find the Trash icon located in the Dock at the bottom of the screen. Alternatively, you can access them via File Explorer (Windows) or Finder (macOS). Step 3: Open the Trash Bin Left-click once on either the Recycle Bin (Windows) or Trash (macOS) icon. This action opens the respective folders, displaying all currently stored deleted items. Step 4: Review Deleted Items (Optional but Recommended) Take some time to review what is inside the trash bin. You may accidentally delete important files, so checking before purging everything ensures no valuable data gets lost forever. To restore a file, simply drag it out of the trash bin back onto the desktop or another folder. Step 5A: Empty the Recycle Bin (for Windows Users) Right-click anywhere within the open Recycle Bin window. From the context menu, select 'Empty Recycle Bin.' Confirm the operation by clicking 'Yes' when prompted with a dialog box asking if you want to permanently delete all files in the recycle bin. OR Click on the 'Recycle Bin' option in the toolbar near the top of the window and then click on 'Empty Recycle Bin'. Again confirm the operation by clicking 'Yes' when prompted. Step 5B: Empty the Trash (for Mac Users) Right-click (or Control + Click) on the open Trash icon in the Dock. Select 'Empty Trash' from the dropdown list. A confirmation dialogue will appear warning you about irreversibly erasing the contents. Press 'Empty Trash' again to finalize the process. Alternative Method for macOS: You can also quickly empty the trash without opening it by pressing Command+Shift+Delete keys simultaneously. Confirm the deletion as described earlier. Conclusion: Congratulations! By following these straightforward steps, you have successfully emptied your trash bin, contributing to improved system efficiency and disk space management. Remember to repeat this practice routinely to maintain optimal computer health. Darwin collected everywhere the ship landed. He found huge fossils of recently extinct mammals, experienced an earthquake in Chile, and noticed the land had been raised. He knew of raised beaches elsewhere, high in the Andes, with fossil seashells and trees which had once grown on a sandy beach. Obviously the earth was constantly changing, with land rising in some places, and sinking in others. He collected birds and insects, and sent shipments back to Cambridge for experts to identify. Today the most common way of making steel is the basic-oxygen process. The converter is a large turnip-shaped vessel. Liquid raw iron called "pig iron" is poured in and some scrap metal is added in to balance the heat. Oxygen is then blown into the iron. The oxygen burns off the extra carbon and other impurities. Then enough carbon is added to make the carbon contents as wanted. The liquid steel is then poured. It can be either cast into molds or rolled into sheets, slabs, beams and other so-called "long products", such as railway tracks. [holds up a penny which he has been trying to pick up with the horseshoe magnet] If You Could Fly like a Bird From earliest times, people have dreamed of flying like a bird. Wouldn't you like to just flap your arms and fly? But birds' entire bodies, not just their wings, are built specially for flight. So to fly like a bird, you'd have to make a few changes, and you might end up looking, well, kind of like a bird. You'll need really big eyes to judge distances accurately for landings and to see the ground clearly from the sky. Teeth are way too heavy. Trade yours in for a nice lightweight beak, and while you're at it, get rid of that nose. To fly well, your entire body needs to be smooth and streamlined. You'll need feathers on your arms, of course. But your arms will have to be extra long to support your weight. A bald eagle has a 7-foot wingspan but weighs less than 15 pounds. A 60-pound kid would need wings nearly 75 feet wide! It takes a lot of muscle to keep those giant arms flapping. If, like a bird, your flight muscles were in your chest, your chest would have to be about 5 feet deep! You'll need a tail to help you steer and land. If you can fly, why walk? And if you're not walking much, you won't need big, strong legs. To stay lightweight for flying, you'll be better off with short, skinny bird legs. - accurate: Exactly right. - streamlined: Made simpler and more efficient. - What are some of the ways in which a person's body is different from a bird's body? - Do you think it is possible to make man-made wings that would allow a person to fly? Title: How to Change a Bag on a Vacuum Cleaner Introduction: Changing the bag on your vacuum cleaner is an essential maintenance task that ensures optimal performance and longevity of your device. This guide provides detailed, step-by-step instructions with clear explanations for changing a bag on various types of vacuum cleaners. Things You'll Need: 1. A new vacuum cleaner bag (compatible with your model) 2. Screwdriver or other tools specific to your model (if required) 3. Trash bag or bin 4. Gloves (optional) 5. Mask (optional) Step 1: Unplug Your Vacuum Cleaner Safety first! Always unplug your vacuum cleaner from the power source before performing any maintenance tasks. This prevents accidental activation during handling, reducing the risk of injury. Step 2: Locate the Vacuum Bag Compartment The location of the bag compartment varies depending on the type of vacuum cleaner you have. Common locations include the top, bottom, side, or back part of the machine. Refer to your user manual if needed. For upright vacuums, often there will be a hatch at the rear; canister models typically store bags within the main body. Key Tip: Familiarize yourself with your particular model so you know exactly where everything is located. Step 3: Open the Bag Door or Flap Once you find the bag compartment, open its door or flap. Some doors may require pressing buttons or sliding latches, while others might simply lift off. Again, consult your user manual as necessary. Step 4: Remove Old Vacuum Bag Carefully remove the full bag by holding onto the edges to avoid tearing. Dispose of it properly in a trash bag or bin. Be aware that dust particles could escape when removing the old bag, hence wearing gloves and masks can help maintain hygiene and protect against allergens. Step 5: Check Filter Condition While replacing the bag, check the condition of any filters nearby. If they look dirty, consider cleaning them according to manufacturer recommendations - usually rinsing under warm water then letting dry completely. Replace severely worn out filters. Key Guideline: Regularly maintaining filters prolongs their lifespan and enhances overall suction power. Step 6: Install New Vacuum Bag Take out the fresh bag from its packaging. Match up the seams of the new bag with those on the opening of the bag chamber. Insert the bag firmly but gently into place, ensuring all sides are correctly aligned. Close the bag door securely after installation. Step 7: Test Run the Vacuum Cleaner Plug in your vacuum cleaner and test run it briefly to ensure proper operation. Listen for unusual noises indicating incorrect assembly, such as paper rubbing against internal components. Also, verify that suction has returned to normal levels. Additional Tips: 1. Keep extra bags handy for quick replacements without interrupting cleaning routines. 2. Store replacement bags away from direct sunlight and extreme temperatures to prevent degradation. 3. Never reuse vacuum cleaner bags, which reduces efficiency and potentially damages machinery. 4. Consider switching to reusable cloth bags if environmental impact concerns you. Conclusion: By following these steps meticulously, you've successfully changed the bag on your vacuum cleaner! Proper maintenance guarantees efficient performance, saving time and energy over time. Happy cleaning! She's having a good time in there. So he was late for the school bus. When Chi's actually going to eat what we put on the plate in front him. Title: Catch Any Pokemon in Pokemon Emerald Using Gameshark Codes Introduction: This tutorial will guide you through the process of using Gameshark codes to catch any Pokemon in Pokemon Emerald. These steps are applicable for both the Gameboy Advance (GBA) and Nintendo DS versions of the game. Please note that using cheats can take away from the challenge and enjoyment of the game, so proceed with caution. For this example, we'll be using two primary codes: * Master Code: D8BAE4D9 5F9BD642 The story of the golden crown does not appear in the surviving works of Archimedes. Archimedes may have got a solution known in hydrostatics as Archimedes' principle, which he describes in his treatise "On Floating Bodies". This principle states that a body immersed in a fluid experiences a buoyant force equal to the weight of the fluid it displaces. Using this principle, it would have been possible to compare the density of the golden crown to that of solid gold by balancing the crown on a scale with a gold reference sample, then immersing the apparatus in water. The difference in density between the two samples would cause the scale to tip accordingly. Galileo considered it "probable that this method is the same that Archimedes followed, since, besides being very accurate, it is based on demonstrations found by Archimedes himself". He thought I said something 'bout window. A central idea in general relativity is the "principle of equivalence." An example is that two people, one in an elevator sitting on the surface of the earth, and the other in an elevator in outer space accelerating at 9.8 m/s, will each observe the same behavior of an object they drop from their hand. The object will accelerate to the floor at 9.8 m/s in either case, making it impossible for either to distinguish whether or not they are at rest in a gravitational field or accelerating upward at one . Other versions of this type of "thought experiment" were used to show that light would curve in an accelerating frame of reference. There are several forms of the equivalence principle. These include: Newton's equivalence principle, the weak equivalence principle, the gravitational weak equivalence principle, Einstein's equivalence principle and the strong equivalence principle. Title: How to Check the Version of Microsoft Excel You Are Using Microsoft Excel is one of the most widely used spreadsheet programs worldwide. It has been around since 1985 and has seen numerous updates and improvements over the years. Knowing which version you are using can be helpful when troubleshooting issues or seeking specific features. This tutorial will guide you through checking your Microsoft Excel version in detail. **Step 1: Open Microsoft Excel** Start by launching the Microsoft Excel application on your device. Whether you're using Windows, macOS, or another operating system, finding the Excel icon should be straightforward. Clicking this icon will open the program and present you with its interface. **Step 2: Locate the File Tab** Once you have successfully opened Microsoft Excel, look at the top left corner of the window. Here, you'll find several tabs labeled Home, Insert, Page Layout, etc. Identify and click on the 'File' tab. This action opens up a new menu containing various options related to managing files within Excel. **Step 3: Select 'Account' Option** From the list displayed after clicking on the File tab, locate and select the 'Account' option near the bottom-left side of the screen. The Account section provides information about user settings, privacy controls, product activation status, and more. **Step 4: View Product Information** Underneath the User Image (which might showcase an initial if no picture was set), there should be a subheading called 'Product Information.' Under this heading, you'll see details such as 'Product Name,' 'License Type,' and crucially, 'Version.' The number next to 'Version' represents your current Microsoft Excel build number. Key Tips & Guidelines: - **Understanding Build Numbers:** Generally, higher build numbers indicate newer versions of software. However, sometimes interim builds may contain bug fixes but won't necessarily represent a major upgrade. For instance, a version listed as 20.0.0.0 could signify Office 365 subscription-based releases while 16.0.0.0 refers to Office 2019 standalone purchase. - **Determining Major Versions:** If unsure what significant differences exist between different major versions, consider visiting Microsoft's official website or other reliable resources online that outline distinctions clearly. These sources usually highlight feature enhancements, deprecated functionalities, and UI changes among others. - **Identifying Compatibility Issues:** When sharing workbooks cross-version, note potential compatibility problems due to varying file formats. Older versions of Excel may struggle opening documents saved in modern XML-based formats introduced post-Excel 2007. Always save copies in compatible formats where necessary. By following these steps meticulously, users should now confidently check their Microsoft Excel version without external assistance. Familiarizing oneself with this process enables quick resolution whenever encountered with technical hitches requiring knowing the exact software build. As speeds get closer to the speed of light, then the changes in mass become impossible not to notice. The basic experience we all share in daily life is that the harder we push something like a car the faster we can get it going. But when something we are pushing is already going at some large part of the speed of light we find that it keeps gaining mass, so it gets harder and harder to get it going faster. It is impossible to make any mass go at the speed of light because to do so would take infinite energy. Title: How to Hide Things in a Book **Introduction:** Hiding things in a book is an age-old trick that has been used by spies, secret agents, and ordinary people who want to keep their valuables safe. This method is simple yet effective because books are common items found in most homes and offices, making them ideal for concealing small objects without raising suspicion. Here's a detailed guide on how to hide things in a book. **Step 1: Choose the Right Book** Key Tip: Select a book that doesn't attract much attention. It should be similar to other books around it regarding size, genre, author, etc. Avoid using rare or valuable editions as they might draw unwanted interest. The first step towards hiding something in a book involves choosing the right book. You need a book with thick pages so you can carve out enough space between them without damaging the entire structure. Hardcover books are generally better than paperbacks due to their sturdiness. Also, consider picking a less popular book from your collection; this way, there's less chance anyone will notice if it goes missing or looks different. **Step 2: Prepare Your Tools** Required Tools: A ruler, utility knife (or any sharp blade), glue stick, and the item you wish to hide. Before proceeding, gather all necessary tools. Using a ruler will help ensure straight cuts, reducing the risk of tearing or damaging the pages. A utility knife or any sharp blade will allow precise cutting. Glue sticks will secure the hidden object inside the book once you're done. **Step 3: Mark the Pages** Using your ruler, measure about halfway down the page where you intend to make the cut. Make light pencil marks at these points across several consecutive pages. These markings will serve as guides when slicing through the pages. **Step 4: Cut Along the Marked Lines** Carefully insert your blade into one of the marked lines and start slicing along the length of the page, stopping just before reaching the edge. Repeat this process for both sides of each marked page until you have created a pocket deep enough to hold your item firmly. Be careful not to cut too deeply or unevenly, which could weaken the structural integrity of the book. **Step 5: Test Fit the Item** Slip the item into the slits you've made to check whether it fits snugly within the pockets. If it's loose, consider adding more pages to the stack you've carved out. Conversely, if it's too tight, widen the slots slightly but avoid overdoing it. **Step 6: Secure the Item** Once you're satisfied with the fit, remove the item and apply some glue to the edges of the carved-out area. Wait for the adhesive to become tacky, then slide the item back in place. Press gently to ensure good contact between the glued surfaces. Allow the glue to dry completely before closing the book. **Step 7: Camouflage the Book** To further discourage suspicion, rearrange the books on your shelf so the altered one blends in naturally. Alternatively, you could place it alongside others of the same genre or author. **Conclusion:** While hiding things in a book is relatively straightforward, remember that practice makes perfect. Don't rush the process - take your time to ensure clean cuts and a well-hidden object. With patience and care, you can turn almost any book into a stealthy storage container! Title: Convincing Your Parents to Turn Off Their Loud Obnoxious Country Music in the Car Introduction: This wikiHow will guide you through the process of convincing your parents to turn down or off their loud obnoxious country music while driving. It is important to approach this situation with respect, understanding, and patience. By following these steps, you can effectively communicate your preferences and increase the likelihood of reaching a mutually agreeable solution. Step 1: Choose the Right Time and Place Find an appropriate time and place to bring up the subject. This should be outside of the car when both of you are calm and free from distractions. Explaining your concerns during a heated moment might lead to misunderstandings and further escalate tensions. Instead, choose a quiet, comfortable setting where both parties can engage in open communication. Key tip: Avoid criticizing or blaming them for their musical choices. Rather, focus on expressing your own feelings about the volume and genre of the music. Step 2: Express Your Feelings Politely Begin by acknowledging that you appreciate their willingness to listen to your thoughts and opinions. Then, explain why the loud country music negatively impacts you without being confrontational or aggressive. Use "I" statements instead of accusing or attacking language. For example, say something like, "I find it difficult to enjoy our car rides because I'm sensitive to loud noises," rather than saying, "Your music is too loud and hurts my ears." Guideline: Clearly articulate what you hope to accomplish (e.g., turning down the music, listening to different genres) but remain flexible and open-minded to alternative solutions. Step 3: Show Empathy and Understanding Recognize that everyone has unique tastes and preferences when it comes to music. Acknowledge your parents' love for country music and thank them for considering your perspective. Emphasize that you value spending quality time together as a family, regardless of whether or not you share the same taste in music. Key Tip: Validate their emotions and experiences related to music, even if they differ from yours. Doing so fosters mutual respect and encourages compromise. Step 4: Propose Alternative Solutions Suggest potential alternatives that cater to both parties' needs and wants. Some ideas include taking turns playing DJ, creating playlists that incorporate various genres, or using headphones or earbuds when listening to personal devices. These compromises demonstrate maturity and show that you are committed to finding a resolution acceptable to all involved. Guideline: Be prepared to offer multiple suggestions and negotiate terms until an agreement is reached. Remember that successful conflict resolution often requires flexibility and adaptability. Step 5: Follow Through With Agreed Upon Changes Once you have agreed upon new ground rules regarding in-car music, make sure to adhere to them consistently. Demonstrating responsibility and commitment increases trust between you and your parents and reinforces the notion that you take their concerns seriously. Over time, consistent follow-through may encourage them to reciprocate similar behavior towards your preferences. Key Tip: Establish clear expectations about future car rides and hold yourself accountable for maintaining any agreements made during discussions. Conclusion: By approaching this issue thoughtfully and tactfully, you can successfully convince your parents to adjust their loud obnoxious country music habits while driving. The skills developed throughout this process-such as effective communication, empathy, and problem-solving-will serve you well in other areas of life, helping you navigate conflicts and build stronger relationships with those around you. Title: Best Lighting for Makeup Application - A Comprehensive Guide Introduction: Applying makeup requires proper lighting to ensure that you look your best. Natural light is ideal, but not always available. This guide will help you understand the importance of good lighting, different types of artificial lights, their color temperature, and how to set them up for optimal makeup application. By following these steps, you can achieve a flawless and consistent look every time. Step 1: Understanding the Importance of Good Lighting Good lighting allows you to see your face clearly, ensuring even foundation coverage, true-to-life colors, and precise eye and lip lining. Poor lighting may result in uneven application, unblended edges, or mismatched shades. Step 2: Choosing the Right Type of Artificial Lights There are three main types of artificial lights used for makeup application: LED, Fluorescent, and Incandescent bulbs. Each has its own advantages and disadvantages. * LED (Light Emitting Diodes): They produce cool white light with minimal heat output. High CRI (Color Rendering Index) LEDs provide excellent color accuracy. However, lower quality ones might distort colors slightly. Look for bulbs marked 90+ CRI for accurate representation. * Fluorescent Bulbs: These emit diffused soft light which reduces shadows. But they often have low CRI values causing color discrepancies. Full-spectrum fluorescents come closest to natural daylight but still lag behind high-CRI LEDs in terms of color fidelity. * Incandescent Bulbs: While providing warm, inviting light, incandescents aren't energy efficient and generate significant heat. Their orange cast affects color perception negatively making them less suitable for makeup application. Key Tip: Always choose lights with a high CRI value (above 90). Higher CRIs mean more realistic rendering of colors. Step 3: Selecting the Correct Color Temperature Color temperature, measured in Kelvin (K), indicates whether a light appears 'warm' or 'cool'. Lower temperatures give off yellowish/orangish hues while higher temps yield bluer/whiter tones. Ideally, aim for around 5000K as this most closely mimics neutral daylight. Step 4: Positioning Your Lights Properly Place two light sources at an angle of about 45 degrees from either side of your face. This setup illuminates all features uniformly, minimizing harsh shadows. If possible, position another light directly above or slightly in front of you to eliminate any remaining shadows under eyes or nose bridge. Step 5: Avoiding Common Mistakes Avoid placing lights too close or far away because both situations create unflattering shadows. Also, avoid using only overhead lighting since it creates dark circles under your eyes. Lastly, steer clear of colored bulbs as they alter the appearance of makeup colors. Guidelines To Follow: 1. Test multiple options before committing to one; consider brightness, warmth, and cost efficiency. 2. Consider investing in professional makeup vanities or ring lights designed specifically for makeup applications if frequent usage necessitates it. 3. Regularly clean your light fixtures to maintain luminosity and reduce potential bacterial growth. Conclusion: Investing time and effort into setting up appropriate lighting conditions significantly improves the overall process and results of applying makeup. With the right tools and knowledge, anyone can enjoy salon-quality looks at home! [MOT and Chi remove objects from the black base and the yellow wand] Okay you got it all set up? This is so that the people will think about the prayers better. A shadow is a dark outline or image cast by an object that blocks light. It is formed when light hits an opaque object that does not let the light pass through. Everywhere else around the opaque object, the light continues in a straight path until it bounces off the ground or wall behind the object. The result is a dark patch, or shadow, with the same outline as the object surrounded by light. Explore some of the features of shadows, such as how the size of a shadow and the size of the object that produced the shadow are related. - White paper or poster board - Ruler or measuring tape - Objects to produce shadows - Science notebook - Graph paper Investigation 1: Outdoor Shadow Activity Find a partner. Go outside and measure his or her height. Have your partner stand facing the sun and draw the shadow that is created. Then answer the following questions in your science notebook: How was the shadow made? What is the length of the shadow? How does this compare with the height of your partner? What affects the height and position of the shadow? Does the shadow give an accurate picture of the shape of your partner's body? Explain why or why not. Draw your partner's shadow again, but this time have your partner turn with his or her shoulder facing the sun. Compare the two drawings you made and write a paragraph about how they are alike and how they are different. Investigation 2: Changing the Length of Shadows Choose several objects from around the room (books, papers, or items that your teacher has provided). Choose one thing that you can see through and one object that you cannot see through. Darken the classroom so that you can use your flashlight to create shadows. Take the object you can see through and your flashlight and produce a shadow. What does the shadow look like? Can you change the length of the shadow? How? Use your flashlight to form a shadow using the object that you cannot see through. What does the shadow look like? How is it similar to the shadow you made in #2 above? How is it different? Investigation 3: Comparing Shapes and Shadows Choose a two-dimensional (flat) object and use your flashlight to produce a shadow. Then choose an object that is three-dimensional and use your flashlight to produce a shadow. Answer the following questions in your science notebook: How are the shadows produced by these objects alike? How are the shadows produced by these objects different? List any questions you still have about shadows in your science notebook and suggest ways that you can answer them. Investigation 4: What Affects the Size of a Shadow? Use the materials in Science, Optics & You to determine what affects the size of a shadow In your science notebook, write your plan for finding the information. Then, share your plan with a classmate and put your plan into action! Write a description of how your plan worked in your science notebook and discuss your findings. Cardboard boxes were first produced commercially in 1817 in England. Corrugated (also called pleated) paper was patented in England in 1856, used as a liner for tall hats, but corrugated cardboard would not be patented and used as a shipping material until 20 December 1871. The patent was issued to Albert Jones of New York City, New York for single-sided corrugated cardboard. Jones used the corrugated cardboard for wrapping bottles and glass lantern chimneys. The first machine for producing large quantities of corrugated cardboard was built in 1874 by G. Smyth, and in the same year Oliver Long improved upon Jones' design by inventing corrugated cardboard with liner sheets on both sides. This was now cardboard as we know it today. Tom and Mia were playing with a balloon in the park. They liked to toss it to each other and watch it float in the air. Sometimes, they would try to catch it with their palms, but it was slippery and bouncy. "Look, Tom, I can hold it with one hand!" Mia said, as she squeezed the balloon with her fingers. "Wow, you are great, Mia!" Tom said, as he clapped his hands. "Can I try?" "Sure, here you go!" Mia said, as she released the balloon and threw it to Tom. Tom reached out his palm and tried to grab the balloon, but he missed. The balloon flew away from him and towards a big tree. "Oh no, the balloon!" Tom said, as he ran after it. Mia followed him, but they were too late. The balloon got stuck on a branch and popped. "Pop!" They heard a loud noise and saw the balloon burst into pieces. They stopped and looked at each other. They felt sad and disappointed. "I'm sorry, Tom, it was my fault," Mia said, as she hugged him. "No, it's okay, Mia, it was an accident," Tom said, as he hugged her back. They decided to go back to their mom and dad, who were sitting on a bench nearby. They told them what happened and asked for another balloon. Mom and dad smiled and gave them a new balloon. They said they were proud of them for being kind and sharing. Tom and Mia thanked them and went back to playing. They were happy again and forgot about the popped balloon. Title: How to Catch a Ghost Crab Ghost crabs are fascinating creatures that can be found along the Atlantic coasts of North and South America, as well as parts of Africa and Asia. They are named for their ability to quickly disappear into the sand, leaving only their eyes visible above ground. If you're interested in catching these elusive critters, here is an extensive guide to help you succeed. **Step 1: Research Your Local Regulations** Before embarking on your ghost crab hunting adventure, make sure to check local regulations regarding collection limits and size restrictions. Some areas may require permits or prohibit collecting altogether. Be respectful of nature and always adhere to posted rules and regulations. **Step 2: Choose Appropriate Equipment** To catch a ghost crab, you will need: - A flashlight (preferably red light; more about this later) - A bucket or container with air holes - A net or scoop (optional but recommended) Red lights don't disturb them as much because they cannot see the color red, reducing stress when capturing and handling them. **Step 3: Timing Is Everything** Plan your outing during low tide, preferably at night since ghost crabs are most active then. The darkness provides cover for these nocturnal animals while minimizing human interference. **Step 4: Find Suitable Habitat** Look for wet sand near bodies of water where there's plenty of seaweed and shell debris - perfect hiding spots for ghost crabs! Also, keep an eye out for burrows leading down to their underground homes. **Step 5: Spotting Ghost Crabs** Scan the area using your red flashlight. Remember, only their eyes should be visible if they're peeking out from their burrows. Once spotted, move slowly and quietly towards the crab to avoid spooking it back into its hole. **Step 6: Capture Techniques** There are two common methods for catching ghost crabs: * **The Scoop Method**: Use a small shovel or specialized crabbing scoop to gently dig around the entrance of the burrow. When the crab emerges, swiftly place the scoop under it and lift it up. Transfer the crab carefully into your prepared bucket. * **The Net Method**: Shine your red light directly onto the crab's eyes. Slowly approach it holding a fine mesh net flat against the sand. Quickly slide the net over the crab and lift it off the ground. Again, transfer the caught crab gently into your bucket. Remember, handle all crabs delicately to prevent injury. Never pull a crab out by its legs or pincers. **Step 7: Proper Housing** Ensure your bucket has enough oxygen circulation by making several small holes in the lid or sides. Keep the habitat moist but not soaked. Avoid exposing the crabs to direct sunlight or extreme temperatures. **Step 8: Observation Time** Spend some time observing these intriguing creatures before releasing them back into their natural environment. Note their behaviors, feeding habits, and unique physical characteristics. **Step 9: Release Safely** After observation, return the crabs to the exact location where they were found. This ensures minimal disruption to their ecosystem and reduces potential harm to the released individuals. By following these steps, you'll have a higher chance of successfully catching and releasing ghost crabs without causing undue distress or harm. Happy crabbing! Mary Kathryn Wheeler Rationale: Phonemic awareness is a very important part of learning to read. Phonemic awareness means understanding the different vocal gestures. It's also important for students to understand the correspondence between written letters and vocal gestures. Vowels are especially important, and often tricky, for young readers. This lesson will guide students to understand the correspondence a = /a/ by explicitly teaching, through a letterbox lesson, that the letter a makes the /a/ sound. This lesson will also help them write the letter a. Picture of crying baby Poster of tongue twister: "Annie saw Ally the alligator at the animal store." Primary paper for each student White board Markers for white board A Cat Nap by Sheila Cushman One list of pseudo-words: wan, dag, taf, gan, and jat Letterboxes for each student Letter tiles for each student Large index cards with words: scram, trap, cat, last, camp, stand 1. Today you are going to learn that the letter a makes the /a/ sound. It kind of sounds like a crying baby. Show crying baby picture. Waaaa--aaaa--waaaaaaaaa. Do you hear the /a/ sound? Let's all pretend to be a crying baby. Waa--aaaaa--aaaaaaaa. Notice how your mouth is most of the way open and your tongue touches the back of your bottom teeth. Let's make the sound again: Waaaa--aaaaa--aaaaaaa. Good! That's the sound that the letter a makes. 2. Now let's practice making this sound in a tongue twister. Show tongue twister poster. Annie saw Ally the alligator at the animal store. Now you say it with me: Annie saw Ally the alligator at the animal store. Good. Now let's listen for the /a/ sound--stretch out the /a/ sound this time as we say the tongue twister together. Aaaaaaaannie saw Aaaaaaaally the aaaaaaaalligator aaaat the aaaaaaaanimal store. Great! I hope you could feel your mouth making our special sound. 3. Now let's try listening for the /a/ sound in different words. Do you hear /a/ in animal or monkey? What about in plant or flower? Cat or dog? Good! 4. You guys have gotten so good at finding the /a/ sound in spoken words. Now let's practice writing the letter a. Start at the fence and go around in a circle--the bottom of the circle should touch the sidewalk. Then put a straight line from the sidewalk (on the right side of the circle) to just above the fence. Like this: (demonstrate on the board). Now you try a few on your own. Everyone take out a piece of paper and a pencil and write five lowercase letter a. Walk around and assist students for a few moments. You guys did a great job with that. Assess whether or not each student correctly wrote the letter a. This will be a formative assessment. 5. I'd like for everyone to take out their letters and their letterboxes now. Spread out the letters so you can clearly tell what they are. Remember to put only one sound in each box. Watch me as I model spelling the word trap. Traaaaaap. Model for students how to put the a in the third box, then the t, then r, then p--sounding out each letter sound as I put it in the correct box. Now I want you guys to put three boxes out and spell the word cat. Call on student to share her spelling. Good. Now try lap. Good. Now let's make it four boxes. Spell camp. Good. Now try last. Good. Now let's make it five boxes. Spell stand. Very good! You guys are such good spellers, and you've gotten especially good at using the letter a! Now we're going to read some words. I'll show you how. Hold up card that says scram. Read: scram. aaaa--aamm--sss-cc-rr-am--scram. Now you try! Hold up cards for: trap, cat, lap, camp, last, and stand. 6. I will now pass out the story A Cat Nap to each student. We are going to read a book called A Cat Nap. In the book, Tab is a fat cat who likes to sleep in a bag. Sam is his owner and keeps his baseball bat in the bag. Uh oh--Sam takes the bag with him to his game! What do you think is going to happen to Tab? Have students read the book to themselves. If you have trouble, raise your hand and I can come help you. When the students are finished reading, I will ask them what words they read that had the /a/ sound in them. I will write these words on the board. Assessment: I will call on students one by one to read a list of five pseudo-words containing the a = /a/ correspondence. They will come to my desk and read them to me quietly while other students are doing other work. The pseudo-words will be wan, dag, taf, gan, and jat. Coker, Mandy. "Inside the Igloo." Title: How to Change the Volume of iPhone Calls Introduction: This tutorial will guide you through changing the volume of incoming and outgoing calls on your iPhone. This can be particularly useful if you find yourself consistently struggling to hear callers or if you want to avoid disturbing others around you with loud volumes. By following these steps, you'll be able to adjust the call volume to suit your preferences. Key Tips and Guidelines: 1. Make sure your iPhone isn't set to silent mode. Check the side switch-if it's positioned towards the screen (vibration mode), slide it away from the screen (ring mode). A bell icon should appear when it's in ring mode. 2. During a call, using the physical buttons is the quickest way to change the volume. If you don't have immediate access to these buttons, you may also change the volume via Settings. 3. The volume levels are shared between various functions like media playback, ringer, and alerts. Adjustments made while playing music or watching videos will affect call volume too. 4. Be cautious about turning up the volume excessively as this might damage your hearing over time. Step-by-step Instructions: Changing Volume during a Call Using Buttons: Step 1: Receive or initiate a call Pick up an incoming call by swiping right on the "Slide to Answer" bar or dial a number and tap the green phone icon to make an outbound call. Step 2: Locate the volume buttons On the left edge of your iPhone, there are two circular buttons - one higher than the other. The upper button increases the volume, and the lower decreases it. These buttons control multiple aspects of your device's sound output including call volume. Step 3: Press the appropriate button To increase the call volume press the upper button; to decrease it, press the lower button. Each press will cycle through several levels indicated visually on-screen next to the speaker icon. Adjusting Volume Through Settings App: Step 1: Open Settings app Tap the grey gear icon labeled 'Settings', usually found on your home screen or within the app library. Step 2: Scroll down and select Sounds & Haptics From the list of settings categories, scroll down until you see 'Sounds & Haptics'. Tapping here opens audio controls related to your iPhone's ringer, keyboard clicks, and more. Step 3: Manage Ringer and Alerts slider Under 'Sound and Vibration Patterns', locate 'Ringer and Alerts'. Dragging the white circle along the gray slider changes the overall system volume which includes call volume. Moving the circle toward the right amplifies the sound, whereas moving it to the left reduces it. Conclusion: By understanding where and how to modify your iPhone's call volume, you now have greater control over your calling experience, whether ensuring privacy during quiet hours or improving audibility in noisy environments. Regularly review your volume settings to ensure they remain optimized based on contextual needs. Title: How to Properly Place Your Hand on Your Thigh Introduction: Placing your hand on your thigh might seem like a simple task, but there are correct ways to do it that can help improve posture, reduce tension, and even make you appear more confident. This tutorial will provide an in-depth guide on how to properly place your hand on your thigh, complete with clear instructions, helpful tips, and guidelines. Step 1: Sit Comfortably Find a chair or seat where you can sit upright with both feet flat on the floor. Make sure your back is straight and your shoulders are relaxed. If necessary, adjust your seating position so that you feel comfortable and supported. A good sitting posture sets the foundation for correctly placing your hand on your thigh. Key tip: Keeping your spine aligned reduces strain on your muscles and promotes better overall health. Step 2: Position Your Legs Place both feet firmly on the ground, approximately shoulder-width apart. Allow your knees to maintain a natural bend without pressing them together or splaying them outwards. This balanced leg position ensures equal support for your upper body when resting your hand on your thigh. Guideline: Maintain a neutral pelvic tilt by tucking your tailbone slightly under while engaging your core muscles. Step 3: Choose Your Resting Hand Decide which hand you'd like to rest on your thigh. Typically, this would be the hand closest to the thigh being rested upon (left hand on left thigh, right hand on right thigh). However, if one arm feels particularly tired or sore, consider using the other hand instead. Key tip: Alternate hands periodically to prevent overusing either arm. Step 4: Find the Ideal Placement Point Locate the midpoint between your knee and hip joint - generally around two thirds up from the knee. Gently lay your forearm or wrist area at this point, ensuring minimal contact with your clothing or skin. Avoid applying too much pressure as excessive force may restrict blood flow and cause discomfort. Guideline: Adjust your seated height if needed; your elbow should ideally form a roughly 90-degree angle when placed on your thigh. Step 5: Relax Your Shoulders As you settle into this new position, take note of any lingering tension in your neck or shoulders. Breathe deeply and release unnecessary stress, letting gravity pull your shoulder blades downward toward your waistline. By relaxing these areas, you create space for unrestricted circulation through your arms and hands. Key tip: Set reminders throughout the day to check in with your posture and ensure continued relaxation. Step 6: Monitor Blood Flow After several minutes, reassess whether your hand placement still allows proper blood flow. Tingling sensations or numbness could indicate restricted circulation due to incorrect positioning or prolonged immobility. When this occurs, simply reposition your hand further away from your knee crease, reducing pressure on underlying nerves and vessels. Guideline: Regularly changing positions every 20-30 minutes prevents potential issues related to poor ergonomics. Conclusion: By following these steps, you now know how to properly place your hand on your thigh, promoting improved posture, reduced muscle tension, and increased comfort during extended periods of sitting. Remember to maintain awareness of your body alignment and regularly assess your need for movement breaks. With practice, incorporating this technique into daily life becomes second nature, enhancing overall wellbeing and fostering healthy habits. And the play food she loves play food. Some architecture, even some paintings, are thousands of years old, but old music could not survive until people learned to write it down. The only way we can guess about early music is by looking at very old paintings that show people playing musical instruments, or by finding them in archaeological digs (digging underground to find old things). The earliest piece of music that was ever written down and that has not been lost was discovered on a tablet written in Hurrian, a language spoken in and around northern Mesopotamia (where Iraq is today), from about 1500 BC. The speed of the solar wind was measured by the Ulysses spacecraft. The red and blue arrows show how fast the solar wind was "blowing". Longer arrows show higher speeds. The picture on the left shows the Sun at "solar min" when there are few sunspots. The picture on the right shows the "solar max" period of a sunspot cycle. That's when the Sun is very active and there are usually lots of sunspots. At solar min, the fast solar wind (~750 km/sec) flows outward from coronal holes near the poles. The slow solar wind (~400 km/sec) flows more slowly from the Sun's equator. At solar max the Sun's magnetic field is a scrambled mess. The speed of the solar wind is not really related to latitude at solar max. Images courtesy of the ESA. No because we're going to have lunch in just a little while.
