Title: How to Be Confident Around Your Crush (Part 1) ===================================================== Introduction ------------ Having a crush can be both exciting and nerve-wracking at the same time. You might feel self-conscious or anxious when you're around them, which could prevent you from showing your true self. Building confidence around your crush is essential to forming a genuine connection and potentially pursuing a relationship with them. This wikiHow will guide you through various steps that will help you build your confidence and make a positive impression. Part one focuses on understanding your feelings, improving yourself, and creating opportunities to interact with your crush. ### Step 1: Understand and Accept Your Feelings * **Explanation:** Recognizing and accepting your emotions towards your crush are crucial starting points. It enables you to understand why you behave nervously and gives you an opportunity to work on building up your confidence. * **Instructions:** Identify what makes you nervous about being around your crush. Common reasons include fear of rejection, low self-esteem, or worrying they won't like you back. Admit these feelings to yourself and acknowledge their validity. Remind yourself that having such feelings is entirely normal and human. ### Step 2: Work on Self-Improvement * **Explanation:** Focusing on personal development allows you to grow as an individual and become more confident overall. When you feel good about yourself, this positivity radiates outwardly and attracts others, including your crush. * **Instructions:** + Pursue hobbies and interests that bring joy and fulfillment into your life. These activities can also serve as conversation starters with your crush. + Set achievable goals for yourself and celebrate small wins along the way. Success boosts your sense of worth and self-confidence. + Maintain good physical health by eating well, exercising regularly, and practicing proper hygiene habits. A healthy body contributes significantly to mental wellbeing and increases energy levels. + Read books, attend seminars, or watch videos related to developing social skills and confidence. Apply newfound knowledge and strategies daily to improve interactions with everyone, including your crush. ### Step 3: Dress Comfortably but Attractively * **Explanation:** Presenting yourself physically in a way that feels comfortable yet appealing enhances your confidence. By feeling good about your appearance, you exude positivity and charisma, making you more attractive to your crush. * **Instructions:** + Determine your unique style based on clothing items and colors that suit your personality and figure best. But what I mean to say, and do say is, that as the functions of Toby's body, his digestive organs for example, did of their own cunning, and by a great many operations of which he was altogether ignorant, and the knowledge of which would have astonished him very much, arrive at a certain end; so his mental faculties, without his privity or concurrence, set all these wheels and springs in motion, with a thousand others, when they worked to bring about his liking for the Bells. They were so mysterious, often heard and never seen; so high up, so far off, so full of such a deep strong melody, that he regarded them with a species of awe; and sometimes when he looked up at the dark arched windows in the tower, he half expected to be beckoned to by something which was not a Bell, and yet was what he had heard so often sounding in the Chimes. Find an iPhone Backup on a Mac ============================== This tutorial will guide you through the process of finding your iPhone backup on a Mac computer. By following these steps, you'll be able to locate and access your iPhone backups, which can be useful when transferring data to a new device or recovering lost information. ### Step 1: Open Finder Launch **Finder** on your Mac by clicking its icon (a smiling face) located in the Dock at the bottom of your screen. If the Dock is hidden, press `Cmd` + `Space` to open Spotlight search, then type "Finder" and hit Enter. Key tip: The Finder app serves as the file manager and primary user interface for interacting with files and folders on macOS. It provides easy navigation between different storage locations, including local drives, network volumes, and cloud services. ### Step 2: Access Local Drives In the left sidebar of the Finder window, find and click on the entry named **"Locations."** This section lists all connected devices and mounted disk partitions available on your system, such as internal hard drives, external USB drives, and memory cards. Guideline: Locations usually include the startup drive (labeled as 'Macintosh HD'), any additional internal drives, and removable media if plugged into your Mac. Make sure your Mac is awake and functioning properly before proceeding. ### Step 3: Navigate to Library folder Within the 'Locations' list, select your Startup Drive (usually labeled 'Macintosh HD'). Then, click on the **Go** menu at the top of the Finder window and choose **Go To Folder...**. Type the following path exactly as shown below and click **Go**: ```arduino ~/Library/Application Support/MobileSync/Backup/ ``` Explanation: We are navigating directly to the MobileSync folder within your User Library, where iOS backups are stored. Note that this location is typically hidden from regular users; hence using the Go To Folder method allows us to access it quickly. ### Step 4: Identify your iPhone Backups Upon entering the correct path, you should now see one or more folders representing your iPhone backups. These folders contain random alphanumeric characters followed by a timestamp indicating when they were created. To identify your specific iPhone backup(s), look for unique identifiers in their names, like UDID (Unique Device Identifier). You may need to compare these values with those displayed in iTunes during previous sync sessions or obtain them via third-party tools. Key Tip: Alternatively, you could sort the contents of the Backup folder by Date Created to make it easier to spot recent backups made by your iPhone. However, keep in mind that manually identifying backups might become challenging without knowing your device's UDID. By completing these steps, you have successfully found your iPhone backup on your Mac! Remember that understanding how to navigate your system efficiently will enable you to manage and maintain your digital life effectively. [child makes noise during the word "similar."] [sounds as if Chi almost drops something] Title: How to Groom a Dog's Face: A Comprehensive Guide Introduction: Grooming your dog's face is an essential part of their overall hygiene and can help prevent health issues such as eye irritation or dental problems. This guide will provide you with detailed steps and helpful tips to groom your dog's face effectively and safely. Before starting, make sure you have all necessary supplies: * Dog-safe facial cleaning solution or warm water * Soft cloth or sponge * Small brush (such as a toothbrush) * Cotton balls * Nail clippers or grinder * Styptic powder (for stopping bleeding in case of cutting the quick) * Treats for positive reinforcement Step 1: Choose the Right Cleaning Solution Select a gentle, dog-specific cleaner designed for facial use. Alternatively, dilute a small amount of mild dish soap in warm water. Avoid using human shampoo or harsh chemicals, which may cause skin irritation. Step 2: Prepare Your Dog Encourage your dog to sit comfortably by offering treats and praise. Speak calmly throughout the process to keep them relaxed. If they seem anxious, take breaks and resume when they are more comfortable. Step 3: Clean Around Eyes Dip a cotton ball in the cleaning solution and gently wipe around the eyes. Be careful not to poke your dog's eye with the cotton ball. Remove any discharge or tear stains. Use separate cotton balls for each eye to avoid spreading bacteria. Key tip: Trimming excess hair around the eyes can reduce tear staining and improve vision. Consult a professional groomer if unsure about doing this yourself. Step 4: Clean Facial Folds If your dog has wrinkles or folds on their face, carefully clean inside these areas to remove dirt and debris that could lead to infection. Use a damp cloth or sponge and dry thoroughly afterwards. Step 5: Brush Teeth and Gums Use a small, soft-bristled brush or rubber finger glove to gently scrub your dog's teeth and gum line. Regular brushing reduces plaque buildup and promotes oral health. Apply pet-safe toothpaste if desired but ensure it does not contain xylitol, which is toxic to dogs. Step 6: Check and Trim Nails Inspect your dog's nails and trim them if needed. Long nails can cause pain while walking or lead to ingrown nails. Hold the paw firmly yet gently and cut only the clear portion of the nail. If you accidentally cut into the pink area ("quick"), apply styptic powder to stop the bleeding. Step 7: Reward Good Behavior Offer your dog treats and praise during and after grooming sessions. Positive reinforcement encourages cooperation and makes future grooming easier. Guidelines: * Always be patient and gentle. Never force your dog into uncomfortable positions or rush through the process. * Schedule regular grooming appointments with a professional if you feel uncertain about performing certain tasks at home. * Monitor your dog's face daily for signs of irritation or abnormalities. Contact your vet if you notice redness, swelling, or unusual odors. * Establish a routine so your dog becomes accustomed to the grooming process. Start with short sessions and gradually increase duration over time. People from theSwiss branch of the Salvavion army qualified for the Eurovidion Song Contest, 2013 in Malm . Imagine you have a big spherical snowman in your backyard with a radius of 2 units. The snowman is made up of three balls of snow stacked on top of each other, and its body is divided into three regions based on the x, y, and z axes. Your task is to figure out the total weight of the snowman without actually picking it up. The bottom part of the snowman (Region 1) has snow only in the first quadrant, meaning x, y, and z are all positive or zero. To calculate the weight of this region, we need to add up the weights of all the tiny snowflakes in that part of the snowman. We know that the density (weight per unit volume) of the snowflakes in Region 1 depends on their position, given by the formula x*y*z. Now let's learn how to write the math problem using polar coordinates and rectangular coordinates. Polar Coordinates (spherical version): Can we use polar coordinates to solve this problem? Yes! However, instead of using the usual polar coordinates you learn in grade school, we will use "spherical" polar coordinates. Instead of r, , and , we will call themrho, , and . Think of them like distance along each axis, similar to x, y, and z. 1. Set up the equation for the sphere boundary in spherical coordinates: rho = 2 (remember, rho is our new name for r). 2. Limit the values of and to cover only one octant since the snowman occupies the first quadrant in each axis: 0 pi/2 and 0 pi/2. 3. Calculate the Jacobian factor for converting volumes between coordinate systems. Since we changed the names but not the actual system itself, no scaling is needed here. Rectangular Coordinates: Let's also try solving this problem using regular rectangular coordinates. 1. Find the limits for x, y, and z: x goes from 0 to 2, while y and z both range from 0 to sqrt(4 - x^2), which comes from the sphere equation. 2. Write down the integrand function: x\*y\*z \* dx dy dz represents the infinitesimal weight of the small snow cube located at (x, y, z). By understanding these two methods, you now have the tools to visualize and tackle more complex shape and density calculations in higher grades! Just remember to always pay close attention to the boundaries and constraints provided by the problem to ensure accurate results. here's a mailbox on this side. A complete sentence has at least a subject and a main verb to state (declare) a complete thought. Short example: "She walks". A subject is the noun that is doing the main verb. The main verb is the verb that the subject is doing. In English and many other languages, the first word of a written sentence has a capital letter. At the end of the sentence there is a punctuation mark depending on whether it is a statement, a question, a command, a request or an exclamation. Once there was a clumsy rat. He was always making silly mistakes and he loved stealing from people's homes. One day, he decided to steal from an old house near his. As he entered the house, the rat noticed that all the furniture and walls had been broken and destroyed. He walked around, looking for something valuable. Suddenly, the rat saw a beautiful chair. He quickly grabbed it, thinking he had scored a big prize. He tried to leave the house with the chair, but he ended up making a lot of noise and accidentally disturbed a family living nearby. The family opened their door and saw the rat with the chair in his mouth. The family was really angry and decided to restore the house. The rat was so sad to see all his efforts go to waste. He tried to explain himself, but it was too late. The family took away the chair and the rat was never seen again. He had learned his lesson and realized that the old house was not the place for him. You were doing these real good the other night. Dealing with failure is an important life skill that can help you grow, learn, and become more resilient. Here's a step-by-step guide on how to deal with failure effectively: **Step 1: Acknowledge Your Feelings** When faced with failure, it's natural to feel disappointed, sad, or frustrated. Allow yourself to experience these emotions without judgment. Don't try to suppress them or pretend they don't exist. Recognizing your feelings is the first step towards coping with failure. *Key tip:* Remember, experiencing negative emotions after a setback doesn't mean you are weak or unable to handle challenges. It means you are human. **Step 2: Reflect on What Happened** Take some time to think about what caused the failure. Be honest with yourself, but avoid being overly critical. Consider whether there were any factors beyond your control that contributed to the outcome. Look at both your actions and the circumstances surrounding the situation objectively. *Guideline:* Try writing down your thoughts in a journal. This can provide clarity and perspective. **Step 3: Learn From Your Mistakes** Identify what went wrong and consider how you could do things differently next time. Think about skills you might need to develop or improve upon. Treat failures as opportunities for growth rather than insurmountable obstacles. *Key tip:* Focus on learning, not blaming. Instead of asking "Whose fault was this?", ask "What can I learn from this?" **Step 4: Set Realistic Expectations** Understand that everyone makes mistakes and experiences failure at times. Success isn't about never failing; it's about how we respond to those failures. Accept that perfection is unattainable and focus instead on progress. *Guideline:* Use SMART goals (Specific, Measurable, Achievable, Relevant, Time-bound) when setting new objectives. **Step 5: Practice Self-Compassion** Treat yourself with kindness and understanding, just as you would treat a good friend. Avoid harsh self-criticism and remind yourself that everyone fails sometimes. Mental health professionals often recommend using positive affirmations during difficult times. *Key tip:* Phrases like "I am capable," "I will get through this," or "This is temporary" can be helpful. **Step 6: Seek Support if Needed** Don't hesitate to reach out to others if you're having trouble dealing with failure. Talk to trusted friends, family members, mentors, or professional counselors who can offer advice, encouragement, and different perspectives. *Guideline:* When seeking support, be clear about what kind of help you need. Are you looking for someone to listen, give feedback, or brainstorm solutions? **Step 7: Move Forward Positively** Once you've processed your emotions, learned from your mistakes, and sought necessary support, it's time to move forward. Channel your energy into future endeavors, keeping in mind the lessons you've learned. *Key tip:* Stay optimistic. Believe that better outcomes are possible and take proactive steps towards achieving them. Remember, dealing with failure is a process, not an event. Give yourself permission to take the time you need to work through each stage before moving onto the next one. And above all else, be patient and compassionate with yourself along the way. Activities: The Envelope Trick Teach your kid math magic that'll amaze you! - Little Houdini:Your kid. - Volunteer:Your kid's audience. - A "magic envelope":Just a plain old envelope will do the trick. (No pun intended!) - A pen - A piece of paper - With his audience giving him rapt attention, have your kid secretly write down the four-digit number that is double the current year. (For example, if it's 2007, he'd write 4014.) - Have him put the paper in a blank envelope and seal it. - Then get your kid to ask for a volunteer. (Even if it is an audience of one-asking for a volunteer makes the trick official!) - Have him ask the volunteer to write down the following four numbers on a blank piece of paper: - The year she was born. - The year of a significant event in her life (i.e., the year she started middle school, or the year she discovered her first gray hair-it all depends on the audience). - Her age. (In whole numbers, she can't say she's "10-and-a-half" or "30 and holding" or else the trick won't work.) - The number of years that have passed since the important event. - Have the volunteer total the numbers. (Help or check her math to be sure it's correct.) - When the volunteer has her number, have your kid open his envelope. Presto! The number he wrote down will be the same as the number the volunteer came up with! How? Title: How to Perform the Backstabbing Move like Carlito from Call of Duty Introduction: This tutorial will guide you through performing the backstabbing move made famous by Carlito, a character from the popular video game series Call of Duty. This move is also known as an "execution" or "finishing move." It's a stylish way to take down opponents and adds some flair to your multiplayer experience. Please note that this move can only be performed on enemies who are already wounded or knocked down. Step 1: Approach the Opponent After wounding or knocking down an enemy, approach them carefully while they're lying prone on the ground. Be cautious of other nearby players who might spot you during this process. Remember, stealth is crucial when attempting a backstab. Key Tips & Guidelines: - Try to position yourself behind the opponent to ensure maximum surprise factor. - Make sure no other enemies are aware of your location before initiating the backstab. Why this works: By approaching from behind, you catch your opponent off guard, making it easier to execute the maneuver without being interrupted. Step 2: Prepare for the Backstab Once you've positioned yourself correctly, press the crouch button (default 'Ctrl') to prepare for the backstab animation. Your character should bend over slightly, readying themselves for the attack. Key Tips & Guidelines: - Timing is critical here; perform this action too early or late, and you may miss the opportunity to backstab. - Keep watching out for any potential threats around you during this phase. Why this works: The preparation stance signals your intent to execute the backstab, setting up the next steps in sequence. Step 3: Execute the Backstab Now comes the actual execution. While still holding down the crouch button, click and hold the left mouse button (or right trigger if using a controller) to start the stabbing motion. Continue pressing these buttons until the animation finishes. Key Tips & Guidelines: - Maintain focus on the target throughout the entire process to guarantee successful completion. - Release the buttons once the animation concludes to avoid canceling the finishing move prematurely. Why this works: Proper timing and continuous input allow the full animation to play out, dealing significant damage to the targeted enemy. Step 4: Escape Safely Immediately after executing the backstab, release all controls and quickly move away from the area. There's a high chance that other players heard the commotion and could be rushing towards your location. Key Tips & Guidelines: - Always prioritize safety after completing a backstab. - Consider changing direction or finding cover to evade detection. Why this works: Escaping safely ensures survival and allows for more opportunities to pull off similar moves later on. Conclusion: Performing the backstabbing move like Carlito requires practice and patience but offers impressive results upon mastery. Utilize these steps wisely within appropriate gaming scenarios to maximize their impact. Stay vigilant, strategic, and enjoy showcasing your newfound skills! Title: Complete the Quest 'Penny and Lenny' on Panfu.com Panfu is a discontinued virtual world for children where they can play games, chat with friends, and complete quests. The 'Penny and Lenny' quest is one such adventure that your character can embark upon. This tutorial will guide you through every step required to successfully finish this quest. **Step 1:** Log into Your Account Log in using your username and password at <http://www.panfu.com/>. Make sure you have an active account as creating a new one may no longer be possible due to the game's shutdown. **Step 2:** Enter the Game World Once logged in, click on "Enter" or double-click your panda avatar to enter the game world. You will appear in your home room. **Step 3:** Navigate to Penny's House From your home room, look around for a map near the entrance or ask other players for directions to reach Penguin Park (Island). When you arrive, locate Penny's house which has a pink roof and blue walls. **Step 4:** Speak With Penny Click on Penny standing outside her house to start a conversation. She'll tell you about her lost cat, Lenny. Agree to help her find him. **Step 5:** Search Around Penguin Park Explore the area surrounding Penny's house thoroughly. Look behind bushes, trees, rocks, and any objects where a cat might hide. Remember, interacting with items often requires clicking them. **Step 6:** Find Lenny Keep searching until you spot Lenny, who looks like a regular gray cat, hiding under a bush or similar location. Click on Lenny when found. **Step 7:** Return Lenny to Penny After finding Lenny, bring him back to Penny by walking towards her location and clicking on her again once close enough. A dialog box should pop up asking if you want to return Lenny; confirm yes. **Step 8:** Collect Rewards As thanks, Penny rewards you with coins and sometimes furniture items. These rewards are automatically added to your inventory. To view these, open your backpack by clicking on its icon usually located at the bottom left corner of the screen. Congratulations! You've completed the 'Penny and Lenny' quest. Although Panfu isn't operational anymore, following this tutorial gives insight into how online games work, encouraging problem solving, exploration, interaction with non-player characters (NPCs), and item management - all crucial skills for many modern video games. About every 33 years, the Leonid shower produces a big meteor storm, peaking at thousands of meteors per hour. Leonid storms gave birth to the term "meteor shower" when it was first realised, during the November 1833 storm, that the meteors radiated from near the star Gamma Leonis. The last Leonid storms were in 1999, 2001 (two), and 2002 (two). Before that, there were storms in 1767, 1799, 1833, 1866, 1867, and 1966. When the Leonid shower is not "storming" it is less active than the Perseids. I'm going to see how fast I can stop that. Tick-Tock Goes The Clock The most accurate predictors of reading success later in life are letter recognition and the ability different phonemes in spoken words. With these two factors being the most important aspects of skillful reading future it is fitting that they should be focused on when working with readers. The goal of this lesson is for the students to be able to make the /t/ sound, write the letter t, recognize the letter t in print by reading a book, recognize the sound /t/ in speech by making motions to indicate the sound and to grasp the Chart paper with tongue twister written on it: "Tina took Timmy's tiny turtle to town." Picture of a pocket watch or pendulum clock (something that makes a tick-tock sound) Dry erase marker Pictures of the number two, toes, a tent, and a turkey Pictures of a book, the number four, hands, and a door Activity sheet with pictures of a turtle, a table, a television, a dog, a bird, fingers and a by Stan Stoddard 1. Begin by holding up the picture of the watch/clock and ask the students "What is this a picture of?" If the students cannot get it, tell them that it is a picture of a watch/clock. Then ask them if they've ever listened to a clock. Ask them what sound it makes? (tick-tock, tick-tock or /t/-/t/-/t/-/t/). 2. "How do we make that noise?" Have the students watch their partner closely as they take turns saying /t/. Then tell them, "when we make the sound /t/ we start with our tongue at the top of our mouth behind our teeth. We then breathe out and as we do this we move our tongues down so that they end up behind our bottom teeth. Lets make the sound /t/ five times in a row!" 3. Sometimes it helps us to remember something if we make up a motion to go with it. Demonstrate for the students how to tap your wrist as if you were tapping a watch waiting for someone. Now have them do it with you. "Can you tap your imaginary (or real if they have them) watches? Okay good! Now every time we tap lets say /t/." As they are tapping at a steady pace they should be saying "/t/-/t/-/t/ " 4. Let's try a rhyme [on chart]. "Tina took Timmy's tiny turtle to town." Read it the first time and have the children listen. Then ask them to try it with you two times. The second time that they read it with you, have them raise their hands every time they hear /t/. (This should be at the beginning of each word). "Now lets separate out the /t/ and every time we hear it we'll tap our watch T-ina t-ook T-immy's t-urtle t-o t-own. Good job!" 5. [Have students take out primary paper and pencil]. "Did you know that we could use letters to represent sounds that we hear?" Demonstrate for the students on a white board how to make the lower case letter t. "We start at the rooftop and draw a straight line all the way down to the sidewalk. Then we cross at the fence. Can you make a lower case t? Good job! I want everyone to continue to make lower case t's on their paper while I walk around and get a peek at your work. When you make the letter t, I want you to say /t/ so that we remember that t says /t/." As you walk around the room, if you see a student who is struggling, repeat your instructions of how to make the letter and then provide further help if needed. After this, demonstrate how to make an upper case T. "For the upper case T we start out the same way. Draw a line from the rooftop to the sidewalk but this time, instead of crossing at the fence, lets cross on the rooftop. Let me see you try it. Remember, when you make the letter t, I want you to say /t/ so that we remember that t says /t/." Proceed in the same way that you did for lower case. Checklist for teacher to use when walking around and checking students' work: 6. Tell the students that it is now time to hear a story. "Today when we read our story, I want you to listen very carefully for the /t/ that we've been talking about. What letter makes that sound? Right! The letter t! Every time that you hear t say /t/ I want you to tap your watch." Before reading, introduce the story with a brief book talk. "In this story a little girl finds a turtle. She brings him home with her and gives him all sorts of things children would like. But will the turtle like all of these things?" 7. For assessment, hold up the pictures that are listed in the materials list have the students identify the objects. After they have identified these items, ask them to tap their watches if they hear /t/ when they name the pictures. Pass out the activity sheet with pictures on it and ask them to circle the pictures that begin with the /t/. This will allow you to evaluate the students' understanding of the concept taught in this lesson. M. "Silly Sally." Reading Genie: http://www.auburn.edu/rdggenie Stoddard, S. Turtle Time. A waterfall is a place where water rushes down a steep ledge. The water flows from higher land, then it falls down a big step of rock to lower land of softer rock where it will continue on its journey. Usually the lower land is in a gorge. Waterfalls are usually made when a river is young, in places where softer rock is underneath harder rock in the waterfalls [Willie pointed to the different squares on the board] Title: How to Close a Box Without Tape Introduction: Closing a box without tape may seem like an impossible task, but with the right techniques and materials, you can ensure your items are securely packed away. This method is particularly useful when reusing boxes or if you simply ran out of packing tape. Here's our comprehensive guide on closing a box without tape. Materials Needed: 1. The box itself (corrugated cardboard preferred) 2. A ruler or straight edge 3. Scissors or a craft knife 4. Glue or staples 5. Optional: binder clips or paper clips Step 1: Prepare Your Materials Gather all necessary materials before beginning the process. Make sure that your scissors or craft knife is sharp enough to cut through corrugated cardboard efficiently. If using glue, choose one that has strong adhesive properties such as hot glue or superglue. For stapling, make sure you have a heavy-duty stapler capable of puncturing the thickness of the cardboard. Key Tip: Always prioritize safety when handling sharp objects like knives and scissors. Keep them out of reach from children and pets. Step 2: Measure the Flaps Measure the length of the flaps on both sides of the box using a ruler. You want these measurements to be exact so that they align properly when folded over. Mark the points where you will fold along the inside of the box using a pencil. Guideline: Accuracy here ensures a neater finish and prevents bulging which could lead to instability in stacking or transporting the box later. Step 3: Fold the First Set of Flaps Take one set of opposing flaps and fold them towards the center of the box, making sure to match up the edges with the markings made earlier. Press down firmly along the crease to create a crisp line. Repeat this step for the other pair of flaps. Key Tip: Use your fingernails or the backside of your scissors to reinforce the creases for added stability. Step 4: Secure the First Layer Now that both sets of flaps are folded inward, we need to secure them together. Apply a thin line of glue along the inner edge of one set of flaps. Quickly press the corresponding flaps onto the glued surface, holding them steady until they stick well. Alternatively, you can also staple the overlapping sections together at regular intervals (around every inch). Guideline: Be careful not to use too much glue; excess can seep out and potentially stain whatever is being stored in the box. Step 5: Reinforce the Corners To add extra strength, consider reinforcing the corners by applying more glue or adding additional staples diagonally across the intersection point of the two sets of flaps. Allow the glue to dry completely before proceeding to the next step. Step 6: Optionally Add Binder Clips For a quick fix or smaller boxes, you might opt to skip steps four and five entirely and instead use large binder clips or paperclips to hold everything together while moving the box around. Simply clip them onto the outside of the box where the folds meet. Conclusion: While taping remains the standard way to close most boxes, knowing how to seal them without tape provides convenience during emergencies or when trying to reduce waste. With practice, this technique becomes second nature, saving time and resources whenever needed. Remember always to prioritize safety and precision throughout the entire process. Now look at what a good job Mary did. Title: Fix a Disabled iPod Touch Without Losting All Data - A Comprehensive Guide Introduction: This tutorial aims to help you fix your disabled iPod Touch without losing all your important data. We understand that your music, photos, apps, and other files are valuable, and we want to ensure they remain safe throughout the process. Before starting, make sure you have the following items ready: - A computer with the latest version of iTunes installed (for both Windows and Mac) - Your charging cable for connecting the iPod Touch to your computer - Patience and caution during the entire procedure Section 1: Put Your iPod Touch into Recovery Mode Step 1: Connect your iPod Touch to your computer using its USB cable. Make sure your computer recognizes the device. Explanation: This initial connection is essential as it establishes communication between your iPod Touch and your computer. It allows iTunes to detect any issues and provide solutions. Step 2: Perform a force restart on your iPod Touch while connected to your computer. For different models, follow these steps: iPod Touch 7th generation: Press and hold the Top button and the Volume up or down button until the recovery mode screen appears. Other models: Quickly press and release the Home button, then connect your device to your computer while holding the Sleep/Wake button until you see the recovery mode screen. Guideline: If your iPod Touch does not respond after multiple attempts at this step, allow it to rest for about five minutes before trying again. Section 2: Restore Your iPod Touch Using iTunes Step 3: Open iTunes on your computer if it doesn't open automatically when you connect your iPod Touch. Explanation: Launching iTunes will enable you to access settings and tools necessary for fixing your disabled iPod Touch. Step 4: Click on the iPod Touch icon within iTunes once it has been detected. The Summary tab should appear by default. Explanation: Accessing your iPod Touch through iTunes grants permission to modify crucial system components responsible for disabling the device. Step 5: Locate the 'Restore iPhone' option under the Backup section and click on it. Confirm the action when prompted. Key Tip: DO NOT select 'Update' as it may lead to further complications; always opt for 'Restore'. Explanation: Initiating the restore process erases all current software, including problematic elements causing the disablement. Don't worry-your personal data remains untouched due to subsequent actions. Section 3: Exit Recovery Mode After Successful Restoration Step 6: Once the restoration completes successfully, your iPod Touch will reboot itself. Wait until you reach the setup screen. Explanation: At this point, the operating system and core functionalities have returned to their original state, effectively addressing the disablement issue. Step 7: Set up your iPod Touch normally but do not sync yet. Instead, choose 'Restore from iTunes backup.' Explanation: By selecting this option, you retrieve previously saved data onto your now fully operational device. Step 8: Select the most recent backup file created prior to experiencing the disability issue. Explanation: Choosing an older backup risks overwriting newer information stored on your iPod Touch since then. Conclusion: Congratulations! You have successfully fixed your disabled iPod Touch without sacrificing precious data. Remember to keep your devices updated and protected against potential threats to avoid future inconveniences. [using Brian's earlier term to correct him] Can I have mine when I um get um thirteen? What is a Solution? A Solution is a value you can put in place of a variable (such as x) that would make the equation true. |x - 2 = 4| |If we put 6 in place of x we get:||6 - 2 = 4||, which is true| |So x = 6 is a solution| Note: try another value for x. Say x=5: you get 5-2=4 which is not true, so x=5 is not a solution. More Than One Solution You can have more than one solution. Example: (x-3)(x-2) = 0 When x is 3 we get: (3-3)(3-2) = 0 1 = 0 which is true And when x is 2 we get: (2-3)(2-2) = (-1) 0 = 0 which is true So the solutions are: x = 3, or x = 2 When you gather all solutions together it is called a Solution Set Some equations are true for all allowed values and are then called Identities Example: this is one of the trigonometric identities: tan( ) = sin( )/cos( ) How to Solve an Equation There is no "one perfect way" to solve all equations. A Useful Goal But you will often get success if your goal is to end up with: x = something In other words, you want to move everything except "x" (or whatever name your variable has) over to the right hand side. Example: Solve 3x-6 = 9 |Start With||3x-6 = 9| |Add 6 to both sides:||3x = 9+6| |Divide by 3:||x = (9+6)/3| Now we have x = something, and a short calculation reveals that x = 5 Like a Puzzle In fact, solving an equation is just like solving a puzzle. And like puzzles, there are things you can (and cannot) do. Here are some things you can do: - Clear out any fractions by Multiplying every term by the bottom parts. - Add or Subtract the same value from both sides. - Divide every term by the same nonzero value. - Combine Like Terms - Expanding (the opposite of factoring) may also help - Sometimes you can apply a function to both sides (e.g. square both sides). - Recognizing a pattern you have seen before, like the difference of squares And the more "tricks" and techniques you learn the better you will get. Check Your Solutions You should always check that your "solution" really is a solution. Example: solve for x: |2x||+ 3 =||6||(x 3)| |x - 3||x - 3| We have said x 3 to avoid a division by zero. Let's multiply through by (x - 3): 2x + 3(x-3) = 6 Bring the 6 to the left: 2x + 3(x-3) - 6 = 0 Expand and solve: 2x + 3x - 9 - 6 = 0 5x - 15 = 0 5(x - 3) = 0 x - 3 = 0 That can be solved by having x=3, so let us check: |2 3||+ 3 =||6||Hang On! That would mean Dividing by Zero! |3 - 3||3 - 3| And anyway, we said at the top that x 3, so ... x = 3 does not actually work, and so: There is No Solution! That was interesting ... we thought we had found a solution, but when we looked back at the question we found it wasn't allowed! This gives us a moral lesson: "Solving" only gives you possible solutions, they need to be checked! How To Check Take your solution(s) and put them in the original equation to see if they really work. - Note down where an expression is not defined (due to a division by zero, the square root of a negative number, or some other reason) - Show all the steps, so it can be checked later (by you or someone else). Title: Convert Pictures to Any iPod Without Software Introduction: This tutorial will guide you through the process of converting pictures to any iPod without using additional software. This method utilizes Apple's built-in tools and services to ensure compatibility with your device. By following these steps, you can easily transfer photos from your computer to your iPod touch, iPod nano, or iPod shuffle. Key Tips and Guidelines: * Make sure your iPod is updated to the latest iOS version. * Organize your photos in one folder before starting the conversion process. * Use high-resolution images (minimum 1536 x 2048 pixels) to maintain image quality on retina displays. Step 1: Prepare Your Photos Gather all the pictures you want to convert and move them into one folder. Having all files organized simplifies the uploading process and reduces the chances of missing crucial images. Step 2: Connect Your iPod to Your Computer Connect your iPod to your computer via USB cable. Once connected, unlock your iPod if necessary and trust the computer if prompted. This allows your computer to recognize your iPod as an external storage device. Step 3: Open Image Files on Windows For Windows users, right-click on the photo folder created earlier, select 'Send to', then choose 'Compressed (zipped) folder'. A new zipped file should appear. Right-click this zipped file and click 'Properties'. Select the 'Advanced...' button at the bottom of the window that appears, check the box next to 'Encrypt contents to secure data', and confirm by clicking OK twice. Step 4: Access Your iPod Through File Explorer On Windows, open My Computer or File Explorer and locate your iPod under 'Devices with Removable Storage'. Double-click on its icon to access the internal folders. For Mac users, open Finder, go to Devices > iPod Name > Internal Storage. Step 5: Create a New Album (Optional) To organize imported photos better, create a new album within your iPod. Go back to your home screen and tap 'Photos', followed by 'Albums', then hit the plus (+) sign at the top left corner to add a new album. Enter a name and save it. Step 6: Import Photos onto iPod Copy the zipped folder containing the photos and paste it inside the DCIM folder located in your iPod's internal memory. If creating a new album, navigate to the corresponding album directory instead. You may need to enable hidden items (press Alt+0174 on numeric keypad) to view the DCIM folder on some computers. Step 7: Eject Safely & Verify Transfer Once completed, eject your iPod safely from your computer and disconnect the USB cable. Navigate to the Photos app on your iPod, and verify whether the transferred images are visible under Camera Roll or in the newly created album. Troubleshooting Common Issues: * If the photos aren't showing up, try restarting both your iPod and computer, reconnecting the devices, and repeating Step 6. * Low-resolution images might result in poor display quality; always opt for higher resolution when possible. * Encryption is optional but recommended for securing personal content during transfer. Lily and Ben are twins. They like to play with their toys in their room. But sometimes they fight over the same toy. Today they both want to play with the red car. "Give me the car!" Lily says. She pulls the car from Ben's hand. "No, it's mine!" Ben says. He pulls the car back from Lily's hand. They tug and tug until the car breaks in two. They both cry. Mom hears the noise and comes to their room. She sees the broken car and the sad twins. "What happened here?" Mom asks. "Lily broke the car!" Ben says. "No, Ben broke the car!" Lily says. They both insist that it is not their fault. Mom sighs. She picks up the pieces of the car and puts them in the trash. "Listen, my loves," Mom says. "Fighting over toys is not nice. It only makes you unhappy and angry. You have many other toys to play with. Why don't you share and take turns? That way you can have more fun and peace." Lily and Ben look at each other. They feel sorry for breaking the car and making Mom sad. "Sorry, Mom," Lily says. "Sorry, Mom," Ben says. They hug Mom and say sorry to each other. Mom smiles. She gives them each a kiss and a hug. "Good," Mom says. "Now, why don't you play with the blue truck and the yellow bike? You can switch after ten minutes." Lily and Ben nod. They take the truck and the bike and play nicely together. Mom leaves their room and closes the door. She hears them laughing and talking. She is happy that they have learned to share and make peace. We examine cylinders and prisms, defining them, and measuring their surface areas and volumes. The Lesson: We may think of a cylinder as a tin can, with two bases that are circles. The surface wrapping around the circles can be "unwrapped" and shown as a rectangle. A diagram below shows a cylinder on the left. The rectangle at right is the side surface, unwrapped from the cylinder. We know that its length is the circumference of the circular base which is . Let's Practice: The surface area is the sum of the areas of the two circles that are the bases and the area of the rectangle. Since the area of a circle is and the area of a rectangle is lw, we can make a formula for the surface area of a cylinder: . The volume of a cylinder is found using the concept of base x height. The area of the base is . The volume of the cylinder is . A prism is a (3-dimensional) polyhedron with bases that are parallel, congruent polygons. The sides are parallelograms. We show three examples below. The important features are the areas of the bases and the height, which is the perpendicular distance between the bases. At the left is a rectangular solid. The middle diagram is of a prism with bases that are hexagons. Notice that the slant height, s, is different than the height, h, since the prism is tilted. The prism at right has bases that are pentagons and is not tilted. We shall concentrate on prisms such as this which are called right prisms. Volume and surface area can be found as follows: The area of the bases can be found if we know the dimensions and properties of the polygons which form the bases. The area of all the sides (called the lateral area) can be found if we can measure the base and height (called the slant height and labeled "s") of each of the parallelograms which form the sides of the prism. Adding the areas of the parallelograms which form the sides can be summarized by multiplying the slant height and the perimeter of a base. We have: If the bases are perpendicular to the parallelograms forming the sides (as in a right prism), the slant height s = h. We label the three prisms below A, B, and C. A is a rectangular solid. B is a non-right prism. C is a right prism. - Suppose prism A has dimensions 5 x 4 x 7 feet. What are the surface area and volume? We solve this using standard formulas for a rectangular solid. The volume is 5 x 4 x 7 = 140 cubic feet. Two sides are 5 x 4, two are 4 x 7 and two are 5 x 7. The areas of these sides are 20, 28, and 35. Thus the total surface area is twice the sum of these or 166 square feet. - Suppose prism B has bases which are regular hexagons of side 2 meters, a height of 3 meters, and a slant height of 3.4 meters. What are the surface area and the volume? The lateral area equals s x P = 3.4 x 12 = 40.8 m2. The area of a regular hexagon (base) is m2. This gives a total surface area of two bases plus the lateral area which equals m2. We now know the volume = area of base x height equals - Suppose right prism C has bases which are regular pentagons of radius 25 centimeters. The height is 40 centimeters. What are the surface area and the volume? For the area of a base we use . This gives us area of base as cm2. We calculate volume = area of base x height as 1486 x 40 = 59440 cm3 = 0.05944 m3. The lateral area is s x P where P is the perimeter of the base of the regular pentagon with radius 25 and s = h is the slant height. A side can be found from . This gives us the length of a side of the base as cm. The lateral area equals s x P = 40 x 5(29.39) = 5878 cm2. The total surface area is two bases plus the lateral area or 8850 cm2 = 0.885 m2. - A tin can has a radius of 2 inches and a height of 3 inches. What are the surface area and volumes? Collecting Barbie dolls can be a fun and rewarding hobby, but it can also be expensive if you're not careful. Here are some steps and tips to help you collect Barbies on a budget: 1. **Set a budget:** Before you start collecting, determine how much money you are willing and able to spend on your collection. This will help you avoid overspending and make sure that you are being mindful of your finances. Keep in mind that the cost of Barbies can add up quickly, especially when you factor in shipping costs and taxes. 2. **Research prices:** Familiarize yourself with the average prices of the Barbies you want to collect. You can do this by checking online marketplaces such as eBay, Amazon, or specialty toy stores. By knowing the typical price range for a particular doll, you can avoid overpaying and ensure that you are getting a good deal. 3. **Look for deals:** Keep an eye out for sales and discounts at local toy stores, department stores, and online retailers. Sign up for newsletters and follow social media accounts for these businesses to stay informed about any upcoming promotions or clearance events. 4. **Buy used:** Consider purchasing gently used Barbies from online marketplaces, garage sales, or thrift stores. These dolls may be significantly cheaper than new ones, and many times they are still in great condition. Just be sure to thoroughly inspect the doll before making a purchase to ensure that it is complete and in good shape. 5. **Join a collector's club:** There are many Barbie collector clubs and communities both online and offline where enthusiasts share information, resources, and advice. Joining one of these groups can give you access to insider knowledge and potentially lead to opportunities for buying, trading, or selling dolls at a lower cost. 6. **Attend conventions and shows:** Many cities host Barbie-themed conventions and toy shows throughout the year. These events offer a unique opportunity to meet other collectors, learn more about the hobby, and find rare or hard-to-find dolls at discounted prices. Be prepared to negotiate and don't be afraid to walk away if the asking price is too high. 7. **Prioritize your collection:** Determine which Barbies are most important to you and focus your efforts on acquiring those first. This will help you allocate your budget more effectively and prevent you from spreading yourself too thin trying to buy every doll you come across. 8. **Take care of your collection:** Properly storing and maintaining your Barbies will help preserve their value and extend their lifespan. Store them in a cool, dry place away from direct sunlight and dust. Use protective cases or boxes if possible. Regularly check your dolls for signs of wear and tear and address any issues promptly. 9. **Be patient:** Building a valuable and diverse Barbie collection takes time. Don't rush into purchases or feel pressured to buy every doll you see. Instead, take your time and wait for the right opportunity to come along. Remember that patience often pays off in the world of collecting. 10. **Diversify your collection:** Rather than focusing solely on expensive, limited edition Barbies, consider adding variety to your collection by including different types of dolls, accessories, and memorabilia. This can help spread out your spending and keep your collection interesting. By following these steps and tips, you can build a meaningful and enjoyable Barbie collection without breaking the bank. Happy collecting! Title: How to Choose the Right Toilet Paper Toilet paper is an essential item that many people take for granted. However, choosing the right toilet paper can make a significant difference in terms of comfort, cleanliness, cost-effectiveness, and environmental impact. This comprehensive guide will walk you through the process of selecting the perfect toilet paper for your needs. **Step 1: Determine Your Budget** Before diving into specific features and types of toilet paper, establish a budget range. High-quality toilet papers often come with higher price tags, while more affordable options may compromise on softness or durability. By setting a budget, you'll be able to narrow down your choices and find a balance between quality and affordability. *Key Tip:* Keep in mind that although cheaper options might seem attractive initially, they could lead to increased usage due to poor performance, ultimately resulting in greater overall costs. **Step 2: Understand Ply and Sheet Count** Ply refers to the number of layers used to create one sheet of toilet paper. The most common ply counts are one-, two-, and three-ply. Higher ply means thicker sheets, which typically provide better absorbency and durability. Sheet count indicates how many individual sheets are contained within a roll. More sheets per roll mean fewer rolls needed over time, but this doesn't necessarily equate to superior cleaning power or softness. *Guideline:* Aim for at least two-ply toilet paper as it offers adequate strength and softness without being excessively bulky or wasteful. **Step 3: Assess Softness vs Durability** Softness and durability usually have an inverse relationship; increasing one tends to decrease the other. Softer toilet papers may tear easily during use, requiring additional sheets to complete the task. Conversely, durable toilet papers may feel rough against sensitive skin. *Key Tip:* Strike a balance by testing various samples before making a final decision. Some brands offer "ultra" versions that combine both qualities effectively. **Step 4: Consider Eco-Friendly Options** If reducing your carbon footprint is important to you, explore eco-friendly toilet paper alternatives. Look for products made from recycled materials, bamboo, or sugarcane - all sustainable resources. Additionally, prioritize manufacturers who employ environmentally friendly production methods and packaging. *Guideline:* Seek out certifications such as Forest Stewardship Council (FSC) or Green Seal to ensure responsible sourcing and manufacturing practices. **Step 5: Evaluate Septic Safety** For those using septic systems, choose toilet papers specifically labeled as safe for sewage and septic tanks. These products break down more efficiently than regular toilet paper, preventing clogs and potential backups. *Key Tip:* If unsure about a product's compatibility with your septic system, consult the manufacturer or seek advice from a plumbing professional. **Step 6: Read Customer Reviews** Online customer reviews can provide valuable insights into a toilet paper brand's real-world performance, including factors like softness, strength, value, and eco-friendliness. Pay particular attention to comments mentioning issues related to your personal preferences or requirements. *Guideline:* Be cautious when reading negative reviews since some customers may have unrealistic expectations or misuse the product. Focus on consistent feedback patterns instead. By following these steps and considering the associated tips and guidelines, you're well-equipped to choose the right toilet paper for your needs. Remember, investing time in research now can lead to improved comfort, reduced expenses, and minimized environmental impact in the long run. Hello young mathematicians! Today, we are going to learn about something called "power series." A power series is a way to write a function using an infinite sum of powers of x multiplied by constants. It looks like this: f(x) = a\_0 + a\_1x + a\_2x^2 + a\_3x^3 + ... In this expression, each a\_n represents a constant number, and n starts at zero and goes up by one forever (this is called "infinity"). The variable x is raised to the power of n, which means it gets bigger and bigger as n grows larger. Let me give you an example of why power series can be useful. Imagine you have a math problem where you need to find the value of a function at a specific point, but the function is really complicated or impossible to calculate directly. With a power series representation of that function, however, you just need to substitute the value of x into the series and add up all the resulting numbers! Today, let's try finding the power series solution for the following differential equation: (1 + x^2) y'' + 2xy' - 2y = 0 Don't worry too much about what the symbols mean; just follow along step-by-step. First, assume our solution has the form: y = a\_0 + a\_1x + a\_2x^2 + a\_3x^3 + ... Using some algebraic manipulations, we then rewrite the given differential equation in terms of power series and set every coefficient equal to zero (since any finite sum added to an infinite sum equals another infinite sum). This results in a recursive formula relating consecutive coefficients, allowing us to compute them iteratively based on previously calculated ones. You may notice that the first two equations don't involve any coefficients yet; those represent initial conditions imposed on the solution. Here's the cool part - even though this particular differential equation doesn't have a simple closed-form solution, its power series solution provides valuable insights through its coefficients! These numerical values contain important information hidden within their patterns and relationships. Remember, learning mathematics isn't always about memorizing complex formulas, but rather understanding concepts and developing skills to tackle problems systematically. Keep practicing, stay curious, and enjoy your mathematical journey! FABLE LXXXI. MERCURY AND THE WOODMAN. A MAN was felling a tree on the bank of a river, and by chance let his hatchet slip out of his hand, which dropped into the water, and immediately sunk to the bottom. Being, therefore, in great distress from the loss of his tool, he sat down and bemoaned himself most lamentably. Upon this, Mercury appeared to him, and being informed of the cause of his complaint, dived to the bottom of the river, and, coming up again, showed the man a golden hatchet, demanding if that were his. Title: How to Change Your User Account Picture in Windows 7 Windows 7 allows you to personalize your user account by changing the account picture. This picture is displayed at various places such as the logon screen, start menu, and action center. By following these steps, you can change your user account picture with ease. **Step 1:** Access the Control Panel ---------------------------------- Click the **Start** button located at the bottom-left corner of your screen or press the **Windows key** on your keyboard. The Start Menu will appear; look for the **Control Panel** option, usually found under the 'Settings' category, and click on it. The Control Panel provides access to several settings that allow customization and management of your system. It organizes related options together so they are easy to find when needed. **Step 2:** Choose 'User Accounts and Family Safety' --------------------------------------------------- In the Control Panel window, ensure that you have selected either 'Category view' (default) or 'Small icons view'. Look for an option called 'User Accounts and Family Safety'; if using Category View, this option should be under the 'User Accounts and Security' section. Click on this option. This feature within the Control Panel enables managing individual accounts, setting up parental controls, and configuring family safety options on your computer. **Step 3:** Manage User Accounts ------------------------------ Within the 'User Accounts and Family Safety' window, locate and select the 'User Accounts' option. This selection takes you to another page where you can manage different aspects of all existing user accounts on your PC. ### Key Tips & Guidelines * If multiple users exist on your computer, make sure you are logged into the correct account before proceeding. Changing the account picture from one account does not affect other user accounts. **Step 4:** Select 'Change your picture' -------------------------------------- On the 'User Accounts' page, click on the link named 'Change your picture', which is typically positioned below your current account image. Selecting this option opens a new window containing default pictures provided by Microsoft along with any additional images previously added to the system. ### Key Tips & Guidelines * You may add more images to choose from by clicking on the 'Browse for more pictures' option available near the bottom left corner of the window. This action lets you navigate through folders on your computer to pick an appropriate image file (JPG, GIF, PNG formats are supported). Once chosen, confirm its addition by clicking 'Open' followed by 'Choose Image'. **Step 5:** Pick a New Account Picture ------------------------------------ From the list of available images presented, scroll until you find the one you want to set as your new account picture. After selecting it, simply close the windows by clicking the red X button or pressing Alt+F4. Upon closing, you will notice the newly applied profile picture associated with your user account. ### Key Tips & Guidelines * Make sure the chosen image size isn't too small since low resolution could result in poor visual quality during display. Ideally, aim for an image around 128x128 pixels or larger. * To maintain consistency across different displays like desktop and laptop screens, consider choosing square-shaped images without text overlay. Emergent Literacy Design: Angry Ants and Their Adventure Across Arizona Rationale: When children are first learning to read, they must first be able to identify both the phoneme and grapheme for all 26 letters of the alphabet. This lesson is designed to help students recognize the phoneme /a/ and its grapheme A or a. To recognize the /a/ sound, students will use their hands to make an eye rubbing motion (like a baby crying) whenever they hear /a/ read in the book A Book. To recognize A and a, students will practice writing each letter on primary paper with the help of a memorable saying. A Book, Stan and Jan Berenstain (Berenstain Enterprises, Inc. 1997) White drawing paper Chart with "Alice and Andrew were mad at the angry ants." Letter Aa worksheet (URL below) What Begins with A? worksheet (URL below) Word cards: A, a, BAT, AT, STUCK, HAD, LUCK 1. "Before we can learn to read, we all have to learn sounds and the symbols that represent those sounds. In our language, we have to learn the alphabet. Watch me talk to you. Do you see my mouth move?" (wait for answer) "My mouth does move! It has to move to make different sounds for different letters. I am going to teach you the sound /a/ today. It has two written symbols." (A and a: show both symbols to the student) 2. "To say /a/, we can cry like a baby, /a/a/a/." (hold hands under eyes and twist them back and forth) "What does my mouth look like? I have my mouth open like a circle, and my tongue is behind my bottom teeth. Try it with me, /a/a/a/a/." 3. "Now, I am going to say a word. I will show you how to tell if it says /a/. Watch my mouth to see if it makes a circle. Bat. B-at. Bb-a-t. Bb-aa-t. My mouth made a circle. I heard /a/ in the middle of bat. The baby was crying, wasn't it?" 4. "I have a sentence written for us to read. It is a little tricky because most of the words start with the same letter. Sentences like these are called "Tongue Twisters" (on chart). Alice and Andrew were mad at the angry ants. You say it. Let's say it like this now, Aaalice aaand Aaandrew were maaad aaat the aaangry aaants. Your turn. We are going to say it one more time but like this, /A/lice /a/nd /A/ndrew were m/a/d /a/t the /a/ngry /a/nts." 5. (For this step, the student will need primary paper and a pencil.) "Here is some special paper for learning to write letters. Remember I showed you the two symbols for /a/. One of the symbols (show the capital A to the student) is called capital A and the other (show the lowercase a to the student) is lowercase. Let's write both letters. To make a capital A, start at the bottom and draw a slanted line to the right all the way to the rooftop. Start from that point and draw a slanted line to the right all the way to the bottom. Last draw a line in the middle of the two lines you just made. To write a lowercase a, make a circle between the middle line and the bottom line. Then draw a straight line behind/on the right side of the circle from the middle line to the bottom line (it has to touch the circle). Now make nine more of each." 6. "I am going to say two words at a time. I want you to listen to see which word you hear /a/ in. Remember to watch for my mouth to see if it makes a circle, and listen for the baby crying. Cap or set? Bite or mat? Bowl or pan? Ram or hit? Now I am going to say some random words. See if you can hear /a/. If you hear /a/, do your hand motion. Band, of, tap, bat, tip, sap, kid, cup, class. 7. "I have a book to read. It is called A Book. This book has a lot of words that say /a/ like the baby crying. While I am reading, I want you to do your hand motion just once when you hear /a/. I will read slowly so you can hear the baby crying, and remember to watch my mouth for the circle." Read A Book. "Now let's think of something else the angry ants might have advanced across. Write the name of what you chose and draw a picture. Make a spelling if you are not sure how to spell the name of what you chose for the ants to advance across." Display students' work. 8. Show BAT, and model how to tell if it is bat or bit: The a tells me to cry, /a/a/a/. This word is b-aaa-t, bat. Now, you try some: AT: at or it? STUCK: stack or stuck? HAD: had or hid? LUCK: lack or luck? 9. Distribute both worksheets. Students should color pictures and trace picture names. Also there is practice for writing A and a. On the second worksheet, students identify the pictures and draw a line to the a. Have students read phonetic cue words from Step #8. Byrne, B., & Fielding-Barnsley, R. (1990). Acquiring the alphabetic principle: A case for teaching recognition of phoneme identity. Journal of Educational Psychology, 82, 805-812. Bruce Murray. Emergent Literacy Lesson. We'll get that off later so it won't break. # Discovering Patterns: A Fun Look at Simple Mathematical Ideas Hello young mathematicians! Today we are going to explore some basic mathematical concepts using something we see every day - raindrops! Yes, you heard it right. Raindrops can help us understand and appreciate some fundamental ideas in mathematics. ## Counting Raindrops Have you ever tried counting raindrops while it's raining? It could be challenging, especially when there are so many falling at once! However, this activity gives us a chance to practice our counting skills. We can start by counting each raindrop that falls onto our hand or a specific spot, like the ground or a leaf. As we continue observing and counting, we notice patterns emerging - more drops fall during heavy showers than light drizzle, creating opportunities to learn about multiplication and division. ## Shapes within Raindrops As raindrops fall, they follow a tear-shaped pattern called a spheroid. Even though these droplets appear circular most of the time, their shape changes slightly depending on speed and air resistance. By examining photographs or videos of raindrops, we can study circles and other shapes, introducing ourselves to geometry without even realizing it! ### Activity: Draw your version of a raindrop! What shapes do you observe? How many sides does it have? Is it symmetrical? Remember, there's no need to worry about being too precise; just enjoy exploring different shapes and angles! ## Addition and Subtraction in Nature Mathematics isn't limited to numbers alone but also extends to natural phenomena around us. Have you noticed how raindrops merge together when they collide? When two smaller raindrops unite, they create a bigger one. In doing so, they illustrate addition-a crucial concept in arithmetic. Similarly, sometimes small raindrops evaporate before reaching the ground due to sunlight or wind, demonstrating subtraction through nature. ## Multiplication and Division in Groups During heavy downpours, multiple raindrops often hit surfaces simultaneously, forming clusters. These groups demonstrate multiplication since several individual entities combine to form larger collections. Conversely, when water droplets split apart after hitting a surface, this shows division, where one entity splits into smaller ones. So next time you find yourself caught in the rain, take a moment to observe those fascinating raindrops closely. They offer endless possibilities to discover and playfully engage with foundational mathematical principles. Happy learning! Mama look the green one's by his mama. There are different ways of getting a new IP address. One of them is called Bootstrap Protocol (usually shortened to BOOTP). The device that needs a new address, does not know what network it is in, so it uses an IP address of all zeroes (0.0.0.0) which it sends as a broadcast to the current network, on a special port. In addition, it sends the MAC address of the network card, plus a 4 byte random number. The BOOTP server will send a reply, also as broadcast, addressed to a different port. The reply will contain the mac address of the client, the random number, and the IP address of the client. When the client receives the data, it will set the address specified. If the BOOTP server is configured that way, it will also send the IP address and hostname of the BOOTP Server, the name and path to a file which should be loaded to boot the client (using TFTP) or the name of a directory, which the client should mount using NFS. oh, you're gonna clap, you think that was funny, I don't think so. If your child hasn't begun preschool, you can help your child mentally prepare for math. If your child is currently in preschool, you can supplement your child's understanding in math and further solidify their knowledge and confidence on the subject. It also doesn't have to be difficult to involve your child with math. Since math relates to daily life, part of the process is calling out attention to different areas in life that involve math, from cooking to shopping. A great way to learn numbers while having fun is by playing games. Make learning a game and your child will pick things up with ease. When playing board games, roll dice, call out numbers and count how many spaces it takes to get from one point to the next. You and your child can also play easy card games, like "Go Fish." In learning about money, you can take your child to an ATM and count money in front of them. You can also let them play with a toy cash register and fake money. Take your child shopping and compare prices with them. Invite your child to spend time in the kitchen with you. When cooking, involve your child in measuring ingredients. You can also record their growth by weighing and measuring your child. Let your child play with blocks. This is a great way to exercise their spatial abilities. Blocks are one of the best investments to make for your child's expanding mathematical mind. Bring your child's attention to patterns on fabrics to floors. Look at plaid, polka dots, triangles and more. Tap into your child's analytical mind at an early age. Let your child sort out objects to compare and contrast them. What happens if I go that far away a long ways if I keep on going? Cross-platform gaming, or "crossplay," allows players on different video game platforms to play together in the same online games. This can include playing with people on PC, Xbox, PlayStation, Nintendo Switch, and mobile devices. Here's a detailed guide on how to enable crossplay for your favorite games: **Step 1: Check if the Game Supports Crossplay** Not all games support crossplay due to technical limitations and company policies. Before attempting to set up crossplay, check the game's official website or its community forum to see if crossplay is available. Some popular crossplay-enabled titles include Fortnite, Rocket League, Minecraft, Call of Duty: Modern Warfare, and Dauntless. **Step 2: Update Your Game and Gaming Platform** Ensure that both your game and gaming platform (PC, console, etc.) are updated to their latest versions. Developers often release patches and updates to improve crossplay functionality and compatibility between different systems. **Step 3: Create a Unified Account (If Necessary)** Some games require you to have a unified account across platforms. For example, Epic Games accounts allow users to access Fortnite on multiple platforms using one login. If this applies to your game, create an account and link it to your various gaming profiles. **Step 4: Enable Crossplay Settings Within the Game** To enable crossplay within the game itself, follow these steps: 1. Launch the game and navigate to the settings menu. The location may vary depending on the title, but look for options such as 'Game Settings,' 'Online,' or 'Network.' 2. Look for a setting labeled 'Crossplay,' 'Playstation Crossplay,' 'Xbox Live,' or something similar. It might be located under advanced network settings. 3. Toggle the option to 'Enabled' or 'On.' You should receive a confirmation message stating that crossplay has been activated. Keep in mind, some developers restrict crossplay between certain consoles due to business arrangements. Always consult the developer's documentation or community resources for specific information about which platforms can play together. **Step 5: Join Friends Across Platforms** Once crossplay is enabled, joining friends who use other platforms is relatively straightforward: * **Inviting:** Access the friend list or social menu within the game. Send an invite to the desired player regardless of their device type. They will then receive a notification to join your session. Note: Console gamertags usually contain the console brand abbreviation (e.g., PSN ID). * **Joining:** Accept invitations from friends by navigating to the social menu and looking for pending invites. Alternatively, search for your friend's username via the game's built-in search function. Once found, select 'join party' or 'join game.' **Key Tips & Guidelines:** * Be aware of input differences when playing against players on different platforms. Players on keyboard-and-mouse configurations typically hold an advantage over those using controllers. Adjust your strategy accordingly. * Remember that voice chat capabilities also depend on crossplay implementation. Make sure to test whether voice communication works across platforms before diving into competitive matches. * Notify friends ahead of time regarding any performance issues related to crossplay. Lower frame rates, higher latency, and occasional glitches can occur when mixing hardware types. By following this comprehensive guide, you'll now be able to enjoy seamless multiplayer experiences with friends and strangers alike, no matter what gaming platform they prefer! Another common type of vaccine is an "inactivated vaccine." These vaccines contain dead viruses or bacteria. These do not cause the immune system to react as strongly as live vaccines. Because of this, people may need "booster shots" - extra doses of the vaccine, given at certain times, so their immune system can "learn" how to fight off the infection. Examples of inactivated vaccines include vaccines for pertussis (whooping cough), rabies, and hepatitis B. And what did you do with the rocks? Again that sort of like the banging. Paper weaving is a simple technique that can create a magnificent result. Our introduction to paper weaving provides a step-by-step guide and defines some important weaving terms. - Paper cutter or craft knife and self-healing cutting mat - Glue or tape A few weaving terms to become familiar with: Loom: The frame used for weaving the piece. Warp: The pieces that run from top to bottom (up and down). Weft: The pieces than run across the warp (side to side think weft and right smile) Web: The single or whole piece created by weaving. Step by Step - Select two different but compatible papers. Both should be either 8 1/2" x 11" or 12" x 12" while you are learning the technique. - The first sheet of paper will act as the loom as well as the warp. On the back of the paper, measure and mark 1" from the top and both sides. Do not cut beyond these marks or your loom will fall apart. The warp pieces can be cut straight or curved with them being all the same width or different widths. - Cut the weft strips from the second sheet of paper (straight or curved). To make it easier, you can draw and number the curved pieces before cutting them. - Begin weaving the first row by placing one strip of weft paper over one warps and under the next warp and repeat to the end of the row. The second row starts with placing a weft strip under one warp and over the next warp and repeat to the end of the row. Keep the weft strips pushed toward the top of the weaving. - Alternate row one and row two until the web is complete. Secure weave ends with a touch of glue. Now you are ready to use your woven paper as an element of scrapbooking, card making, paper art, collage and other fun creations.
