In 1913, Niels Bohr came up with the idea that electrons could only take up certain orbits around the nucleus of an atom. Under Bohr's theory, the numbers called m and n in the equation above could represent orbits. Bohr's theory said electrons could begin in some orbit m and end up in some orbit n, or an electron could begin in some orbit n and end up in some orbit m so if a photon hits an electron, its energy will be absorbed, and the electron will move to a higher orbit because of that extra energy. Under Bohr's theory, if an electron falls from a higher orbit to a lower orbit, then it will have to give up energy in the form of a photon. The energy of the photon will equal the energy difference between the two orbits, and the energy of a photon makes it have a certain frequency and color. Bohr's theory provided a good explanation of many aspects of subatomic phenomena, but failed to answer why each of the colors of light produced by glowing hydrogen (and by glowing neon or any other element) has a brightness of its own, and the brightness differences are always the same for each element. Hard 'o get a spoon down 'n that jar, huh? [long silence while S gets his animal-noise toy, starts pulling the string at 1.] Title: Exploring Balanced Friendships with Graphs Hey everyone! Today, we're going to learn about graphs and balanced friendships using a fun game. Imagine you have two groups of people - Group A and Group B, both having the same number of individuals. Each person in Group A will make friends with some people in Group B, and together, they will form a friendship network or "graph." We call this type of graph "bipartite," meaning it consists of two separate sets (Group A and Group B). Our goal today is to understand if there's a way to pick a group of friends from Group A who share approximately the same number of mutual friends in Group B without picking too few or too many. This concept can actually be found in complex problems like analyzing networks and solving puzzles! But don't worry - we'll break it down into easy steps using our new friendships graph. Step 1: Creating your graph Let's say Group A and Group B both contain 5 people each. Write their names on sticky notes and place them on opposite sides of a table. Now connect every individual in Group A with lines (edges) to those they would befriend in Group B. Make sure no one in Group A becomes friends with someone else within their own group! Step 2: Identifying degrees and neighbors Take note of the number of edges connected to each person in Group A. These numbers represent their "degree." In our example, nobody should have just 1 friend since that makes things too easy; instead, ensure everybody in Group A has at least 2 connections. Check out which members of Group B belong to the neighborhood of each member of Group A. Step 3: Seeking balance Now here comes the tricky part-can you select a subgroup from Group A with more than one but fewer than all its members while ensuring that these selected ones share nearly equal numbers of common friends in Group B? You cannot change the existing friendships, so think carefully before making your choice! Remember, even though finding a solution may feel challenging, understanding balanced relationships helps us analyze real-life scenarios better, like comparing trade between countries or looking at ecosystem interactions. It could even inspire ideas for creating fairer rules when sharing items among classmates! So go ahead, explore different possibilities, and enjoy discovering the hidden patterns in your unique friendships graph! Okay do you want to call someone else to come for dinner? Title: How to Call a Control's Methods in a Multi-Threaded .NET Application Introduction: In this tutorial, we will guide you through the process of calling a control's methods in a multi-threaded .NET application. This is an essential technique when working with graphical user interfaces (GUIs) where multiple threads can interact simultaneously with different controls. We will discuss best practices, common pitfalls, and provide code samples using C# and Windows Forms as our primary tools. Background: The .NET Framework provides support for multithreading via System.Threading namespace. Multithreading enables applications to perform several tasks concurrently by utilizing system resources efficiently. However, manipulating GUI components from secondary threads may result in unexpected behavior or exceptions since UI elements are designed to be accessed only by the thread that created them - typically the main thread, also known as the UI thread. To overcome this limitation, .NET offers mechanisms like delegates and Invoke() method. Step 1: Understanding Delegates Delegates are type-safe function pointers used in .NET programming languages such as C#. They allow encapsulation of methods into objects which then can be passed around just like any other object. Define your delegate at the form level: ```csharp public delegate void MyDelegate(string message); ``` Here, `MyDelegate` is defined to take one string parameter and return nothing (void). You should replace 'MyDelegate' with something more meaningful based on what the actual task does. Step 2: Implement Your Target Method Create a public method within your form class that matches the signature of your delegate: ```csharp public void DisplayMessage(string msg) { textBox1.Text += msg; // Append incoming messages } ``` This simple example appends incoming messages to a TextBox component named `textBox1`. Remember to invoke this method from the correct thread context later! Step 3: Creating a Separate Worker Thread Now let's create another thread to simulate some CPU intensive work: ```csharp private void buttonStart_Click(object sender, EventArgs e) { Thread worker = new Thread(() => PerformLongRunningTask()); worker.Start(); } private void PerformLongRunningTask() { // Simulate heavy operation for (int i = 0; i < 1000000; i++) { } // Once done, call our delegate this.Invoke((MethodInvoker)(() => DisplayMessage("Worker thread finished."))); } ``` Note how after completing its job, the worker thread calls back to the UI thread using `this.Invoke`, ensuring the UI update happens safely. Key Tips & Guidelines: * Always remember that UI operations must occur on the same thread they were created on. Use invocation techniques provided by .NET to ensure safe cross-thread communication. * Be mindful of potential deadlock situations caused by improper synchronization between threads. Using locks sparingly and correctly can help avoid these issues. * Avoid lengthy computations directly inside event handlers because doing so would block the UI thread causing unresponsiveness until processing finishes. Instead, offload heavy lifting onto separate background threads. Conclusion: By following these steps and understanding the principles behind multi-threaded programming in .NET, developers can build responsive and efficient applications capable of handling complex interactions seamlessly. As always, test thoroughly under various scenarios to catch possible edge cases early during development cycles. Happy coding! I have no salt on it Astra. The next word's a bird what's a bird? Eating candy in class can be a fun and enjoyable experience, but it's important to do so safely and discreetly to avoid disturbing others or getting into trouble with your teacher. Here is a detailed tutorial on how to eat candy in class safely: **Step 1: Choose the Right Type of Candy** When selecting candy to bring to class, opt for options that are quiet to open and consume. Hard candies, such as lollipops or Jolly Ranchers, are good choices because they don't make noise when you unwrap them. Avoid crinkly wrappers, like those found on chocolate bars or Starburst, which can create unwanted noise. Additionally, choose candy that won't leave visible residue on your hands or mouth, as this could attract attention. *Key tip:* Opt for small-sized candies; larger ones might take longer to finish and increase the chances of being caught by your teacher. **Step 2: Unwrap Carefully** Once you have chosen your candy, wait for an opportune moment to begin unwrapping it. This should ideally be during group discussions, while working on individual assignments, or any time where the focus isn't solely on you. Slowly and quietly remove the wrapper piece by piece, ensuring no sudden movements draw unnecessary attention. If possible, dispose of the wrapper in a nearby trash bin or hide it within your belongings until later. *Guideline:* Practice opening the wrapper without making sound at home before attempting it in class. **Step 3: Time Your Consumption** Start consuming your candy once you feel comfortable that there will be minimal distractions around you. Suck on hard candies slowly to prolong their taste and reduce the frequency of having to discard used wrappers. For gummies or other soft candies, chew gently and try not to smack your lips together excessively. Maintain normal eye contact with the teacher and participate in classroom activities as usual. *Key tip:* Keep track of time; if the class period is almost over, save your candy for another day. **Step 4: Discreetly Discard Wrappers** After finishing your candy, find a way to dispose of the wrapper without drawing attention to yourself. If there's a trash bin nearby, casually walk towards it and drop the wrapper inside. Alternatively, tuck it away in a pocket or compartment of your bag until you can properly throw it out. Make sure all traces of the candy are gone before resuming regular activities. *Guideline:* Don't leave wrappers lying around; clean up after yourself to maintain respect for shared spaces. **Step 5: Stay Alert and Adaptable** Be aware of changes in the classroom environment that may require more discretion. For example, if the teacher suddenly calls on you or approaches your desk, quickly conceal any evidence of candy consumption. Always pay close attention to the lesson and engage in class discussions to demonstrate attentiveness. *Key tip:* Develop a keen sense of awareness about your surroundings and adapt accordingly. By following these steps, you'll be able to enjoy eating candy in class safely and inconspicuously. Remember to remain mindful of others and prioritize learning despite engaging in this minor act of rebellion! Chicago is an American soft rock / jazz group, from Chicago, Illinois. They formed on February 15, 1967. They are one of the best selling American groups of all time, second only to the Beach Boys. Chicago has released at least 30 albums in their career. Getting followers on Twitch can be a challenging but rewarding experience. By growing your follower count, you'll increase your visibility, reach a wider audience, and potentially make streaming a full-time career. Here is an extensive guide with clear steps and helpful tips to help you get started: **Step 1: Choose Your Content Niche** Determine what type of content you want to stream. Focus on games or topics that interest you, as authentic passion will come across to viewers. Consider popular genres like gaming, music, art, or IRL (in real life) streams. Choosing a niche allows you to target specific audiences more effectively. *Key tip:* Aim for a balance between popular and less crowded niches to maximize growth potential while minimizing competition. **Step 2: Set Up Your Channel** Create a Twitch account and set up your channel by adding relevant information such as a catchy name, engaging bio, attractive profile picture, and banner. Make sure these elements reflect your chosen niche and personality. *Key guideline:* Keep all visuals consistent and professional, making a positive first impression on potential new followers. **Step 3: Invest in Quality Equipment** To ensure high-quality audio and video, invest in good equipment, including a webcam, microphone, and lighting setup if necessary. High production values attract and retain viewers better than low-quality feeds. *Key tip:* Don't break the bank; start small and upgrade over time based on feedback from your community. **Step 4: Stream Regularly** Consistency is crucial when building a following on Twitch. Create a schedule and stick to it so viewers know when they can find you online. This also demonstrates commitment and seriousness about your streaming endeavors. *Key guideline:* Start with at least three scheduled hours per week and gradually build up as needed. **Step 5: Engage With Viewers** Interact with your chat during streams to create a welcoming atmosphere and encourage engagement. Respond promptly and politely to questions, comments, and requests. Treat every viewer as important, regardless of their follower status. *Key tip:* Encourage regulars to invite friends and share links to your channel, increasing organic exposure. **Step 6: Use Social Media Platforms** Promote your Twitch channel through social media platforms like Twitter, Facebook, Instagram, or TikTok. Share highlights, upcoming schedules, and behind-the-scenes content to pique curiosity and draw attention to your stream. *Key guideline:* Consistently post quality updates without overwhelming your followers with too much frequency. **Step 7: Network Within the Community** Collaborate with other streamers within your niche or local communities. Host, raid, or co-stream together to introduce yourself to new audiences and form mutually beneficial relationships. *Key tip:* Be genuine and respectful when interacting with others; avoid self-promotion tactics that may appear insincere or pushy. **Step 8: Participate in Events and Giveaways** Join Twitch events, marathons, or charity drives related to your niche. Additionally, consider hosting giveaways or contests to incentivize viewership and sharing among existing followers. *Key guideline:* Always adhere to Twitch's terms of service regarding promotions and gifts. Remember, patience is essential when trying to grow your Twitch following. Implement these strategies consistently and thoughtfully, monitor progress regularly, adjust as needed, and enjoy the process! Title: How to Determine the Mood of a Painting Introduction: Understanding the mood of a painting can help you appreciate art more deeply or assist in creating your own masterpieces. The mood is an emotion or feeling evoked by the artwork, often conveyed through color, composition, subject matter, and technique. This tutorial will guide you through determining the mood of a painting systematically and thoughtfully. Step 1: Analyze Color Palette Colors significantly influence the overall mood of a painting. Different colors have distinct emotional associations. Here are some general guidelines: * Warm Colors (reds, yellows, oranges): These hues generally create feelings of happiness, energy, passion, anger, or tension depending on their intensity and saturation. For example, bright red flowers may suggest joyfulness while deep red background could imply drama or aggression. * Cool Colors (blues, greens, purples): Typically associated with calmness, serenity, stability, sadness, or melancholy. However, they might also represent mystery, danger, or eeriness when used darker shades. A landscape filled with lush green trees usually conveys tranquility whereas turbulent blue waves express turmoil. Key Tip: Remember that these interpretations aren't absolute; cultural differences and personal experiences may alter color perceptions. Always consider other elements before making conclusions about the mood. Step 2: Examine Composition & Technique The arrangement of visual components within a frame influences how we respond emotionally. Pay attention to lighting, perspective, balance, contrast, etc., as well as brushwork quality. Rough textures tend to be dynamic and spontaneous, suggesting excitement or chaos. Smooth strokes typically indicate peace and order but could also mean cold detachment. Step 3: Identify Subject Matter Identifying what objects, figures, or scenes are depicted in the painting provides crucial context clues. People, animals, buildings, landscapes - all carry certain connotations based on societal norms and historical significance. If there are people present, observe body language, facial expressions, clothing, activities, setting, etc., which provide insights into possible emotions being expressed. Step 4: Consider Contextual Factors An artwork doesn't exist in isolation-its creation date, artist's life circumstances, artistic movement affiliation, geographical location, medium used, size, intended audience, symbolism employed, religious or political implications, etc., can greatly impact its perceived mood. Researching these factors enriches understanding and interpretation. Step 5: Synthesize Findings Now synthesize all gathered information to determine the mood. Ask yourself questions like: What dominant colors do I see? Are they vivid or muted? Is the composition balanced or chaotic? Does it depict peaceful nature or intense human interaction? What era does this belong to and who made it? Asking such probing questions allows forming comprehensive answers leading towards accurate identification of the painting's mood. Conclusion: Practicing these steps regularly enhances one's ability to discern different nuances in various paintings. Bear in mind that interpreting art isn't always straightforward due to individual perspectives shaped by diverse backgrounds and knowledge levels. Nevertheless, engaging in this process stimulates critical thinking, aesthetic appreciation, and intellectual curiosity. Put that one back in your mouth. The medlar needs warm summers and mild (not too cold) winters and likes to grow in sunny, dry places. In the best places, the plant can grow up to eight metres tall. The medlar is "deciduous": it is not green all year. Usually, it is shorter than a tree. It lives between thirty and fifty years. Medlar leaves are dark green, maximum fifteen centimetres long and three centimetres wide. The plant has flowers in late spring. The flowers are white. The red-brown medlar fruits are similar to apples and grow to two to three centimetres. They are very hard and acidic, and we can only eat them after the cold weather makes them soft. Global Warming Hands-On Activity Predict: Will melting "icebergs" cause water levels to rise? 1. Fill a cup with ice cubes. 2. Pour warm water to the very top of the cup. 3. Observe the water level in the cup as the ice melts. Once the ice cubes in your experiment melted, did the water from the cup overflow? Why or why not? Water expands as it freezes. When it melts, water takes up less space. Do melting icebergs, or chunks of ice floating in the oceans, cause ocean levels to rise? Not all ice floats in oceans. Huge sheets of ice cover land. If land-based ice melts, water will form rivers and flow into the ocean. Do you think this added water would raise ocean levels? If so, how might that affect coastal cities? Design an experiment that shows how melting landlocked ice would raise sea levels. Now test out your experiment. We have songs for both of these, don't we? Title: How to Figure Out What Will Untuck Your Shirt Introduction: Untucking your shirt can be a great way to switch up your style and add some casual flair to an outfit. However, sometimes finding the right shirt that looks good untucked can be tricky. This tutorial will provide you with detailed steps on how to figure out what will untuck your shirt successfully. By following these instructions, you'll learn about essential factors such as length, fit, fabric, and design elements that contribute to achieving a stylish and well-fitted look when leaving your shirt free from your pants. Step 1: Understand the Ideal Length The first crucial factor is determining the appropriate length for an untucked shirt. Aim for a hemline that falls between the top and middle of your pant zipper or belt buckle. This range ensures coverage while avoiding excessive bunching or bulkiness around the waist area. It also prevents revealing any undergarments unintentionally. Key Tip: When trying on shirts, make sure they cover your entire backside but do not extend below your pockets or reach your thighs. Step 2: Check the Fit Around the Chest and Waist A properly fitting shirt contributes significantly to its appearance when worn untucked. Look for shirts that have enough room in the chest area without being too loose or tight. Similarly, ensure there is minimal excess material near the waist so that the shirt does not billow out or create unsightly folds. Guideline: Use a tape measure to determine your chest size by wrapping it horizontally across your nipples. For the waist, measure just above your belly button where you want the shirt to sit comfortably without riding up. Compare these measurements to the manufacturer's sizing chart to find the best fit. Step 3: Consider Fabric Choices Fabrics play a significant role in determining whether a shirt will work well when left untucked. Opt for lightweight materials like cotton, linen, or chambray, which drape nicely over the body and allow for natural movement. Avoid heavier fabrics like denim or corduroy, as they tend to appear bulky and may cause discomfort due to their stiffness. Key Tip: If you choose a fabric prone to wrinkling (like linen), consider ironing or steaming before wearing to maintain a crisp appearance. Step 4: Examine Design Elements Certain design features can enhance the overall aesthetic of an untucked shirt. For instance, looking for shirts with straight hems instead of curved ones helps create a cleaner line and avoid unnecessary bulk at the hips. Additionally, choosing tops with subtle patterns or solid colors adds versatility and sophistication to your wardrobe choices. Guideline: Stay away from extreme details like exaggerated tails, large pockets, or bold prints, as these can overwhelm the overall look and draw unwanted attention to specific areas of your body. Conclusion: By considering factors such as length, fit, fabric, and design elements, you now possess the knowledge needed to confidently select shirts suitable for untucking. With practice and patience, you'll soon master this skill and enjoy experimenting with various styles that complement your personal taste and physique. Happy dressing! Motion makes the moon go 'round too. In fact, motion makes lots of things go. When we think of motion we often think of cars, bicycles, kids running, basketballs bouncing and airplanes flying. But motion is so much more. Motion is important to our lives and impacts so many things that we do. Motion is the changing of position or location. But motion requires a force to cause that change. Let's learn about force and motion and the effects of these physical laws in our world. What is Force? Force is just a fancy word for pushing or pulling. If I push on something or pull on it, then I am applying a force to it. Force makes things move or, more accurately, makes things change their motion. Two natural forces that we have experienced are the force of gravity and magnetic forces. These two forces act at a distance and do not require direct contact between the objects to function. Gravity produces a force that pulls objects towards each other, like a person towards the ground. It is the force that keeps the Earth revolving around the sun and it's what pulls you toward the ground when you trip. See D4K's site on Gravity. Magnetism produces a force that can either pull opposite ends of two magnets together or push the matching ends apart. A magnet also attracts objects made of metal. Types Of Contact Forces There are 6 kinds of forces which act on objects when they come into contact with one another. Remember, a force is either a push or pull. The 6 are: Let's investigate how these forces can be seen in our lives. A book resting on a table has the force of gravity pulling it toward the Earth. But the book is not moving or accelerating, so there must be opposing forces acting on the book. This force is caused by the table and is known as the normal force. You can "see" the normal force in some situations. If you place a thin piece of wood or plastic (a ruler works) so that it is supported by both ends (by books perhaps) and place a small heavy object in the center, the piece of wood will bend. Of course it wants to straighten out so it exerts an upward force on the object. This upward force is the normal force. You can feel the force yourself if you push down in the center of the piece of wood. The harder you push, the more the wood bends and the harder it pushes back. Applied force refers to a force that is applied to an object such as when a person moves a piece of furniture across the room or pushes a button on the remote control. A force is applied. Frictional force is the force caused by two surfaces that come into contact with each other. Friction can be helpful as in the friction that allows a person to walk across the ground without sliding or it can be destructive such as the friction of moving parts in a motor that rub together over long periods of time. Tension force is the force applied to a cable or wire that is anchored on opposite ends to opposing walls or other objects. This causes a force that pulls equally in both directions. The spring force is the force created by a compressed or stretched spring. Depending upon how the spring is attached, it can pull or push in order to create a force. Resisting forces like air resistance or friction change motion. Whether the forces actually stop or slow something depends upon your point of view. Air friction makes a leaf travel along in the wind. When you pick up a pencil, it's friction with your fingers that gets the pencil in motion. In each case, the friction makes the two things (like the air and the leaf) move together. What is Inertia? Inertia is actually not a force at all, but rather a property that all things have due to the fact that they have mass. The more mass something has the more inertia it has. You can think of inertia as a property that makes it hard to push something around. What is Friction? Friction is a force that happens when objects rub against one another. Say you were pushing a toy train across the floor. It doesn't take much effort or force, because the toy is light. Now say you try to push a real train. You probably can't do it because the force of friction between the train and the ground is more intense. The heavier the object, the stronger the force of friction. Velocity is the speed of an object in one direction. If an object turns a corner, it changes its velocity because it is no longer moving in its original direction. Newton's Laws of Motion Some consider Sir Isaac Newton to be the greatest English mathematician of his time and perhaps one of the greatest scientists the world has known. According to a story, Newton saw an apple fall to the ground and he figured out that the same force which caused the apple to fall also governed the motion of the Moon and the planets. In 1687 Newton published his three laws of motion in the "Principia Mathematica Philosophiae Naturalis." His three laws explained how the concepts of force and motion work. Newton's first law of motion states: A body in motion tends to remain in motion, a body at rest tend to remain at rest unless acted on by an outside force. So, if an object is moving - its inertia (mass) will tend to keep it in motion, and if something is at rest, its inertia will tend to keep it at rest. From the Goddard Space Center: learn more about Newton's First Law. Newton's second law of motion states that a force, acting on an object, will change its velocity by changing either its speed or its direction or both. If your basketball goes rolling into the street and is hit by a bike, either the ball will change direction or its speed or both. It will also be true of the bike. From the Goddard Space Center: learn more about Newton's Second Law. The third law is probably the best known of Newton's laws. It states that for every force and action, there is an equal and opposite reaction. This is what causes a cannon to recoil when it fires. The 'kick' from the firing of the ammunition is what makes the cannon jump backwards. From the Goddard Space Center: learn more about Newton's Third Law. Yeah 'cause my bike is going to crash. She pours the milk and the sugar into the bowl. "Can you stir some more, Tom?" "OK, Sara, I can stir some more," Tom says. He stirs and stirs until the milk and the sugar are mixed with the flour and the eggs. He stirs and stirs until the batter is smooth. "Wow, Tom, you are a good stirrer!" Sara says. It ranges from the Gobi and Taklamakan Deserts in the north to subtropical forests in the south. The Yangtze and Yellow Rivers run from the Tibetan Plateau to the densely populated eastern coast. It is bounded by the Bohai, Yellow, East China and South China seas. China connects through the Kazakh border to the Eurasian Steppe. Title: How to Determine the Value of Any Thrift Store Item Quickly Introduction: Thrifting can be an exciting and rewarding experience, but knowing the value of items you come across might seem challenging at first glance. However, with some practice, patience, and knowledge, you'll soon become skilled at determining if that hidden gem is worth buying or passing up. This comprehensive guide will walk you through the steps required to quickly estimate the value of thrift store finds. Step 1: Familiarize Yourself With Common Brands and Designers Knowledgeable buyers are more likely to spot valuable items. Research popular brands known for producing high-quality merchandise within your area of interest (clothing, furniture, housewares, etc.). Make note of designer names as well; they often hold their value over time. For clothing, check out websites like Vintage Fashion Guild or The RealReal to learn about sought-after vintage labels. Key Tip: Focus on learning about brands from various decades rather than just current ones. Many thrift stores carry both new and old stock, so having broad brand awareness increases your chances of identifying treasures. Step 2: Check Condition The condition of an item significantly impacts its value. Assess wear and tear carefully before making any assumptions about resale potential. Look for signs such as stains, tears, missing buttons, scratches, or broken parts. A general rule is that minor imperfections reduce the value slightly, while major flaws considerably decrease it. Guideline: Use the Goldilocks principle when evaluating condition - neither too perfect nor too damaged. An item should have some character but remain functional and aesthetically pleasing. Step 3: Identify Rarity Scarcity drives demand and price points. If you find something unique or hard to find, there's a good chance it could fetch a higher price. Pay attention to details like materials used, intricate designs, or unusual features that set an item apart from others. Key Tip: Keep up-to-date with trends in fashion, art, and design. Being aware of what's currently desirable makes it easier to identify rare pieces. Step 4: Compare Prices Online Use online marketplaces like eBay, Etsy, Poshmark, or Chairish to compare prices for similar items. Be sure to filter searches based on factors like age, condition, size, and material. This process provides insight into market rates and whether an item has selling potential beyond its thrift store tag price. Guideline: When comparing prices, focus on completed listings instead of active ones. Completed listings give a better idea of actual sales prices rather than inflated asking prices. Step 5: Consider Restoration Costs Some thrifted gems may require restoration work before being sold. Before purchasing, consider the cost of repairs and factor this into your estimated value calculation. It's essential to strike a balance between profitability and investment. Key Tip: Learn basic repair skills yourself or establish relationships with local craftspeople who can help restore items at reasonable costs. Step 6: Trust Your Instincts While research and knowledge play crucial roles in valuing thrift store finds, sometimes trusting your gut pays off. If something catches your eye and ticks most of the boxes mentioned above, don't hesitate to take a closer look. You never know what undiscovered treasure awaits! Conclusion: By following these six steps, you're now equipped with the tools needed to determine the value of thrift store items quickly. Remember, patience and persistence are vital as you hone your skills. Happy thrifting! So what should we cook it in? You'll height get it for New Years. I need ta slow down because that was scary. Different varieties or forms of alloys can be made from the same constituent materials (substances from which the alloy is formed). These different forms or varieties can be formed by using different amounts of the constituents. The whole range of possible varieties of an alloy is called a "system". All the forms of alloy having only two constituents are called a "binary system". All forms of an alloy having three constituents are called a "ternary system". Leave the paper in the box and color. Many people use the word Catholicism to talk about religious beliefs of the Catholic Church, whose leader is called the "Bishop of Rome" and often called the "Pope". The Catholic Church is based in the Vatican City, a small independent country in the city of Rome, Italy. Sometimes the word also refers to beliefs of other Christian churches, including the Eastern Orthodox Churches, who have many beliefs similar to the Catholic Church, but do not believe the Bishop of Rome is their leader. Once upon a time there was a truck. The truck was all alone on the road. It kept driving without knowing where it was going. Suddenly, it stopped in front of a farm. There was a donkey standing next to the gate. The truck spoke to the donkey. "Why are you alone here?" asked the truck. "I'm waiting for the farmer," answered the donkey. "He is always busy working in the fields and doesn't have time to come check on me." The truck was feeling lonely too and had an idea. "Why don't we act together and find the farmer?" they said. So they started to run through the field and the truck beeped its horn. The farmer heard it and came to check on them. The truck and the donkey were so glad to see him. They started to act together and showed the farmer how happy they were. The farmer was so pleased that they acted together. And they all lived happily ever after. Activity: Lengths of Leaves this Activity you are going to investigate the lengths of leaves and work out the mean (average) You are also going to learn a method to find an estimate of the mean length. The first thing you need to do is to find some leaves ... What kind of leaves? You can use leaves from any tree or plant; but if it's a tree, then choose one that is easy to reach (we don't want any accidents), and don't take your neighbor's leaves without asking! Also it's best to use simple leaves. Don't try to use leaves with strange shapes. The kind of leaves you see in the photograph below are ideal. Some of the rules you might want to consider are: - I must choose all my leaves from the same tree or plant. - I must make sure the leaves are whole - broken leaves just won't do. - I must try to get a random sample of leaves - not all big ones and not all small ones. The following photograph shows four leaves of different sizes that I collected from a tree in my garden: You will see that some leaves are small and some are larger. The next thing you need to decide is how many leaves to pick and how to get a random sample. You could pick as many as you like. Ideally you would use all the leaves on the tree, but that would be rather a large number to measure, and your parents might not be very happy if you stripped the tree bare! So let's try 100 leaves. to get a random sample? Here is one way you could get a random sample: taking all the leaves on one branch of the tree. After you've discarded any that are broken, then see how many there are. If there are less than 100, then get the leaves from another branch until you have more than 100. Then write a different number (starting with 1) on each leaf with a marking pen. - Write the same numbers on small pieces of paper. - Fold the pieces of paper. - Put them into a hat and mix them up. - Draw 100 pieces of paper (without looking) from the hat. The first 100 pieces of paper you draw would give you the numbers of the leaves to use in the experiment. how accurate your measurements should be: How to measure? Keeping your leaves flat, use a ruler to measure the length of each leaf from the pointy part at one end of the leaf to the point where the leaf joins the stalk at the other end. Maybe your leaves bend a bit, but don't follow the main rib of the leaf as this would make measurement too difficult. Just measure in a straight line as shown in the following diagram: You should measure the length of each leaf to the nearest millimeter. Now you're ready to begin. Measure the length of each leaf to the nearest millimeter and record you results in a table, as follows: (5 columns of 20 each equals 100 measurements) Add up each column, then add those sums together for the grand total: _______ + _______ + _______ + _______ + _______ = _________ Finding the Mean Now you should be able to calculate the mean length of your leaves. Simply divide the sum of lengths by 100. |Mean (= Grand Total / 100):| Is There Another Way? There is a way to estimate the mean length by grouping your results. This makes the calculation quicker, but is not as accurate. |20 - 29||3| |30 - 39||8| |40 - 49||15| |50 - 59||26| |60 - 69||23| |70 - 79||16| |80 - 89||7| |90 - 99||2| If this is not clear, then let me explain: The three shortest leaves in my sample were leaves of lengths 22 mm, 25 mm and 27 mm respectively. These were all between 20 mm and 29 mm, so each of them contributed one tally mark for the group 20 - 29. So there were three tally marks altogether () in the group 20 - 29, and the frequency for this group was 3 (i.e. there were 3 leaves in this group). Similarly, there were eight leaves whose lengths were between 30 mm and 39 mm, so there were 8 tally marks for this group and the frequency was 8. And so on. Once you've grouped your lengths, how can you estimate the mean? What we do is we assume all leaves in a group have the same length, which is the average for that group. This value is called the midpoint of the group and is simply found by taking the average (mean) of the smallest and greatest lengths in that group. The midpoint for the group 20 - 29 is (20 +29)/2 = 49/2 = 24.5 The midpoint for the group 30 - 39 is (30 +39)/2 = 69/2 = 34.5 So, returning to my example, I am going to assume that there are 3 leaves with a mean length of 24.5 mm, 8 leaves with a mean length of 34.5 mm, 15 leaves with a mean length of 44.5 mm, etc. I can then work out my estimate of the mean length from a frequency table, as follows: |f x| |20 - 29||24.5||3||73.5| |30 - 39||34.5||8||276.0| |40 - 49||44.5||15||667.5| |50 - 59||54.5||26||1,417.0| |60 - 69||64.5||23||1,483.5| |70 - 79||74.5||16||1,192.0| |80 - 89||84.5||7||591.5| |90 - 99||94.5||2||189.0| | f = 100|| (f x) = 5,890| So the estimate of the mean length = (f x)/ f = 5,890/100 = 58.9 mm This value differed a little from the exact value of the mean that I obtained earlier. It's important to understand that this is just an estimate, but it can be much quicker to calculate. Try using the method for your sample and see how good an estimate you get. You can use this table. You will have to decide which length groups to use. You may have to use fewer groups or more groups than we did. For example, if you had a leaf of length 105 mm, then you will need an extra group 100 - 109. On the other hand, you might not have any leaves as short as mine, so you might not require the group 20 - 29. Everyone's results will be different, so you will have to decide what are the right groups for you to use. |f x| x) = Divide (f x) by f to get the mean length: (f x) / (f) = ________ / 100 = _________ Why use an Estimate? Faster! Easier! (But less accurate.) It might not have made much difference in our example, with only 100 leaves, but it could be a useful method to use when the number in a sample is much much larger. Title: How to Display PowerPoint Presentations on Wide Screen Monitors 1. **Create or Open your PowerPoint Presentation:** Start by opening Microsoft PowerPoint on your computer. You can either create a new presentation or open an existing one that you want to display on a wide screen monitor. This is where you'll add, edit, and arrange all the content you wish to present. 2. **Check Your PowerPoint Slide Size:** Before proceeding, ensure that your slides are set up correctly for widescreen viewing. Go to the 'Design' tab, click on 'Slide Size', then select 'Widescreen (16:9)'. This will adjust the dimensions of your slides from the standard 4:3 aspect ratio to the wider 16:9 format used in most modern monitors and projectors. Changing the slide size now ensures optimal visual quality during projection. 3. **Adjust Content for Widescreen Format:** After changing the slide size, some elements like text boxes, tables, charts might need resizing too. Select any item needing adjustment and drag its corners diagonally while holding down the shift key to maintain proportions until they fit well within the new boundaries. Remember, white spaces make your design look more professional than crowded slides. 4. **Preview Your Presentation:** Use the 'View' tab to switch between Normal view (editing mode), Slide Show view (presentation mode), and Reading view (scrollable full-screen). Previewing your presentation in Slide Show view lets you see exactly what viewers will when displayed on a wide screen monitor. It also allows you to practice your delivery and check timing. 5. **Connect to the Wide Screen Monitor:** Connect your laptop to the wide screen monitor using appropriate cables such as HDMI or VGA. Once connected, change the output source so that the image appears on the large screen rather than your laptop screen. The method varies depending on the device but generally involves pressing a function key followed by another specific key (e.g., Fn+F8) or selecting 'Duplicate' or 'Projector only' under Display Settings in Control Panel. 6. **Start Your Presentation:** With everything ready, begin your presentation by clicking on the 'From Beginning' button in the bottom left corner of the PowerPoint interface, or press F5. If you prefer navigating manually through your slides, use the arrow keys or mouse clicks. 7. **Manage Slides During Presentation:** While presenting, if needed, navigate back or forward through your slides using the keyboard shortcuts Alt + Left Arrow / Right Arrow respectively. To access speaker notes, move your cursor to the bottom center of the screen and click 'See All Slides'; this reveals additional information without distracting the audience. Key Tips & Guidelines: - Test your setup beforehand to avoid technical issues during important presentations. - Keep font sizes readable even on larger screens - aim for minimum 24pt for body text. - Maintain high contrast between backgrounds and texts for easy reading. - Don't overload slides with information; bullet points should be brief and clear. - Practice speaking along with your slides to get comfortable with the flow. By following these steps meticulously, you're guaranteed a smooth and impressive PowerPoint presentation on a wide screen monitor. It's an animal sometimes a mammal with wings that flies. Here's a step-by-step guide on how to create your own Splunk.com account: 1. Go to the Splunk website (<https://www.splunk.com/>). At the top right corner of the page, click on the "Sign Up" button. This will take you to the registration page. Key tip: Make sure to bookmark the Splunk website for easy access in the future. 2. On the registration page, fill out the required fields, which include your first name, last name, email address, password, and company name (optional). It is important to provide accurate information as this will ensure that you receive relevant communications from Splunk and are able to recover your account if needed. Guideline: Choose a strong password that includes a mix of letters, numbers, and special characters. This will help keep your account secure. 3. After filling out the form, check the box indicating that you have read and agree to the terms of service and privacy policy. These documents outline how Splunk collects, uses, and protects your personal information. Key tip: Take the time to review these documents to understand how Splunk handles your data. 4. Click the "Submit" button at the bottom of the page to submit your registration request. You should then see a confirmation message stating that an activation link has been sent to your email address. 5. Check your email inbox for the activation link. If you don't see it within a few minutes, make sure to check your spam or junk folder. Click on the activation link to activate your account. 6. Once your account is activated, log in using your email address and password. Congratulations! You now have access to your Splunk.com account. 7. From here, you can explore the various resources available on the site, such as documentation, training materials, and community forums. Key tip: Be sure to familiarize yourself with the layout of the site so that you can quickly find what you need in the future. 8. To get started with Splunk products, consider downloading a free trial version of the software. This will allow you to test drive Splunk's capabilities before making a purchase decision. 9. As you continue to use Splunk, be sure to stay up to date with new releases and updates by checking the Splunk website regularly. In conclusion, creating a Splunk.com account is a straightforward process that provides access to valuable resources and tools. How do I "Hic!" "Hic!" You've just hiccupped for what seems like the hundredth time in a row. Ever wonder where those hiccups come from? You can blame your diaphragm, a dome-shaped muscle at the bottom of your chest below your lungs. When you inhale, the diaphragm pulls down to help more air enter your lungs. When you exhale, it pushes up to help push the air out of your lungs. Sometimes, the diaphragm just does not work in the way it should. You have probably felt a muscle spasm in your arm or leg, where the muscle twitches without you doing anything. The same thing can happen to your diaphragm. When it twitches, it causes a quick intake of breath. This breath is stopped by the vocal cords in your throat. This makes the hiccup sound. The diaphragm can twitch for a variety of reasons. One of the main causes is a full stomach. Eating too much food too fast, drinking too quickly, or swallowing too much air can make your stomach too full, and cause hiccups. In most cases, hiccups go away on their own in a few minutes. when I sneeze? A sneeze is a reflex that clears the nose of irritants, including viruses, bacteria, and pollutants. Every time you breathe in through your nose, thousands of tiny particles enter your nose. Many of these particles are stopped by the hairs in your nose. If the particles make it past those, shelf-like structures, called turbinates, help to trap some of those particles. A sneeze begins when the inside of your nose is irritated by some of those particles. Tiny nerves signal the "sneeze center" in the brain. The brain signals the muscles in the chest and throat to contract, the eyes to shut, and the roof of the mouth to close. Powered by contractions in the chest and throat, the sneeze exits through the nose, clearing the nasal cavity. Saliva spews from the mouth and mucus from the nose. A sneeze can send thousands of particles into the air at 100 miles per hour. Some people, like me, sneeze if they step out into bright sunlight. These people are called photic sneezers. The bright sunlight causes a misfire in the nerves in the nose sending a signal to the "sneeze center" when it is not needed. One interesting fact about sneezes is the saying "bless you" after someone sneezes. This started in the year 590 by Pope Gregory I. This blessing was a response to the outbreak of the bubonic plague. At the time, sneezing was thought to be an early symptom of the plague, so people began the blessing for others affected by the plague. Take three whole numbers. The differences between them give you three new numbers. Find the differences between the new numbers and keep repeating this. What happens? Can you use his example to make some more? And how about Magic Ls, Ns and Ws? Imagine we have four bags containing a large number of 1s, 4s, 7s and 10s. What numbers can we make? Choose four consecutive whole numbers. Multiply the first and last numbers together. Multiply the middle pair together. What do you notice? Um one morning a boy woke up. Chicka, Chicka, Choo, Choo Beginning Reading (Digraph) To learn to read and spell words children must first learn phonemes and letter correspondences. Sometimes a phoneme is represented by two letters that go together to make one sound such as ch. This is what we call a digraph. Digraphs are a necessity to the use of the English Language. Without digraphs, we would be limited in the vocabulary that we could use. This lesson will help children understand that when they see a c and h together, they make the ch sound. So that the students may learn this digraph and its use in everyday language. Poster with the tongue twister "Chappy chased the Choo Choo to Church" Dry erase board and marker for every student Index cards with ch written on the card (every student will have one) Letterboxes with at least four boxes ( each student) All lowercase letters for the letterboxes (each student) Worksheet with pictures and words to match that make the ch sound for each student Book: Chicka, Chicka, Boom, Boom by Bill Martin Jr., Michael Sampson, Lois Ehlert. Simon & Schuster Children's Publishing, 1989 1.Introduce lesson by explaining that when certain letters are combined they make a special sound. Today, we are going to talk bout the way our mouths move when we put c and h together. They say ch. Now watch the way my mouth moves when I read the word chew. Can everyone make that sound with me? "Ch." Notice the way your teeth are together and your lips are slightly puckered when you say "ch" Say it again. 2.Ask students, Have you ever heard the sound of a train? Chugga, chugga, chugga, chugga, choo chooooooo Now lets say that together. Can you spot that sound in a word? Lets try chip. Ch-ch-ch-i-i-i-p-p-p. Good There was the ch sound, right at the beginning of the word. (It's important to tell the students that the ch sound isn't always at the beginning of words.) Give an example of a word that does not begin with ch like peach. 3.Let's say the tongue twister together that is written on the chart. "Chappy chased the choo choo to church." Have the students copy the sentence onto his/her individual dry erase board. Read the sentence slowly and emphasize the ch sound. Then have students read it with you and circle the words that they hear ch in. (this can be done in a group or individually depending on the class) 4.Lets see if you can spot the mouth movement ch in some words. Hold up your card if you hear ch. Change, doodle, bench, creep, watch, choose, goose, chip, dot, hat, chomp and score. 5.Now I want for everyone to take out their letterboxes and all your lowercase letters. -We are going to spell some words that include our new diagraph ch. Since ch makes one sound but is two letters, how many boxes should it take up? One-good. I am going to show you how and then I will let you practice. The word is rich r-i-ch. -Here are your words. Words: 3-[chop, chug, chat, chip] 4-[chomp, chunk]. -Now I am going to spell the words without my boxes and I want you to tell me what I am spelling. For example, "Ch-u-g. Oh, Chug" Now you do it. Good. 6.Introduce the book Chicka Chicka Boom Boom (Martin, 1989.). Book Talk: Tell the children that each letter is climbing the coconut tree. What will happen when X, Y, and Z get up to the top of the coconut tree? Read to find out. While reading I want you to hold up your ch card when you hear ch throughout the book. I will have a worksheet with a bunch of pictures. Some will have ch and some pictures will have different sounds. There will be a list of three words and they will have to match the word to the picture. Biggee, Ashley. Briefly, the hydrogen bonds holding together paired bases are broken and the molecule is split in half: the legs of the ladder are separated. This gives two single strands. Title: How to Get Tyranitar in Pok mon Diamond, Pearl, and Platomat ## Step 1: Obtain a Pupitar To get a Tyranitar, you must first obtain its pre-evolved form, Pupitar. You can find Pupitar in the Wayward Cave located in Route 209. However, before entering the cave, make sure your lead Pokemon is at least level 35 because there are wild Pokemon inside that can be quite challenging if your team isn't strong enough. Once inside the cave, navigate through it until you reach an area with several boulders blocking your path (requiring HM Rock Smash). Use Strength (pressing 'Select') to move these boulders out of the way and continue deeper into the cavern where stronger Pokemon reside, including Pupitar. Key Tip: Save your game frequently during this process as some battles may prove difficult and require retreat. ## Step 2: Level Up Your Pupitar Title: How to Do a Side Dutch Braid A side dutch braid is a stylish and chic way to wear your hair. It's similar to a traditional french braid but has a unique twist that makes it stand out. This hairstyle works well for both casual and formal occasions, and it can be dressed up or down depending on the event. With this comprehensive guide, you will learn how to create a beautiful side dutch braid with ease. **Things You'll Need:** * Hairbrush * Hair ties or bobbi pins * Optional: hairspray or texturizing spray ## **Step 1: Brush Your Hair** Before starting any braiding technique, make sure to brush your hair thoroughly to remove any knots or tangles. This ensures a smooth and tangle-free braid, making it easier to manage and style. ## **Step 2: Section Your Hair** Divide your hair into three equal sections at the crown of your head, slightly off center - this will determine where the braid starts. Take one section as your working strand, leaving two sections aside. ## **Step 3: Cross the Working Strand Underneath the Middle Strand** Take the rightmost section (working strand) under the middle strand, placing it between the leftmost section and the original position of the rightmost section. The order should now go from left to right: leftmost, new middle, and rightmost sections. ## **Step 4: Add Hair to the Leftmost Strand** Now, gather some hair from near the parting line along the top of your head and add it to the leftmost strand; hold all these added hairs together as a single section. Keep in mind that when adding hair while doing a dutch braid, always bring the new strands underneath the middle strand instead of over like a regular braid. ## **Step 5: Repeat Steps 3 & 4 on the Opposite Side** Cross the rightmost (newly formed) section under the middle strand, just like before. Then, collect additional hair close to the parting line and incorporate it into the rightmost strand, again crossing it under the middle strand. Continue alternating sides until you reach the nape of your neck. ## **Step 6: Finish Off as a Regular Three-Strand Braid** Once all of your hair has been incorporated, continue braiding the remaining length using a standard three-strand braid method without adding more hair. Secure the end with a hair tie or bobbi pin. ## **Key Tips and Guidelines:** * Always keep tension while braiding by pulling each section tightly towards your scalp after completing every crossover move. This prevents loose gaps within the braid and creates a neater appearance. * Practice maintaining an even distance between each section throughout the process. Consistent spacing results in a uniform and polished look. * If needed, use hairspray or texture spray to help control flyaways and provide extra grip during the braiding process. Additionally, applying product can give your braid a fuller and more voluminous effect. * Experiment with different sizes and placements of the initial section to change up the overall aesthetic of your side dutch braid. A smaller beginning section will result in a larger braid closer to your face, whereas a larger initial partition will yield a sleeker and more subtle appearance. Evaporation greatly exceeds precipitation and surface runoff in the Mediterranean, a fact that is central to the water circulation within the basin. Evaporation is especially high in its eastern half, causing the water level to decrease and salinity to increase eastward. This pressure gradient pushes relatively cool, low-salinity water from the Atlantic across the basin; it warms and becomes saltier as it travels east, then sinks in the region of the Levant and circulates westward, to spill over the Strait of Gibraltar. Thus, seawater flow is eastward at the Strait's surface, and westward near the bottom. In the Atlantic, this chemically distinct deep "Mediterranean Intermediate Water" can persist thousands of kilometers away from its source. See it's got black in it like Mr B see black. (In total - we're not asking you just to count the 64 single black and white squares!) Once you think about this puzzle in the right way, it's not hard at all. - There is one square that is the full size of the chessboard. - There are 4 squares with sidelength one unit less than this (taking a single square of the chessboard as having one unit length). - There are 9 squares with sidelength one unit less again, ie with sidelength 6 units. - In general, there are n2 squares of sidelength [8-(n-1)]. Adding all these numbers, for n between 1 and 8, we see that there are 1 + 4 + 9 + 16 + 25 + ... + + 64 = 204 squares in total on the chessboard. Back to main puzzle page Does it matter if it's in pencil or pen. Good listening skills are essential for school success. If your child's listening skills could use sharpening try these three easy, quick, and fun activities. These games can be played in a 10-20 minute period of time and will help your young child become a better listener! - Have your child close their eyes. Start by saying, "Listen carefully." Make a noise using a familiar household sound. For example, run the vacuum, ring the doorbell, turn on some music, fill a pan with water, etc. Have your child identify the sound. If he or she can't identify the sound give them a hint. For example, "This is what you hear when I'm getting ready to cook spaghetti." - Play a "directions" game. Again say, "Listen carefully." Start with a two-step direction, such as, "Hop to the refrigerator then skip back to the chair." Or if you are outside you might try, "Jump to the tree then hop over to the swing." When your child can do a "two-step" direction with ease, increase the difficulty by adding another step. For example, "Run to the tree, hop to the swing, then come back and give me a hug." - Read a favorite rhyme or story, and substitute an incorrect word. Start by saying "Listen carefully." "The three little kittens, they lost their scarf ?" and let your child say the correct word. Or, pretend to forget a word and let your child say it for you. "Once upon a I forgot what the next word is. Can you say it?" By starting each listening game with the words, "Listen carefully" your child will soon identify this as a signal requiring his or her full attention. As you can imagine this can apply and be helpful in other situations, such as when you are at the mall and you say "Listen carefully, stay by my side." Well you don't height hit it that hard. That same year Spain and the United States fought the Spanish-American War, after which Spain gave up the Philippines to the United States for US$20 million. The Filipinos had declared independence by that time and the assertion of American control led to the Philippine-American War that officially ended in 1901, but fighting continued well into 1913. Between 1899 and 1913 the American-Philippines war waged, about one million Filipinos and well over 5500 American soldiers (including missionaries and private contractors, military families) lost their lives, tens of thousands more were wounded. Most of the Philippines' casualties came from starvation, injuries, diseases, lack of clean living. Hostilities continued until 1914 when Philippines was promised future independence. Title: How to Add a Subscribe Button to Your YouTube Videos Introduction: In this extensive guide, you will learn the steps required to add a subscribe button to your YouTube videos. This feature encourages viewers to become subscribers, helping you grow your channel's audience and reach more people with your content. By following these clear-cut, easy-to-understand steps, you can enhance viewer engagement and expand your online presence. Step 1: Understanding the Importance of a Subscribe Button A prominent subscribe button increases visibility and accessibility, inviting users to join your community without interrupting their viewing experience. It enables them to stay updated with new uploads effortlessly. A strong subscription base strengthens your credibility as a creator while fostering an active, engaged fanbase. Step 2: Verify Channel Eligibility Before proceeding, ensure that your channel meets the following criteria for enabling customized branding elements like a subscribe button: * Be in good standing (i.e., adhere to YouTube's Community Guidelines). * Have no active copyright strikes or Terms of Service violations. Once eligibility is confirmed, log into your Google account associated with your YouTube channel. Step 3: Access YouTube Studio Navigate to <https://studio.youtube.com/> and sign in using your Google credentials linked to your YouTube channel. The YouTube Studio provides comprehensive tools to manage channels effectively. Step 4: Navigate to Customization Options From the left sidebar within the YouTube Studio dashboard, select 'Settings.' From the expanded menu, click on 'Channel' and then choose 'Branding.' Here, you can modify various aspects of your channel's appearance, including adding a subscribe button. Key Tip: If you don't see the Branding option under Channel settings, try clearing cache and cookies from your browser before attempting again. Step 5: Upload Video Watermark Image Click on the 'Upload watermark' button to add a transparent image containing text prompting users to subscribe. Adhering to specific dimensions ensures optimal rendering across devices. Suggested size: 150x150 pixels; maximum file size: 1MB. Once selected, press 'Open' followed by 'Save.' Guideline: Use simple yet compelling language that resonates with your target audience. Avoid complex designs or excessive colors which might distract viewers from watching your video content. Step 6: Enable Channel Branding Toggle on the switch next to 'Video overlay' below the watermark preview area to enable channel branding during video playback. Save changes made thus far by clicking on the blue 'Save' button at the top right corner of the page. Step 7: Test Functionality To verify if the subscribe button appears correctly, watch one of your recent videos via desktop or mobile device. During playback, look for the translucent icon resembling a person with a plus sign (+) typically located at the bottom right corner of the screen. Hover over it to reveal the subscribe action. If successful, congratulations! You have now added a subscribe button to your YouTube videos. Encourage interaction by reminding viewers about the convenience of staying informed through this handy feature. Conclusion: By diligently implementing these steps, you have significantly improved user navigation towards becoming loyal followers. As your subscriber count grows, so does your influence and potential revenue stream, making every effort worthwhile. Remember to consistently produce high-quality engaging content tailored to your niche market to maintain and attract further subscribers. She never cared for me in the least. Oh, I am not complaining; the thing was a perfectly respectable bargain. Why do rats have long tails? ANSWER 1: Rats use their tails to balance and also to help maintain body temperature. The rat has specialised blood vessels, which control the amount of blood flowing to its tail. Blood flowing into the furless skin of the tail carries heat, which is then radiated out of the body. The rat can direct anywhere between 0.1 and 10 per cent of its cardiac output to the tail. Under ideal conditions, it can get rid of up to 20 per cent of its metabolic heat in this way. Conversely, restricting blood flow to the tail reduces heat loss in cold weather. It is interesting to note that the length of the rat's tail is partly determined by the temperature of the environment in which it is raised. Radiating heat through the skin plays a major part in regulating body temperature in humans as well. ANSWER 2: Observing three rats suggests that the long tail usually around the same length as the body is used for balancing. Although rats cannot grasp objects with their tails, they do seem able to control the muscles along the tail's length with considerable precision. If a rat is running along a narrow surface such as a rope, the tail constantly flicks from side to side to steady them, in the same way tightrope walkers at the circus use a pole to help them stay on the wire. A cat walking along a fence does the same thing with its tail. When rats are eating, they perch on the edge of the food bowl, which is about 20 cm from the floor of the cage, grasping it with their back paws and eating with their front paws. In this position their rear ends stick out quite a long way over the side of the bowl so that it looks as if they should fall off backwards. They manage to stay balanced by curling their tails under the bowl. This moves their centre of gravity below its back paws, keeping it balanced. The rat's centre of gravity is lower too, so it is more stable and less likely to fall. As an experiment, a food bowl was moved to about 10 cm above the floor of the cage, which is about the length of a rat's tail. Instead of curling their tails under the bowl when they are feeding, the rats stiffened their tails and pointed them straight down so that they touched the floor, propping themselves up with this "third leg." [side comment addressed to whomever listens to the tape] Title: Understanding Inequalities with a Fun Number Puzzle! Hello young mathematicians! Today we are going to learn about inequalities using a fun number puzzle. An inequality is like an equation but uses symbols "<", ">", "<=", or ">=" instead of equals sign (=). These symbols tell us whether one side is greater than, less than, or equal to the other side. Let's start by looking at our problem: Find the smallest value of x for which 5 - 2x < 5 1/2 - 5/3x where x is an integer. Step 1: Simplify both sides of the inequality First, let's simplify the fractions on each side to make it easier to compare them: 5 - 2x < (16 - 5) / 2 - 5/3x 5 - 2x < 11/2 - 5/3x Now multiply all parts by 6 (the least common multiple of 2 and 3) so no more fraction exists: 30 - 12x < 33 - 10x Step 2: Isolate variable term on one side We need variables together, so subtract 10x from both sides: (30 - 12x) - (33 - 10x) < cancel(33 - 10x) - (33 - 10x) -3 - 2x < -3 Add 3 to both sides: cancel(-3) - 2x + cancel(3) < cancel(-3) + cancel(3) -2x < 0 Divide both sides by -1 while flipping the sign since dividing negative numbers reverses the inequality: (-2x)/(-1) > 0/(-1) 2x > 0 Finally divide both sides by 2: x > 0/2 x > 0 Since x needs to be bigger than zero and should also be an integer, the smallest possible value for x is 1. So, the smallest value of x that satisfies the given inequality is 1! Keep practicing these steps to master solving inequalities, and soon you will find yourself tackling more complex problems just like this one! Happy math learning! Title: How to Block Someone on Discord Discord is a popular communication platform that allows users to chat, voice call, and video conference with others while playing games or participating in online communities. However, there may be instances where you want to block someone due to harassment, spamming, or other unwanted behavior. This tutorial will guide you through the process of blocking someone on Discord, both on desktop and mobile devices. **Desktop Version:** 1. **Launch Discord.** Open your preferred web browser and navigate to <https://discordapp.com/>. Alternatively, if you have installed the Discord application, locate its icon on your computer's desktop or applications menu and click on it to launch the program. 2. **Log in to your account.** If necessary, enter your email address and password associated with your Discord account to access your dashboard. 3. **Navigate to the user's profile.** You can do this by clicking on their username either from a server they are part of or from a direct message conversation. A drop-down menu should appear upon clicking their name. From the list, select 'Profile.' This action will take you to their public profile page. 4. **Access User Settings.** Once on the user's profile page, look at the top right corner for three vertical dots (also known as an ellipsis). Clicking these dots will open up a new set of options; one of which being 'Block.' Note that some versions might display a gear icon instead of three dots. 5. **Select 'Block.'** After clicking the three dots or gear icon, another drop-down menu will emerge. Select 'Block' from the available choices. By doing so, you prevent the selected individual from sending you messages, inviting you to servers, or interacting with any content created by you. 6. **Confirm the action.** Upon selecting 'Block,' a confirmation popup window will appear asking if you indeed wish to proceed with blocking the chosen user. To finalize the process, press 'Block' once more within this dialog box. 7. **Optional: Report the user.** If the person has violated Discord's Terms of Service or Community Guidelines, consider reporting them. To report, return to Step 4 above but choose 'Report' instead of 'Block.' Then fill out the required information explaining why you are submitting the report. Remember, only resort to reporting when necessary, as misuse could lead to false reports and hinder genuine efforts to maintain a safe community environment. Key Tips & Guidelines: * Keep in mind that blocking someone doesn't delete past conversations between you two. It simply prevents further interactions going forward. * Users who have been blocked cannot see when you change your status or custom game activity. Similarly, they won't receive notifications about your activities anymore. * Mutual friends may still mention the blocked user in chats or vice versa. Mentions initiated by the blocked user will not trigger notifications nor show up in your feed. --- **Mobile Version:** Once there were two little girls named Sally and Annie. They were best friends and they lived together in a big house. One day, Sally had an idea. She said to Annie, "Let's try going to the gym today." Annie smiled and agreed. Sally and Annie watched people playing basketball, lifting weights, and running on the track. The two friends decided to try the rowing machines. As soon as they started, their arms began to get tired. Sally suggested, "Let's go get some juice. That will help us feel better." The two friends knew the juice at the gym was usually sour. But they decided to try it anyway and see how it tasted. When they took a sip, it was very sour! But they both managed to drink it down. After that, they were able to row even more, and eventually they felt much better. They decided that the sour juice had done the trick! You really just like to chew on stuff, huh? Title: How to Care for Your Budgie Correctly Introduction: Budgies, also known as parakeets, are small, vibrant, and intelligent birds that can make wonderful pets. They're relatively low-maintenance compared to other pet birds but still require proper care to ensure they stay healthy and happy. This comprehensive guide will walk you through everything you need to know about caring for your new feathered friend. I. Housing 1. Choose an appropriate cage: Select a spacious cage with enough room for your budgie to fly horizontally. A minimum size should be 18 x 18 x 24 inches (LxWxH). The bars should be close together to prevent escape, yet wide enough apart for easy cleaning. Key Tip: Avoid cages with sharp edges or zinc coatings, which can harm your budgie. II. Placement & Environment 1. Place the cage in a safe location: Find a spot away from drafts, direct sunlight, heaters, air conditioning vents, open windows, and toxic fumes such as cooking smoke or cigarette smoke. 2. Create a stimulating environment: Provide toys, perches, ladders, and swings to keep your budgie active and engaged. Rotate these items regularly so they don't become bored. III. Diet 1. Offer balanced nutrition: Feed your budgie high-quality pellets formulated specifically for budgies as their primary food source. These provide essential nutrients better than seeds alone. 2. Supplement with fresh fruits and vegetables: Provide dark leafy greens, carrots, apples, berries, peas, etc., daily. Remove uneaten produce after a few hours to prevent spoilage. 3. Monitor water intake: Clean and refill water bowls or bottles every day to maintain hydration and prevent bacterial growth. IV. Grooming & Healthcare 1. Clip nails carefully: Trimming budgie nails too short may cause bleeding; if unsure, seek professional help. Schedule trims every 6-8 weeks. 2. Bathe regularly: Mist your budgie with warm water using a spray bottle once or twice a week. Alternatively, offer a shallow dish filled with clean water for them to bathe in. 3. Watch for signs of illness: Look out for symptoms like lethargy, loss of appetite, ruffled feathers, discharge around nostrils or eyes, abnormal droppings, and difficulty breathing. Consult a vet if any concerns arise. V. Socialization & Training 1. Spend quality time: Dedicate at least two hours per day interacting with your budgie outside its cage. Gradually build up trust by talking softly, offering treats, and letting them get used to your presence. 2. Teach basic commands: Train your budgie to step onto your finger, return to their cage when called, and stop nipping gently. Use positive reinforcement techniques only. VI. Maintenance & Safety 1. Regularly clean the cage: Spot clean droppings daily. Perform full cleans weekly using mild soap and hot water, ensuring all parts are thoroughly dried before returning your budgie. 2. Keep hazards out of reach: Store harmful substances securely, cover electrical wires, remove poisonous plants, and supervise children and pets near the cage. By following this extensive guide, you'll set yourself up for success in providing top-notch care for your beloved budgie companion! So do you think that she's imitating you? You got a bunch of blocks over here. I can see a mountain right there. What do you do with the blocks Arlo? Moral Dilemmas Book 1 aids parents and teachers in discussing problems and situations that students may face. This book is meant for grades 2-5. It is not intended to teach morals or ethics or to be used as a textbook for a student's personal use. This is a discussion guide only. The introduction provides teaching suggestions and suggests way the book can be used. Thirty scenarios are covered, and with each one is a list of questions that will prompt a student's problem-solving skills as well as show them how to navigate their moral compass. Here is an example: "Suppose a neighbor asks you to rake some leaves in her yard. She suggests you ask a friend to help you. You and your friend, working side-by-side, finish the job in a little over an hour. The neighbor gives each of you five dollars. The following week you run into the neighbor, and she gives you three more dollars, telling you, 'I really appreciated the fine job you did removing the leaves from my yard.' " (p. 6) The teacher or parent would then discuss in-depth with the child how he/she should handle the situation. "Would you split the extra money with your friend?" or "What if your neighbor told you the three dollars is just for you?" What would you want your child to do? Cheating, care for stray animals, and disobedience are just a few of the moral struggles faced by children. The author covers these topics and many more. It is important to use this book as a tool to prepare the child to make these decisions and to think over these topics in the safety and comfort of an adult's supervision. The adult or parent can help the child see the different sides of the issue so they can come to the best solution themselves. Then if a similar situation does occur, the child will already have a plan of action. Although the book covers a large age group, each topic would need to be evaluated for appropriateness for the age of the child. And on Thursday he ate through what are these? Let's Get to Know One Another! - Grades: 9-12 - Unit Plan: - Work cooperatively to develop collegial relationships with classmates - Access prior knowledge - Use context clues and read between the lines - Think critically in order to problem solve These can be done on the same day or used separately to break up lectures about classroom rules, procedures, and expectations. - Arrange students in groups of no more than four. - Distribute Guess What It Says? Worksheet 1 (PDF). - Explain that each initial stands for a word that completes a common measurement or group of things. - Go over the first two as examples to make sure the students understand what they're doing. - Give them about 20 minutes to complete this worksheet. Remind students to focus on working together. - Distribute Guess What It Says? Worksheet 2 (PDF). - Explain that each square contains a commonly used phrase. - Explain that students must pay attention to the placement and size of the words within the square in order to figure out the phrases. - Use two of the easier squares as examples. - Give students about 20 minutes to complete this worksheet.
